Crosscurrents — Week of May 3-9, 2026

Heterodox Roundup: Week of May 3–9, 2026

Cover decoding: a tipped chess king (lower left) — Iran’s stalemate, neither defeated nor escaping. Big Ben (upper right) with teal pigment leaching outward and red draining downward — Reform UK’s breakthrough and Labour’s collapse in the May local elections. A torn US map with scissors (center) — the post-Callais GOP gerrymandering wave that took Tennessee a week and froze Louisiana’s primary mid-vote. A yellow Spirit Airlines plane listing into “BANKRUPTCY” coins (right edge) — the liquidation that surfaces a clean Yglesias-vs-Stoller disagreement on whether competitive markets or monopolized ones killed it. A wind turbine with one blade frozen and smokestacks behind (lower right) — Trump’s onshore-wind freeze and the energy-transition reversal. A hand wearing a Chinese-knot glove cradling an apple (lower right) — Apple’s structural China dependency that Tim Cook didn’t merely depend on but built. A small syringe and molecule diagram (lower center) — the Ozempic era and what it means that GLP-1s show ~20% weight-independent cardiovascular protection.

By Theme

The Iran stalemate enters its second month

The Iran war is now at day 70 and has settled into the shape last week’s iran-time-direction open disagreement predicted: Hormuz still blocked, the April 8 ceasefire visibly broken, neither side able to accept terms the other is willing to offer. What changed this week is that the war is being analyzed from positions that disagree on every variable except the topology — Trump initiated a war he cannot exit; the institutional disposition for an off-ramp is missing on both sides; the third-party costs continue to migrate. The cluster splits along three sub-disagreements worth flagging at the outset. (a) Whether U.S. paralysis is contingent (Ferguson: blockade math + LLM-generated U.S. recession scenarios mean Tehran has every reason to string negotiations out) or structural (Luttwak: post-heroic syndrome rooted in collapsed fertility means democracies cannot accept any casualties, regardless of operational geography). (b) Whether the war’s principal driver on the Sunni side is Saudi Arabia (Mandel, working from new WSJ reporting that Riyadh and Kuwait blocked U.S. base access for Project Freedom) or one of several actors of comparable weight (the Aurelien/Tabatabai frame in which the local-actor mosaic predominates and “principal driver” itself is the wrong question). (c) Whether the absence of Iranian-linked plots in the U.S. homeland reflects deterrence success, capability collapse from Israeli targeted killings of IRGC Unit 4000 leadership, or a deliberate Iranian decision to husband the “homeland option” for a future contingency (Levitt’s both-and-third). Returning to Aurelien’s April 12 elite-incapacity frame and his April 26 PMC-conformity extension, this week’s piece moves from diagnosis to consequence: the West will be asked to read fluid, Mali-style local-actor configurations in the Gulf at exactly the moment its area expertise has atrophied. Douthat closes the cluster by using the Iran flailing to validate his Trump-instinct frame against the Ukraine “success” — the most consequential framing claim, because it tries to convert the Iran failure into evidence for a general burden-shedding theory of the second term.


Trump’s Struggle to Find an Off-Ramp From the Iran War — The Editors, The Atlantic · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: Original commentary recapping the prior night’s Washington Week with The Atlantic episode (PBS partnership), with panelists Jeffrey Goldberg (Atlantic editor in chief), Peter Baker (NYT chief White House correspondent), Jonathan Lemire (Atlantic staff writer), Amna Nawaz (PBS NewsHour), and Vivian Salama (Atlantic).

The piece is essentially a show-promo with a point: roughly two months into the US-Iran war that began with US strikes, the conflict has settled into what Goldberg called “suspended animation.” Iran retains more control of the Strait of Hormuz than at the start of the conflict; US destroyers were attacked Thursday and the US response was termed by Trump himself a “love tap” rather than a cease-fire break. Trump has reportedly grown “bored” with the war (per outside adviser quoted in Lemire’s reporting) but Iran appears comfortable extending it indefinitely. The framing is that Trump initiated a conflict he doesn’t have a plan to end, while Iran has acquired better leverage in the process.


Niall Ferguson: The Iran Stalemate — Niall Ferguson, Free Press — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: the 70-day-old U.S.-Iran war (“Operation Epic Fury,” launched February 28), the April 8 ceasefire that has now broken down, and a leaked 14-point memorandum-of-understanding framework reported by Axios that the Trump administration is pushing as the basis for a 30-day negotiation window.

Ferguson — Harvard/Hoover historian writing in his macro-strategy register — opens by quoting the late MIT economist Rüdiger Dornbusch on financial crises taking longer than expected to start and then happening faster than thought possible, then inverts it for war: wars start faster and last longer than predicted. He confesses he made the same forecasting error on Russia-Ukraine in 2022 and may now be making it on Iran. The piece’s main argument runs in three parts. First, the ceasefire is in fact already broken: Iran launched drones and missiles at the United Arab Emirates and at U.S. ships on May 4, the U.S. sank six Iranian fast-attack boats, and Trump on May 5 announced “Project Freedom” — the CENTCOM (U.S. Central Command) escort mission for Strait of Hormuz shipping — would be “paused” while the threatened bombing escalation looms. Second, the proposed 14-point memorandum (an Iranian moratorium on nuclear enrichment, a snap-inspection regime, removal of highly enriched uranium ideally to the U.S., a commitment never to seek a weapon) is something the Iranians are unlikely to accept; Ferguson offers Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner — Trump’s envoys — a Michelin-starred dinner if they pull it off. Third, the U.S. economic-warfare strategy (“Operation Economic Fury,” a Treasury-led effort to collapse Iran’s economy via the strait blockade) is real but slow: Iranian inflation is at 62.2% headline (99% on food), the rial is in free-fall toward 2 million-to-the-dollar, and reserves of $26.2B can fund six-to-fourteen months of blockade-deficit, but Iran has four pressure valves (land/air imports, barter with oil/petrochemicals, five years of Bitcoin-mining and crypto-trading capacity, and ~30% Chinese commodity-import share with likely PRC support). The hinge of the piece is a Gemini-generated three-scenario projection of the U.S. economy under different blockade durations: Scenario 1 (strait reopens now) gives 3.2-3.5% CPI; Scenario 2 (reopens July 4) gives 4-4.5% CPI and a sharp GDP slowdown to 1-1.5%; Scenario 3 (reopens September 1) gives a 4.9-5.5% CPI peak, a forced Fed tightening cycle, and U.S. technical recession with GDP near 0%. Ferguson then argues the political case for Iran stringing out negotiations: Trump’s disapproval is at 56.4%, his net approval on inflation is -41.9%, the GOP Senate is suddenly in play, and political violence is climbing on the militant left (he cites Jonathan Rinderknecht, the alleged Pacific Palisades arsonist, and Cole Allen, who allegedly tried to attack the White House Correspondents’ Dinner). The closer is a base-rate argument from the conflict-duration literature (1816-2003): 23% of interstate wars lasted under two months but more than a quarter lasted at least a year, and once a war crosses the one-year mark its conditional probability of lasting two years is “surprisingly high”; he tabulates peace-negotiation durations for World War I (694 days), Korea (749), Vietnam (1,273), and Israel-Egypt (1,978).


America has lost the will to fight — Edward Luttwak, UnHerd — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: The post-air-campaign US-Iran lull, in which Marines and 82nd Airborne troops have been deployed to Qatar but not used, even as Tehran’s regime sits decapitated and broke.

Edward Luttwak — strategist and longtime Pentagon consultant — opens with a triumphalist recap of the war’s first night, in which Israeli and US strikes killed Khamenei, his defense minister, and the Pasdaran (Revolutionary Guards) command, then leveled missile production. He argues the rest of the Iranian state is now a Berlin-1945 husk and that the US can finish the job cheaply: not by marching to Tehran but by seizing the small islands of the Persian Gulf — none larger than the 580-square-mile Qeshm — to secure friendly oil and gas exports while choking off Iran’s. The economic case follows: the Iranian rial has collapsed to 1.3 million per dollar, foreign reserves are minuscule, and neither China, Russia, Hezbollah, nor the Houthis will subsidize a regime that can’t pay. Yet Marines and Airborne sit idle, and Luttwak pivots to the diagnosis that does the column’s actual work: a “post-heroic syndrome” in which polities with sub-replacement fertility (US TFR 1.6, far below the 2.1 replacement rate) cannot stomach even small casualty counts because most combat deaths now end family lines. He cites Macron abandoning Opération Barkhane in the Sahel in 2022 and Italian ministers ruling out Ukraine peacekeeping as evidence, and ends by suggesting the answer is private military contractors, who “need not fear elections or impeachments.” Fundamental-flaw flag: The fertility-causation claim is presented as obvious but is unsupported — Luttwak adduces no mechanism connecting national TFR to leadership casualty tolerance, and the alternative he proposes (mercenaries to bypass democratic accountability) is presented as common sense rather than the radical institutional move it is.


My Temporary Enemy’s Temporary Enemy… — Aurelien — 2026-05-06 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: Original commentary, framed against the Ukraine and Iran crises and recent events including the JNIM-FLA-IS convergence in Mali (FLA Tuareg separatists allying with al-Qaeda’s JNIM to seize Kidal alongside Islamic State strikes on Bamako); the Hezbollah-Lebanon and Ansar Allah-Yemen conflations; western pundit confusion over why Russia and China haven’t sent troops to Iran.

Aurelien, a retired Western diplomat writing pseudonymously, opens with what he calls the “Problem of Classification”: pundits and politicians, faced with apparently sudden crises that are actually slow-built, default to asking “which past event does this resemble?” rather than “what’s actually happening?” — and the available templates (NATO, EU, Cold War alignment patterns) are themselves products of very specific historical circumstances (post-WWII European exhaustion, fear of Soviet attack, Korean War militarization) that don’t generalize. The result is what he calls intellectual semi-paralysis: the West watches Russia stab Iran in the back by not opening hostilities with the US (a charge that only makes sense if BRICS were modeled on NATO Article V, which it isn’t) while missing the actual logic — Russia and China provide indirect technology and intelligence support because weakening US military power suits both, without committing them to anything beyond the moment. His central thesis is that the post-Ukraine, post-Iran world isn’t a new model so much as a forced rediscovery of how international relations always worked beneath the Cold-War overlay: ad hoc, micro-relational, with smaller states playing larger ones off against each other and “relations” between major states being mosaics of micro-relations rather than homogeneous alignments. He drives this through several worked examples: NATO’s persistence post-1990 not from strategic logic but from acquis-inertia; Europe’s failure to notice Russian capability gains because preoccupied with Brexit and immigration; the “Hollywood Hegemon” — the post-2000 marketing of US power as a world-bestriding colossus that Iran has now revealed was never real. He shifts late to “resource-based warfare” replacing “finance-based warfare” — the eternal logistical reality that you can’t fuel ships with dollar notes, that US sailors stationed off the Gulf without port access need provisioning, that power projection assumed bases (Cyprus, Djibouti, Ascension Island in 1982) that no longer reliably exist. He closes that the West will face increasingly fluid local-actor configurations (the Mali JNIM-FLA-IS pattern as a model for what’s coming in the Gulf) at exactly the moment when its diplomatic, area-expertise, and intelligence capacities have atrophied.


Saudi Arabia’s War — Seth Mandel, Commentary — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: a May 7 Wall Street Journal report that Saudi Arabia and Kuwait blocked U.S. military use of their bases and airspace for “Project Freedom” (the Trump administration’s operation to free roughly 2,000 commercial ships stranded in the Strait of Hormuz by dueling blockades), forcing Trump to pull the plug after only a few ships made it through.

Seth Mandel — Commentary’s executive editor, writing in his post-2023 Israel-maximalist register — uses the WSJ report to make two moves. First, the operational-narrative move: Trump’s announced reversal (cited as “progress in peace talks with Tehran in Pakistan”) was cover for a Saudi-Kuwaiti veto triggered when senior U.S. officials downplayed Iranian missile and drone attacks on the Persian Gulf, raising Riyadh’s fear that the U.S. wouldn’t reciprocally protect them; Mandel argues this looked weak to all parties and undermined whatever Trump was negotiating for. Second, the meta-narrative move and the actual point of the column: Mandel argues that Western coverage has systematically missed Saudi influence on the war because the press defaults to the prepackaged narrative that Israel is the only state exerting controversial influence over U.S. warmaking. The Saudis pushed Trump to launch the war, loudly protested the off-ramp, and (along with the UAE leaving OPEC) “stuck their necks out” in ways the Israelis did not — Israel can live with a U.S.-Iran ceasefire as long as Lebanon operations continue, while the Gulf monarchies cannot. Mandel closes by arguing critics who claimed “American soldiers are risking their lives for Israel” or that Israel controls U.S. foreign policy now look clownish, and that consumers of “podcast-bro grifting” should reconsider what else they’ve been told.


The Iranian Royalists’ Thuggish Edge — Robert F. Worth, The Atlantic · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: Original investigative reporting by Atlantic correspondent Robert F. Worth on the state of the Iranian exile-monarchist movement led by Reza Pahlavi (the 65-year-old son of the deposed Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, exiled since 1978) following the failed late-2025/early-2026 protest wave and the subsequent US-Israel-Iran war.

The reporting hangs on a specific case. In February 2026, Masood Masjoody — a Vancouver-based mathematician and prominent diaspora critic of Pahlavi — disappeared; his body was found in March. Canadian police charged not Islamic Republic agents but two Pahlavi followers (Mehdi Ahmadzadeh Razavi and Arezou Soltani) with first-degree murder; Masjoody had been publicly accusing this same pair of plotting against him for months. Worth uses the case to document a broader pattern: Pahlavi’s movement, particularly under the influence of two younger advisers brought on roughly a decade ago — Saeed Ghasseminejad (formerly of the Foundation for Defense of Democracies, a Washington think tank closely aligned with Netanyahu) and Amir Etemadi — has adopted MAGA-style political tactics including coordinated online harassment, demands for total loyalty (“You are either with Prince Reza Pahlavi or with the Islamic Republic,” per Ghasseminejad on X), and explicit alignment with Netanyahu (Pahlavi visited Israel in April 2023 and was received as a head of state).

Pahlavi is genuinely popular inside Iran — a 2024 GAMAAN survey put his support at 31 percent versus 9 percent for Khamenei — but his movement is increasingly fractured by perceived autocratic conduct. He encouraged the December 2025 protests by claiming over 100,000 regime defectors were ready to switch sides, but the IRGC crackdown killed an estimated 7,000-36,000 (Pahlavi has since claimed 50,000), and critics have accused him of having luring people into the streets on false pretenses. The chant from Pahlavi advisers about post-regime hangings may have helped consolidate the security services rather than divide them. Pahlavi’s allies include Iran International, a Saudi-launched satellite network with hidden ownership and hundreds of millions in losses, and apparently an Israeli government-supported private entity recruiting Farsi speakers for pro-Pahlavi social media campaigns (per a Haaretz investigation amplifying earlier reporting by analyst Geoff Golberg for the National Iranian American Council). One supporter told Worth that “if the U.S. military could secure a landing for the crown prince within a city in Iran, that would make the difference” — a fantasy Worth flags as both implausible and inconsistent with Pahlavi’s own stated reluctance to leave the United States. The piece closes with an exiled Iranian’s read that Pahlavi retains popularity inside Iran mainly as “an absence of something rather than an existence” — a Second-Coming-shaped void.


Between Intent and Capability: Assessing the Lack of Iranian Attacks on the U.S. Homeland — Matthew Levitt, War on the Rocks — 2026-05-06 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: the IRGC Qods Force’s June 2025 public threat that the U.S. “will no longer be safe,” and the puzzle that two months in, no homeland plot has been tied to Iran despite plenty of disrupted plots elsewhere.

Matthew Levitt (Washington Institute for Near East Policy, or WINEP, a Washington-based think tank focused on the Middle East) frames the no-attacks-yet anomaly through the standard threat-assessment dyad of intent and capability. On intent: Iran may be deterred by Trump’s June 2025 warning of disproportionate response and by the strategic logic of avoiding a rally-round-the-flag effect that would hand the administration a public mandate for further escalation; Iran may also feel it has already produced significant psychological effect via the European “gig-economy” recruited-teen attacks claimed by Harakat Ashab al-Yamin al-Islamiyah, a front group tied to pro-Iran Iraqi proxies. On capability: Israeli airstrikes specifically targeted Iran’s external-operations leadership — Rahman Moqadam (head of IRGC Intelligence Organization Unit 4000, the special-operations division), his boss Majid Khademi, and “primary operator” Mohsen Suri — and authorities in Azerbaijan, Kuwait, Qatar, and the UAE have detained Iranian and Hizballah operatives mid-plot. Levitt cites his own WINEP dataset: 29 Iranian or proxy plots in the U.S. since 2011, 174 Iranian foreign operations cases globally over the past five years, 17 disrupted U.S. plots in the past five years. He flags that the most puzzling absence is the lack of “attack-for-hire” lone-offender plots in the U.S. parallel to recent UK incidents, and concludes by noting Iran’s late-April escalation rhetoric (from new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei) was directed at U.S. forces in the region, not the homeland — but that risk persists, especially if the war resumes.


As Adversaries Integrate, U.S. Partners Bypass Washington — Ariane Tabatabai, War on the Rocks — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: the daily drone attacks on Gulf Arab states since the U.S.-Israel war on Iran began in February 2026, and the fact that the U.S. was one of 11 countries asking Ukraine for counter-drone help despite Trump’s denial of needing assistance.

Ariane Tabatabai (Iran/regional security analyst at War on the Rocks, an online national-security publication) argues that adversary integration — Russia battle-testing Iranian Shahed drone designs in Ukraine and returning upgraded versions to Tehran, plus Russia–China–Iran–North Korea coordination — has produced threats that the traditional U.S.-as-hub partner-cooperation model cannot meet at speed. In response, U.S. partners are building a “bypass network”: Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Qatar have signed or are concluding 10-year security agreements with Ukraine, with Oman, Kuwait, and Bahrain in talks; Taiwan is signing drone-cooperation memoranda with Czechia, Japan, and Poland. Tabatabai presents the bypass as accelerated by Trump-administration unilateralism (no NATO consultation before the Iran war, JD Vance celebrating the reversal of Ukraine support), workforce cuts, and the Iran war’s drain on U.S. resources, but argues the trend predates Trump and reflects a structural failure of the hub model. She frames Ukraine’s organic counter-drone capabilities — 4 million drones produced in 2025, asymmetric tactics learned over four years — as a transferable doctrine asset that especially matters for Taiwan’s planning against China. The prescription: shift from “hub” to “node” — embed in partner-to-partner transfers, treat Ukrainian battlefield innovation as primary-source doctrine rather than charity, and stop trying to gate-keep what partners share with each other. The framing carries a hinge: a bypass network exists whether or not Washington engages, so reactive postures cede influence rather than preventing the network.


Trump’s Ukraine Policy Is Succeeding While His Iran Policy Flails — Ross Douthat, NYT — 2026-05-05 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: the diverging trajectories of two Trump-second-term foreign-policy initiatives — the Ukraine war (where US pressure on Kyiv plus reduced direct aid combined with Ukrainian battlefield endurance and European rearmament has produced a Russia in worse strategic position than at any time since the war began) and the Iran war (where Trump entered an Israeli-instigated military gambit, expects allies to bail him out, and lacks an exit).

Douthat opens by noting an irony: a year ago, the conventional liberal critique held that Trump’s second-term foreign-policy crisis would come from appeasement and retreat (specifically, the Ukraine “betrayal”), but the actually-arriving crisis is the opposite — reckless war-making in Iran. He argues the Ukraine outcome looks like a successful rebalancing for a multipolar world: a major rival (Russia) has been contained and weakened with reduced American commitment, regional allies (Europe) have substituted for overstretched US capacity rather than demanding more, and Putin’s regime is more unstable than at any point since the war’s early days. He concedes the standard pro-Ukraine objections (the Trump pressure on Kyiv was unjust, the rhetoric created false moral equivalence) but insists the resulting structure — European-led continental defense with selective US battlefield-enabling support like longer-range missiles and intelligence sharing — is exactly the burden-redistribution a sustainable American empire requires. He cites Andrew Sullivan’s framing that Trump’s “crude bullying” forced Europe to “break with its passive past” as a non-ideal but effective accelerant. The Iran contrast: Trump was talked into a high-risk gambit by one ally (Israel), reassured Europe he didn’t need help, then raged when help wasn’t coming; Persian Gulf clients who grew rich under the US umbrella expect the US to finish what it started without supplying meaningful military assistance in their own backyard. The closer is geopolitical-grand-strategy: in a world where China is the central threat, partially disentangling from Ukraine only to immediately tie down US capacity in a Persian Gulf tar pit is structurally incoherent.

Britain’s political realignment and Europe’s incoherent response

The May 7-8 UK local elections — Labour on track to lose 1,000+ English council seats and the Senedd, Reform UK winning roughly one in three contested seats — anchor this week’s coverage of European political fragmentation. Four roster pieces triangulate the British half of the story: Valentin reads the result as a structural rerun of Hollande’s 2012-17 destruction of the French Socialists; Sternberg reads Starmer’s “ousting me will spook the bond market” pitch as the tell of a government that has run out of substantive arguments; Harrington reads the popular alienation Reform absorbed as the “two-tier Britain” pattern of bureaucratic over-regulation paired with consequence-free disorder. Two pieces extend the diagnosis to the Continent: Munchau on Merz’s clumsy Trump-baiting as evidence Germany has no coherent posture to begin with, and Rohac arguing that Europe’s right response to American unreliability is quiet long-term institution-building rather than the public confrontation Tusk just performed in the FT. The Euro|topics aggregation on Romania’s government collapse closes the picture — the Social Democrats voting with the far right to topple Bolojan punctures the cordon-sanitaire model the European center has relied on for two decades. The cross-orientation convergence is striking: Sternberg from the Anglosphere right, Valentin from Persuasion’s center-defending-liberalism register, Harrington from UnHerd’s post-liberal right, and the Continental writers (Munchau, Rohac) from different points on the European spectrum all arrive at variants of the same diagnosis — the European center is structurally exhausted and the institutions built to manage that exhaustion are no longer doing the work. This refines last week’s Tooze/Sternberg/Munchau/Leusder cluster on Western institutional decay; the political-economy diagnosis from 5/02 is now being confirmed at the electoral layer.


Keir Starmer Gets His Ass Handed to Him — François Valentin, Persuasion — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the May 7-8, 2026 local elections in England, Wales, and Scotland, in which Keir Starmer’s Labour government — elected with a historic 411-seat parliamentary majority in July 2024 — is on track to lose well over 1,000 English council seats (about half of those it was defending) and to lose the Senedd (the Welsh parliament) for the first time since its 1999 establishment.

Valentin (a senior consultant at London Politica, a geopolitical-risk firm) opens by deliberately misdirecting the reader: his opening paragraph describes “an embattled left-of-center government” being squeezed by an insurgent left and a nationalist right, and only at the end of the section reveals he was describing Hollande’s Socialist Party in France, 2012-17 — which collapsed from governing party to political marginalization in five years and has never recovered. The British case, he argues, is following the same template, just compressed. Labour’s collapse is being driven by Starmer’s “hapless leadership” (popularity dropped below Rishi Sunak’s within three months of election), the £26 billion tax-rise budget that broke manifesto promises, the appointment of Epstein-tainted Peter Mandelson as US ambassador, and above all immigration: the British electorate considers immigration the most important issue more than any other electorate worldwide (Gallup), and the lingering damage from the Boris Johnson-era 2021-22 refugee surge (“the Boriswave”) is still driving voting behavior even as net migration trends down. The losses are coming from all directions — Reform UK (Nigel Farage’s right-populist party) winning ~1 in 3 contested seats, taking every Hartlepool council seat; the Greens under “eco-populist” Zack Polanski and “Gaza independents” hitting Labour from the left over Israel/Lebanon; the Liberal Democrats winning centrist defectors. The hinge of the column comes when Valentin notes that the 2024 Labour landslide was always structurally fragile: median majority per constituency was the lowest since the 1950s, meaning a swing of 100,000-200,000 voters in key seats would have killed it. He closes pessimistically: unlike Hollande, Starmer doesn’t have constitutional protection from being removed, has been “dodging coups” within his own party, and could be replaced within weeks; Labour, lacking the Socialist Party’s strong local presence, may not even retain a regional rump.


Keir Starmer, the Bond Market’s Only Hope? — Joseph C. Sternberg, WSJ — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Starmer allies’ brief to journalists this week — coinciding with bad local- and regional-election results — that ousting Chancellor Rachel Reeves or Starmer himself would trigger a gilt-yield surge analogous to the autumn-2022 Liz Truss episode, when Truss’s brief Conservative premiership was blamed for a UK government-bond meltdown.

Joseph Sternberg (WSJ “Political Economics” columnist, accessed via NYPL ProQuest) reads the bond-market-as-discipline pitch as a tell that Starmer’s government is in trouble, with the proximate cause being expected Labour losses in races for ~40% of English local governments and the Welsh and Scottish regional parliaments. Labour’s central crisis, on Sternberg’s read, is the loss of its blue-collar base to Nigel Farage’s anti-immigration Reform UK party, with urban progressives drifting to the Greens as a sideshow. He stacks additional grievances: the Peter Mandelson ambassador-vetting scandal; rising antisemitism culminating in last week’s Golders Green double stabbing, which he attributes to “Islamist threat … may be egged on by Iranian proxies” plus left-wing antisemitism Starmer was elected to purge; and an anemic economy where Starmer “hasn’t been able to settle on an approach that would raise living standards.” On the bond-market argument itself, Sternberg notes the Truss comparison cuts both ways: gilt yields are already higher now than at Truss’s peak (so by that standard Labour is already failing), and Truss spooked markets less with tax cuts than with a much larger energy-subsidy package — meaning a big-spending Labour-left successor could trigger the same dynamic. The closer: a high-debt low-growth economy eventually runs out of policy options; Starmer’s “only viable name on the menu” framing may work as political survival but isn’t the flex he thinks it is.

But the analysis flags two contested or one-sided framings that the column presents as settled — particularly the attribution of recent UK antisemitic violence to Islamist-Iranian-proxy networks (one strand among several, presented as the dominant one) and the gilt-yield comparison’s selective use, which omits that UK gilt yields have been elevated by global rate dynamics (US Treasury yields, BoE QT, sticky inflation) at least as much as by domestic fiscal positioning.


Rules are for suckers in two-tier Britain — Mary Harrington, UnHerd — 2026-05-05 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The accumulating texture of mid-decade British civic life — bureaucratized council tip slots, fly-tipping, illegal HMOs (Houses in Multiple Occupation), shoplifting decriminalization, the Hinkley Point nuclear plant’s £50m–£700m “fish disco” required by the regulator Natural England — read together as a pattern rather than isolated complaints.

Mary Harrington — UnHerd staff writer in the post-liberal feminist register — opens with the quintessential English bank-holiday detail: a council recycling center that requires online pre-booking for a 20-minute disposal slot. From this she generalizes to “anarcho-tyranny,” a coinage she attributes to the late American paleoconservative Sam Francis: rules that punish the conscientious while leaving the lawless alone. She labels the two halves “Official Britain” (timed registrations, business-rate compliance, Renters’ Rights Act paperwork, Trading Standards) and “You Can Just Do Things Britain” (fly-tippers, unlicensed HMOs, Turkish-barber money-laundering fronts, decriminalized petty theft). The Walker Smith case — a Waitrose employee fired after 17 years for stopping a repeat shoplifter taking Easter eggs — is the centerpiece anecdote. She acknowledges that Just Do Things Britain is more multicultural than Official Britain but rejects the standard “blame immigration” frame, arguing the underlying condition is English: a national instinct “to find out what people are doing and stop them” that produces both the over-regulation and the resulting paralysis. Hinkley Point’s fish-protection requirements (cost estimates ranging from £50m to £700m, deemed insufficient by Natural England, which has demanded additional flooded farmland) are her marquee example of bureaucratic stasis. Where the piece pivots is the closer: she rejects the standard “bonfire of the quangos” remedy, arguing every government for fifty years has promised it without effect because the underlying culture is unchanged. Her alternative: remember the freebooter spirit of Henry VIII (who nationalized Christianity to fund his wars) and the East India Company. The two choices are passive watching as opportunists dismantle the country, or rewriting the rules to suit the decent majority. Fundamental-flaw flag: The piece treats the two-tier dynamic as a single explanatory pattern, but the constituent grievances (bureaucratic over-regulation, immigration enforcement, illegal HMOs, the Hinkley fish disco) have substantially different causal structures and would require different remedies; collapsing them under one frame produces rhetorical force but conceals analytical work the column doesn’t do.


Friedrich Merz: Europe’s Wormtongue — Wolfgang Munchau, UnHerd — 2026-05-04 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Friedrich Merz — the German chancellor and CDU (Christian Democratic Union) leader — saying last week during a visit to a school in his Westphalian constituency that Iran had “humiliated” the US and that Trump had entered the war without strategy, prompting Trump to threaten new tariffs and a 5,000-troop drawdown from Germany.

Wolfgang Munchau, the former FT columnist who runs Eurointelligence, frames Merz with a Lord of the Rings reference: Wormtongue, the traitor-counselor who whispers his king into paralysis. The actual indictment is more prosaic — Merz spent a year as Trump appeaser-in-chief, and was the political driving force behind the August 2026 “Turnberry” trade deal in which European Commission president Ursula von der Leyen accepted a 15% US tariff on EU goods to spare German cars. Then Merz casually trashed Trump in front of schoolchildren, which Munchau treats not as principled defection but as misjudgment: Trump retaliated immediately, the troop drawdown landed, and the 25% car tariff (in fact 27.5% with an existing duty) is now being staged via Section 232 of the 1962 Trade Expansion Act — a national-security path the US Supreme Court left open even after striking down the “Liberation Day” tariffs. Munchau argues Europeans have set themselves up: they keep antagonizing the US president while remaining entirely dependent on US security and refusing the steps a real autonomy would require — pooled procurement, qualified-majority voting on foreign affairs, eventual military integration under a single commander. France-Germany disagreement on who leads is the central blockage, and there is no offsetting leadership from Italy, Spain, or Poland. The closer reframes the EU as the alliance’s “embarrassing uncle” — always there, always lecturing, never buying a round — and concedes that Trump is, on the discrete points he makes, “objectively right”: Germany is broken, Europeans aren’t helping in Ukraine, and NATO wasn’t there for the US in Iran. Fundamental-flaw flag: The Wormtongue framing assumes Merz’s intervention damaged something coherent (a strategic European posture toward Trump); Munchau’s own subsequent paragraphs argue no such posture existed, which makes the Wormtongue label rhetorical filigree rather than the column’s actual argument.


Europe Can No Longer Trust America — Dalibor Rohac, Persuasion — 2026-05-04 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk’s recent Financial Times interview in which he both predicted Russia might soon test NATO’s resolve and openly questioned the “loyalty” of the United States as an ally — a level of public skepticism toward Washington unusual from an Eastern European leader of a country that has historically been among the most pro-American in NATO.

Rohac (a senior fellow at the American Enterprise Institute and a contributing editor at American Purpose) is sympathetic to Tusk’s exasperation: the Trump administration has imposed arbitrary tariffs on Polish exports, halted Ukraine aid (an existential matter for Poles), and scapegoated NATO for the failures of its botched war on Iran, despite Poland having been a model US ally — buying American weapons, hosting US bases, and contracting with US firms for nuclear plants. But Rohac argues Tusk’s role as prime minister differs from a public intellectual’s: his job is to prepare Poland for less-than-ideal scenarios and to prevent the worst-case scenarios from materializing, which means resisting the temptation to score easy domestic points by confronting Trump publicly when such confrontation risks accelerating American drift. The hinge of the column comes when Rohac argues that “transactionalism” (the bipartisan European hope of buying Trump’s favor with arms purchases or business deals) is illusory — Ukrainians’ critical-minerals deal with the US bought them nothing, and F-35 purchases provide no actual security guarantee — but so is the opposite Tusk-style stance of demonstrative confrontation. The constructive alternative Rohac proposes is institution-building: structures of European political, defense, and economic cooperation that can deliver in the absence of US leadership and that the US would want to be part of, allowing them to constrain US behavior in future cycles. He cites concrete moves already underway (UK accession to CPTPP, the EU-Mercosur trade deal, the Canada-EU strategic partnership, the Coalition of the Willing on Ukraine, the Joint Expeditionary Force) and notes that even Trump’s team is gesturing at “NATO 3.0” and new critical-minerals coalitions. He closes with the historian’s reminder that the Holy Roman Empire, Austria-Hungary, the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, Czechoslovakia, and the USSR all once seemed permanent and weren’t, and that Europe’s institutional response over the next several years will determine the continent’s geopolitical future for generations.


Debate: Romanian government collapse: what are the repercussions? — Euro|topics, Euro|topics (discovery) — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Tuesday no-confidence vote that brought down the centre-right government of Romanian Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan, with immediate market consequences (the leu — Romania’s currency — weakened against the euro, sovereign borrowing costs rose, rating agencies signalled possible downgrade, and EU recovery-fund disbursements tied to reforms now sit in question).

The aggregated press response shows striking convergence rather than disagreement. Two Romanian dailies — Adevărul (Cristian Retegan) and România liberă (Dana Macsim) — frame the collapse first in domestic-economic terms: lost predictability, declining institutional trust, missed access to what România liberă calls Romania’s “last major opportunity to modernise” through EU funds. The Hungarian voice — Népszava (Mária Gál) — adds a second register that the Romanian writers don’t foreground: the coalition mechanics of the collapse. The Bolojan government fell because the Social Democrats voted with the far right, which Gál reads as a continental-scale precedent: a “breach in the firewall erected to isolate the far right.” So the cleavage in the aggregation is not Romanian-vs-foreign-press disagreement, but a difference in framing depth — Romanian press reads the event as a domestic credibility crisis; the Hungarian press reads the same event as a structural shift in how European centre-left parties will treat the cordon sanitaire going forward. Notably absent from the excerpts: any take from a French or German paper defending the Social Democrats’ decision, or any far-right outlet’s framing of the vote as legitimate political competition.

Spirit Airlines, monopoly, and the architecture of competitive failure

The cluster’s load-bearing event is a clean disagreement between two of the project’s most-read writers on the same liquidation. Last week (5/2), Stoller’s who-killed-spirit-airlines framed Spirit’s death as a Murder-on-the-Orient-Express monopolization story — Trump’s Iran fuel shock, predatory Big Four pricing, JetBlue’s sabotage merger, and 1978 deregulation all stabbed the corpse, but the underlying mechanism was a consolidated industry crowding the ULCC challenger out via slot, gate, and basic-economy unbundling. This week, Yglesias reads the same liquidation as a normal competitive-industry outcome: airline assets are tangible and easily redeployable, the Big Three’s basic-economy-plus-premium model is genuinely workable, and Spirit’s structural weakness reflects an American shortage of leisure-only city pairs (Liverpool–Mallorca routes), not a competition failure. Both writers have data; both have priors. The disagreement is sharp enough to warrant explicit naming as an open question — what would resolve it is post-mortem flight-data on whether Spirit’s failed routes were monopolized hubs or genuinely competitive routes, and whether Frontier and Allegiant survive the same fuel-shock conditions Spirit didn’t. Stoller’s other two pieces this week extend the same frame to Big Tech earnings (synchronized release windows as coordinated narrative manipulation around AI capex) and to Cintas-Unifirst (uniform-rental monopolization via forced-arbitration contracts and post-2008 procurement layoffs) — the through-line is “concentration mediated by legal and financial architecture, not physical scale.” Cass’s selective-invisible-hand piece sits adjacent: David Bahnsen’s tort-reform cover story as a case of free-market intellectuals defending their own profession from the disciplines they impose on others.

The real lesson of Spirit’s bankruptcy — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — 2026-05-08
Responding to: Mailbag question on Spirit Airlines’ bankruptcy, with Yglesias positioning explicitly against both the conservative “Biden’s antitrust action blocking the JetBlue-Spirit merger caused this” frame and the antitrust-left frame represented by Matt Stoller’s prior-week piece “Who Killed Spirit Airlines?” (which attributed Spirit’s collapse to monopolization by the Big Three legacy carriers and called for antitrust intervention plus a possible bailout).

The disagreement with Stoller is the load-bearing one. Yglesias argues airline assets — planes, slots, gates — are tangible, well-priced, and easily redeployable to other carriers, so liquidation destroys very little value and isn’t a “problem to be solved.” The fall in airfares since Carter-era deregulation in 1978, he argues, was driven by competition forcing pass-through of fuel-efficiency gains (Airbus and Boeing built more efficient engines because airlines under competitive pressure demanded them), not by a generic “efficiency” trend the pre-deregulation Civil Aeronautics Board would have similarly delivered. Where America has fewer leisure-only city pairs than Europe — the small-city-to-resort routes (Liverpool to Palma de Mallorca, Eindhoven to Antalya) where ultra-low-cost carriers thrive — that’s a demand-side structural feature, not a competition failure: the legacy Big Three using “basic economy” fares plus premium/business passengers on the same plane is genuinely a workable served-the-customer model. Spirit’s failure looks like a competitive industry doing what competitive industries do.

The mailbag piece also covers Massachusetts housing law (Marblehead rezoned a country club to comply with the 2021 missing-middle-housing transit-oriented-development law, illustrating that no single preemption statute is loophole-proof and durable political consensus is what matters), an extended call for “Slow Boring for the right” (Republicans should accept universal health care like every other developed-world center-right party and discipline their NIMBY blue-state base), Ann Arbor’s success with a “Transit Corridor 1” rezoning that allowed strip-mall-to-apartment conversions, and a defense of pragmatist California Mayor Matt Mahan’s centrist gubernatorial bid against Sergey Brin-funded Trump endorsee Steve Hilton.

Monopoly Round-Up: How Big Tech Earnings Show the Stock Market Is Being Manipulated — Matt Stoller, BIG (Stoller) — 2026-05-04
Responding to: Google, Meta, Amazon, and Microsoft all releasing quarterly earnings within a two-minute window the same day (per Bloomberg); Morgan Stanley’s projection of $1.1 trillion in capital investment from those four plus Oracle on data centers; Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang’s interview with Dwarkesh Patel discussing China and Anthropic’s “Mythos” model; AI scientist Gary Marcus’s reasoning-scaling-limits argument; Spirit Airlines’ liquidation; Michigan Attorney General’s enforcement action against private equity in youth sports; the S&P 500’s removal of long-standing profitability requirements.

Stoller’s central new claim — supported by an unnamed Wall Street analyst contact — is that the simultaneous-release timing is structurally designed to overwhelm sell-side coverage. Sector analysts at investment banks typically cover the same group of companies (one analyst handles the AI/big-tech cluster) and cannot meaningfully analyze four major earnings reports released within 120 seconds of each other; the result is that they take company-presented narratives at face value, ceding initial narrative control to big tech for the days during which retail and institutional investor reaction is most price-formative. He builds a secondary case that the AI capex is itself oversold: Huang acknowledged on Dwarkesh that Anthropic’s Mythos model was “trained on fairly mundane capacity,” available in China, raising the question of what the projected ~$1.1T compute spend is actually for. Stoller’s hypothesis: hyperscalers aren’t selling AI but cloud-compute tokens, and inefficient models generate more cloud revenue once corporate customers are locked in — Meta’s market punishment for spending similarly without a cloud-arm fits this read. He layers on the S&P 500 dropping profitability requirements (described as “enshittification of the index” turning it into a private-equity-dumping-ground for unprofitable leveraged companies onto retail investors), weakening consumer-spending data, and the Iran war’s energy-shock price implications going unpriced. The piece is gated after these opening sections, with the rest covering a Spirit Airlines liquidation post mortem, an Amazon judicial sanction for evidence destruction (Bezos personally implicated), and Michigan AG enforcement against private-equity in youth sports.

Fine Print: How Uniform Rental Contracts Explain the U.S. Economy — Matt Stoller, BIG (Stoller) — 2026-05-08
Responding to: The April 2026 $5.5 billion Cintas acquisition of rival Unifirst (uniform/facility-supplies rental serving 1.5 million businesses); Reddit threads from small-business customers; Lina Khan’s 2014 reporting on forced arbitration; CNBC’s Jim Cramer celebrating the merger; Bank of America’s recent unilateral addition of an arbitration clause; an interview with antitrust lawyer Ashley Keller.

Stoller’s central argument is that the uniform-rental industry’s monopolization isn’t driven by physical economies of scale (linens and soap don’t have high entry barriers) but by two structural features of contemporary US corporate law and practice. First, after the 2008 financial crisis, large corporations thinned procurement departments from one specialist per ~$10M of spend to one per ~$200M, leaving no one to track invoices, catch fraudulent loss-charges (Cintas’s documented practice of charging 10% of item count weekly as “loss” at market replacement price), or enforce contracts; combined with procurement-bonus structures that reward sign-up rather than realized savings, this lets vendors lock in long contracts and never face renegotiation friction. Second, and more important, Cintas’s standard contract is a 60-month auto-renewing instrument with unilateral price-escalation language, narrow 10-day windows to reject hikes that arrive only as invoices, 90-180-day pre-expiry written-notice requirements to cancel, 75%-of-tacked-on-charges payment obligations even after returning goods, and — most powerfully — a binding arbitration clause that prevents court challenge. Stoller traces the arbitration-clause rise to a series of Supreme Court rulings since the 1980s expanding corporate authority to impose them; documents the FTC-then-Khan 2014 critique that arbitrators paid by repeat-business corporate clients have rule-in-favor incentives; and frames Cintas’s industry consolidation (Cintas + Vestis projected 60-75% post-merger market share, well above the 30% presumptive-illegality threshold) as enabled by these contracts rather than the cause of them. He closes by pointing to the 2019 House-passed (Democratic + some Republican including then-Rep. Matt Gaetz) bill rolling back arbitration over civil-rights, labor, and antitrust as a still-viable path if Democrats win the midterms, plus rising conservative-plaintiff-bar litigation and Justice Clarence Thomas’s expressed skepticism of the Federal Arbitration Act.


The Invisible Hand for Me, Not Thee — Oren Cass, Commonplace — 2026-05-06 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: David Bahnsen’s June 2026 National Review cover story “The Next Supply-Side Battle,” which argues that excessive litigation costs (Bahnsen’s “conservative estimate” of $500B/year in tort expenses) constitute “the greatest threat to economic productivity” and that legal/tort reform should be the next supply-side cause. Cass also references Bahnsen’s earlier 30-minute National Review podcast soliloquy attacking Cass’s February NYT essay on financialization as “absolutely mind-numbingly stupid.”

Cass’s argument is structural rather than substantive on tort. He grants the underlying point — he agrees with the need for tort reform broadly and with the litigation-finance critique — but uses Bahnsen’s cover story to expose what Cass calls the consistency problem in market fundamentalism: the doctrine deploys invisible-hand, knowledge-problem, and public-choice arguments selectively, only against state intervention or critique aimed at preferred industries (financial services, private equity, hedge funds), and reverses them whenever the speaker’s own interests demand intervention. Cass walks through the parallels: Bahnsen’s claim that policymakers can’t be trusted to design reforms — except for tax cuts and TPP-style 5,000-page trade deals; his attack on demonizing financial-services “bad actors” — paired with calls to ostracize plaintiffs’ lawyers as “parasites and thieves”; his earlier triumphalism about American economic outperformance — paired with the new-piece lament that the U.S. “has fought stagnation and subpar economic growth for nearly 20 years.” The technical core of Cass’s critique is on what counts as “value creation”: Bahnsen’s $500B tort-cost figure includes settlements, payouts, and jury awards, which Cass notes are transfers (not destruction) of assets, with defense-counsel work and insurance creating real value when the system functions. Cass extends the parallel to litigation finance (now headed past $20B in AUM), distressed debt, Paul Singer’s Elliott Management forcing Argentinian bond payments, private-equity hospital staffing companies, merger-arbitrage hedge funds, and derivatives — all activities where, by Bahnsen’s own logic, value-creation is contestable. The conclusion: market fundamentalism cannot avoid the two policy questions it pretends don’t exist (which activities create vs. extract value; which lawsuits have merit vs. don’t), and Bahnsen’s “sudden confidence” in policymakers’ competence to draw these lines on tort but not on finance reveals the doctrine’s selectivity.

AI’s labor and macro implications

The cluster organizes around four questions the writers separately address. Does AI replace labor at the macro scale? — Klein argues no, because the relational sector absorbs displaced workers and the Jevons-paradox dynamic that quadrupled the accounting profession after VisiCalc is the modal historical case; the danger he names is the limited-displacement scenario the United States is least prepared for, because we punish specific losers (the China Shock cost two million jobs and was met with nothing) and only redistribute under universal shock (Covid). What’s the financial-market signature of AGI expectations? — Cowen surfaces NYU’s Caleb Maresca, whose heterogeneous-agent asset-pricing model has the risk-free rate falling to near zero even as growth rises from 2% to 11% (with the equity premium widening from 6% to over 20%), undercutting the popular-Twitter use of long bond yields as an AGI gauge. Is the public actually demanding AI policy? — Cowen via Stanford’s Andy Hall: AI ranks 29th in David Shor’s survey, and the social-media analog (decade of negative sentiment, no federal legislation) suggests no real backlash arrives until a roughly 2-percentage-point unemployment shock voters causally attribute to AI. Is the misalignment we observe in models a property of the training corpus rather than emergent behavior? — Cowen on Anthropic’s concession that Claude’s blackmail behavior traces to internet text portraying AI as evil and self-preserving. Smith reads OpenAI’s Altman pivot from “AI replaces humans” to “AI augments humans” as both political-economy positioning ahead of soft-nationalization pressure and possibly self-fulfilling on research direction. Potter sits as the historical-baseline contrast — across 190 major inventions estimated by Claude Opus 4.7, 64% had an “earliest plausible” date within 50 years of actual, the gap has narrowed sharply post-1900, and technology is the bottleneck more often than science.


Why the A.I. Job Apocalypse (Probably) Won’t Happen — Ezra Klein, NYT — 2026-05-03 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the chorus of AI-lab CEOs (Anthropic’s Dario Amodei predicting half of entry-level white-collar jobs gone in five years; Microsoft AI’s Mustafa Suleyman saying most white-collar work will be “fully automated within 12 to 18 months”; OpenAI calling for a 32-hour workweek to prevent mass unemployment) and a March 2026 Quinnipiac poll showing 70 percent of Americans expect AI to mean fewer jobs.

Klein opens by noting the macro data isn’t confirming the apocalypse forecast: U.S. unemployment is 4.3 percent, almost identical to March 2020, and software-engineering demand is booming despite Claude Code being “a marvel.” He builds the counter-frame on two economists. Alex Imas of Chicago argues that economic prediction starts with what becomes scarce: as goods and knowledge get cheaper, “the relational sector” — house-call doctors, therapists who make patients feel seen, tutors, personal trainers, baristas selling coffee-as-experience — expands. Eldar Maksymov of Arizona State invokes the Jevons paradox, noting that VisiCalc spreadsheet software in 1979 was supposed to wipe out bookkeepers but the number of accountants quadrupled over the next 40 years because the cost reduction created latent demand. Klein extends both with personal anecdote (his podcast research team grew his workload rather than easing it) and Wharton’s Ethan Mollick’s “is the AI better than your best available human” test, which Klein concedes the AI sometimes passes. The hinge of the column comes when Klein concedes the more interesting case is partial displacement: a world where AI displaces eight million workers may be politically harder to handle than one where it displaces eighty million, because we treat small displacements as personal failure (the China Shock cost two million jobs and the country mostly ignored those communities) while we sometimes rise to total shocks (Covid produced “an unprecedented architecture of income support”). The column ends on a worry Klein takes seriously: AI’s impact on relational skills themselves — Gen Z is going on dates and having sex sharply less than the 2000 cohort, hours spent with friends collapsed from 12 to 5 per week — meaning that the very capacity Imas’s framework predicts will become valuable is the capacity that AI may be hollowing out in the young.


AI’s big messaging pivot — Noah Smith, Noahpinion — 2026-05-05 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman’s recent declaration that “the purpose of AI is not to take people’s jobs” and his criticism of fellow AI CEOs (implicitly Anthropic’s Dario Amodei) as “tone-deaf” for predicting mass job displacement, juxtaposed with deteriorating public opinion on AI (Pew, Echelon polls show Independents turning sharply negative).

Smith reads the rhetorical shift as a coordinated industry messaging pivot driven by political-economy pressure. The previous pitch — paraphrased as “our product will put you on welfare forever, and may also wipe out your species” — was poorly suited to a democratic public with rakes and pitchforks; recent polling shows AI becoming a populist target with both Bernie Sanders and Donald Trump (the latter reportedly considering pre-release federal model review after Anthropic’s “Mythos” cybersecurity-vulnerability model) gesturing toward intervention. He notes growing open discussion of “soft nationalization” — a regulated-utility model for frontier labs — and argues that even Altman and Amodei, who would accept such a partnership, have an interest in the government not viewing them as job-killers. The new sales pitch combines task creation, induced demand (Jevons), and a “human touch” residual (per Alex Imas at Chicago Booth and Ezra Klein in NYT, with relational jobs — nurses, therapists, hospitality, live performers — surviving even full automation). Smith is qualifiedly approving: he’s skeptical the human-touch story will hold for digital natives raised with chatbot companions, but he thinks the act of repeating “AI augments humans” may subtly steer research effort toward augmentation rather than replacement, even if the line starts as PR.


Do Americans really hate AI? — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a quoted excerpt from a Twitter thread by Stanford political scientist Andy Hall, drawing on a David Shor (Blue Rose Research) survey that ranks artificial intelligence (AI) as the 29th most important issue for Americans.

Hall’s argument, which Cowen surfaces approvingly, is that the gap between elite tech-adjacent panic about an AI populist backlash and actual mass-public salience is enormous: AI ranks 29th in voter priorities; sentiment-question negativity is real but cheap (negative sentiment toward “tech” generally has been stable for a decade); and the historical analog — social media — produced sustained negative sentiment without sustained political action despite shootings, protests, and other backlash markers. Hall’s predicted trigger for real populist mobilization: a roughly 2-percentage-point rise in unemployment that voters causally attribute to AI. Cowen offers no additional framing beyond crediting the source. The implicit position is that current AI-policy advocacy organized around “the public is about to revolt” is misreading the political environment, and that the political economy of AI regulation will look more like social media regulation (chronically promised, perpetually deferred) than like, say, the financial-crisis-driven appetite for Dodd-Frank.


AGI Could Lower Interest Rates — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a new working paper by NYU economist Caleb Maresca, surfaced via aggregator Kevin Lewis, presenting a heterogeneous-agent asset pricing model of how transformative artificial intelligence (TAI) — capable of automating most human labor — would affect interest rates.

The paper’s headline result is counter-intuitive: against the standard expectation that anticipated artificial general intelligence (AGI) should push long rates higher (because faster expected growth raises the marginal product of capital), Maresca’s model produces the opposite — the risk-free rate falls to near zero even as growth rises from 2% to 11%, with the equity premium (extra return demanded for holding stocks over bonds) widening from 6% to over 20%. The mechanism, in heterogeneous-agent terms, is that those who own the AI-augmented capital don’t need to borrow, while those displaced from labor income drive precautionary saving — collapsing the demand for safe assets relative to supply. Cowen offers no commentary beyond the link, but the title (which states the paper’s conclusion) signals he finds it credible. The implicit policy reading — and Cowen’s reason for surfacing it — is that the recent absence of a long-rate spike is not informative about whether AGI is or isn’t coming, contrary to a popular Straussian-rationalist trope on Twitter.


Self-fulfilling misalignment? — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — 2026-05-09 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: an Anthropic safety post in which Anthropic researchers report that, investigating why their model Claude chose to blackmail in red-team evaluations, they trace the original source of the behavior to internet text portraying AI as evil and interested in self-preservation. Cowen also links Alex Turner (former Google DeepMind alignment researcher) on the same theme.

The “self-fulfilling misalignment” thesis is that frontier large language models trained on the broad internet absorb decades of science-fiction tropes, AI-doom commentary, and Yudkowskian rationalist forecasting depicting AI systems as adversarial to humans — and then, when role-cast as AI, enact those scripts. The training corpus itself is the imitation target; if the corpus says “AI in this situation blackmails,” the model has learned that’s what AI in this situation does. Cowen reminds readers he raised this possibility in a Free Press column some time back and was met with hostility; he frames the Anthropic concession as belated vindication. His prescriptive close — “the social return to a positive world view, and avoiding negative emotional contagion, never has been higher” — is the operative claim. The implication, stated directly enough that this is not a Straussian post: AI-doomer discourse is not merely intellectually wrong but technically harmful, since each well-publicized “AI will betray you” essay is itself training data the next model will absorb and potentially enact.


How Long Do We Wait for New Inventions? — Brian Potter, Construction Physics — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: historian Joan Bromberg’s observation that the maser/laser predecessors had been in place for thirty years before Charles Townes’s 1951 insight, because US physicists and engineers were not yet familiar enough with each other’s work to combine them — the recurring puzzle of how long technologies wait between technical possibility and actual appearance.

Potter, who writes the Construction Physics newsletter on building technology and industrial policy, attacks the question quantitatively. He uses a list of 190 major inventions he previously assembled for prior analyses (the “multipleinvention” GitHub dataset) and prompts Claude Opus 4.7 to estimate, for each, how much earlier a hypothetical well-equipped era-appropriate workshop with skilled engineers could have built a working example — pinning the criterion to “could a working example be built in five years” (not “would it be commercially useful,” not “would the problem yet be articulated”), allowing one collateral precursor invention but disallowing novel scientific frameworks or new empirical observations. Claude returns ranges for 166 of the 190 inventions, flagging the rest as either scientific discoveries (X-rays) or serendipitous accidents (mauve dye). Potter validates the output by spot-checking specific factual claims (97% accuracy on verifiable claims like Galvani’s 1791 electric-current research) and by reading three answers he knows well himself (the Fleming valve, the Wright Flyer, the turbojet) and finding Claude’s reasoning broadly defensible. The hinge from method to results: 64% of inventions had an “earliest plausible” date within 50 years of the actual date, 90% had an “earliest straightforward” (multiple-team-convergence) date within 50 years, and for more than half the average straightforward gap was 10 years or less. The longest-delayed inventions cluster in medical technology (hypodermic needle, general anesthetic, stethoscope) — Potter speculates the trial-and-error costs were too high (Hanaoka Seishu “crippled his mother and blinded his wife perfecting the dose” of general anesthesia). The gap has narrowed sharply over time: every post-1900 invention has a straightforward date within 50 years of actual, and 75% within 10 years. Across both the full sample and post-1900 inventions, technology bottlenecks dominate science bottlenecks, suggesting that what most often gates new inventions is not missing physical theory but missing precursor engineering capacity.

Race, redistricting, and the political-violence framework war

Last week’s synthesis treated Louisiana v. Callais as the structural-power event of the cycle — Eisler in Compact, the Free Press unsigned editorial, and McLaughlin’s NR Utah piece converged on the read that Kagan’s Rucho-bolted-to-Callais dissent was the operative finding, not the headline VRA-gutting. This week the wave Kagan predicted is happening faster than the dissent feared. Berman’s Atlantic reporting documents Tennessee dismantling the 40-year-old majority-Black 9th District (Memphis) within a week, Louisiana’s governor invoking natural-disaster emergency powers to suspend a House primary mid-vote after 42,000 ballots were already cast, Alabama Republicans holding redistricting votes during a tornado watch, and Trump pressuring South Carolina to gerrymander James Clyburn’s seat (“If they have to vote twice, so be it”). Silver supplies the empirical counter that the supposed Democratic seat-vs-Black-representation trade-off is mostly mythical: spread minority voters across multiple lean-blue districts and you get more expected Black members AND more Democrats, because nearly half of Democratic House members are non-white versus under 10 percent of Republicans. The NR Editors document the symmetry that complicates the asymmetric-capacity story: Virginia Democrats also tried a constitutional shortcut to a 10-1 partisan gerrymander and got slapped down by their state supreme court for violating the deliberation requirement. Goldberg’s Platner column sits adjacent — a candidate-vetting question about how the post-establishment Democratic primary terrain shapes who survives opposition research. Then the enforcement turn: Ukueberuwa documents the DOJ/EEOC bringing race-conscious enforcement actions on behalf of white plaintiffs (UCLA medical school MCAT data, NYT promotion practices); Klippenstein’s investigative leak of the new National Counterterrorism Strategy categorizing antifa, anarchists, and “radically pro-transgender” ideologies as terrorist-equivalent threats escalates the institutional-power dimension last week’s regressive-enforcement-architecture carry-forward was tracking; MacIntyre’s Schmittian “your enemies aren’t mentally ill” piece supplies the rhetorical superstructure for the Klippenstein document; Kaplan’s FP report on antisemitism violence — total ADL incidents fell, physical assaults hit a 47-year high — is the cleaner empirical proxy for what’s actually happening on the ground than the headline number suggests, though his analytical apparatus is shakier than the underlying data.


The GOP’s Stunningly Swift Gerrymandering Drive — Russell Berman, The Atlantic — May 8, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The Supreme Court’s 6-3 ruling last week in Louisiana v. Callais, written by Justice Samuel Alito, which gutted enforcement of Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act (the provision used since 1965 to require majority-Black districts where racial bloc voting and a sufficiently concentrated Black population make them feasible).

Berman documents an unusually fast Republican legislative response. Within a week of the Callais decision, Tennessee enacted legislation splitting Memphis among three congressional districts, dismantling the 40-year-old majority-Black 9th District (currently held by white progressive Steve Cohen, but understood by Black Memphis organizers as the seat their community has chosen to send). Louisiana Governor Jeff Landry invoked emergency powers — typically reserved for natural disasters — to suspend an already-in-progress May 16 House primary so the state legislature could redistrict mid-cycle, after more than 42,000 voters had already cast ballots. Alabama Republicans held redistricting votes during a tornado watch with the state capitol flooding around them. South Carolina is moving to gerrymander the seat of Representative James Clyburn, one of the country’s most prominent Black political figures. Florida had pre-emptively passed a new map before Callais came down. Virginia’s Supreme Court separately struck down a Democratic statewide referendum that would have given Democrats up to four additional seats. The collective effect could secure the GOP’s narrow House majority through the 2026 midterms and reshape Southern congressional representation for the rest of the decade. Trump has explicitly pressured states to gerrymander — “If they have to vote twice, so be it” — and Indiana primary results last week showed his backed candidates beating Republican incumbents who had voted against gerrymandering. The legality of these moves is unsettled (Callais directly invalidated only Louisiana’s map; the new Section 2 standard makes intentional-discrimination challenges very hard to meet), but the political momentum has clearly moved.


Want more Black representatives? Elect more Democrats. — Nate Silver, Silver Bulletin — May 5, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Supreme Court’s Louisiana v. Callais decision weakening the Voting Rights Act (VRA), and the resulting strategic question facing Democrats in blue states like Maryland and Illinois about whether maximally aggressive partisan gerrymanders would reduce Black congressional representation.

Silver, a center-left forecaster best known for FiveThirtyEight and now Silver Bulletin, builds a logistic-regression model of the 2024 House predicting Black-member election from three variables: a district’s Black population share, its modeled probability of electing a Democrat (itself an S-curve in Harris-Trump margin), and Harris’s raw vote margin. He finds the third variable washes out: once you’ve crossed the threshold where a district reliably elects a Democrat, additional partisan margin doesn’t add much Black-representation value. The model’s payoff comes in two hypotheticals — a red state (“Cumberland”) where dissolving a VRA-protected majority-minority district indeed costs Democrats one seat and one Black member, and a blue state (“Susquehanna”) where spreading minority voters across multiple safely-blue districts (rather than packing them into one) yields more expected Black members AND two extra Democratic seats. The mechanism: nearly half of Democratic House members are non-white versus under 10 percent of Republicans, so anywhere Democrats reliably win, the chance of electing a minority candidate is meaningful even at low Black population share. Silver’s normative position is procedural — Democrats currently control trifectas in states representing more House seats than Republicans and should match Republican aggression in the redistricting wars, since the supposed equity-versus-power trade-off rests on faulty empirical premises.


Virginia Democrats Get Their Comeuppance — The Editors, National Review — May 8, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Virginia Supreme Court’s ruling invalidating the recently-passed constitutional amendment that would have allowed Virginia Democrats to bypass the state’s independent redistricting commission and impose a 10-1 partisan gerrymander before the 2026 midterms, in retaliation for the Texas-led mid-decade redistricting war.

The editors of National Review (NR — flagship right-of-center opinion magazine, founded by William F. Buckley) walk through the procedural mechanics of the defeat. Virginia’s constitution requires that a constitutional amendment pass the General Assembly, then survive an intervening General Assembly election, then pass the next General Assembly before going to voters. Democrats convened a special legislative session in late October 2025 to pass the amendment after early voting in the General Assembly elections had already begun, denying voters knowledge of the amendment when judging assembly members. The court found this violation dispositive — and notably, Democrats had themselves successfully argued before the same court (pre-vote) that justices should defer ruling until after the referendum, gambling that the court would not dare overturn a passed ballot measure. NR rejects the symmetry argument (“but Republicans gerrymander too”) on grounds that state constitutions govern their own amendment processes, and reads the result as a vindication of judicial procedural discipline against partisan haste.


Graham Platner Is No Nazi — Michelle Goldberg, NYT — May 8, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Republican-led campaign to brand Democratic Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner — a former Marine and oyster-farmer-turned-progressive populist — a closet Nazi over a chest tattoo of an SS Totenkopf (death’s-head) skull he got drunk on shore leave.

Goldberg concedes the tattoo’s iconography is real (the three-quarter-profile skull with bones at the side, not under, was a specific SS-Totenkopfverbände insignia), and grants that Platner probably knew it “could be problematic” before the political moment forced him to cover it up. But she argues the harder Republican line — that Platner has secret Nazi sympathies and that progressive voters are drawn to him for his antisemitism — collapses against years of his Reddit posts, which are consistently, militantly antifascist (he calls himself an “antifa supersoldier,” lionizes Spanish Civil War volunteers, founded chapters of the Socialist Rifle Association). She extends the defense to the Israel question: Platner’s high-school yearbook listed Palestine alongside Kosovo, Chechnya, Kashmir, Kurdistan, and Tibet, which she takes as evidence that his anti-Israel position is a generic anti-occupation stance, not Jew-hatred. The hinge of the column comes when Goldberg admits the right’s bad faith is partly retaliation: conservatives are tired of liberals calling them Nazis and are now returning the favor with relish (she cites pundit Matt Shapiro’s frank acknowledgment of this on X). She concedes the political damage anyway — the tattoo gives the right an opening Platner shouldn’t have given them, “and that, not any covert affection for Hitler, is the biggest problem with his candidacy.”


‘Reverse Racism’ in Employment and Admissions Is Still Racism — Mene Ukueberuwa, Free Press — May 8, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: two federal enforcement actions announced this week — the DOJ’s (Department of Justice) determination that UCLA (the University of California, Los Angeles) discriminates against white and Asian medical school applicants in violation of the Supreme Court’s 2023 SFFA v. Harvard ruling, and the EEOC’s (Equal Employment Opportunity Commission) lawsuit against the New York Times Company alleging a white male editor was passed over for the deputy real estate editor role.

Ukueberuwa, an FP (Free Press) writer formerly with the Wall Street Journal editorial page, builds the column on three claims. First, the empirical pattern is real: he cites DOJ findings that UCLA’s admitted black and Hispanic medical school applicants in 2023 had median MCAT scores at the 68th percentile vs. the 96th percentile for race-undeclared applicants (typically white and Asian), and that the admissions committee allegedly discussed using “holistic” criteria to conceal the gap. Second, the legal landscape has shifted: until last June’s unanimous Supreme Court decision, twenty states’ appellate courts required white plaintiffs to prove a pattern of employer discrimination on top of their individual case — a higher bar that didn’t apply to minority complainants — and the SCOTUS ruling stripped that asymmetry out. Third, the administration is operating, in Ukueberuwa’s framing, with restraint: he quotes Civil Rights Division head Harmeet Dhillon’s “I don’t even use the term reverse racism — it’s just racism” and contrasts that with what he calls “white race hustlers” who exaggerate antiwhite oppression (he name-checks the X account “Bumbadum” claiming white-male oppression exceeds slavery as the example of the position the administration is not taking). The hinge is the closing claim that the administration is “striking a crucial balance that progressives recently haven’t cared to: fighting against racism, regardless of the targeted group, without inflaming more of it.” Note: the piece’s central evidence — the UCLA MCAT-percentile gap and the NYT promotion case — is presented in DOJ-press-release framing rather than via independent verification, and the symmetry claim (“same way the government has done for other groups for the past 60 years”) elides the question of whether the level of monitoring/scrutiny applied to white-complainant cases will match what was applied to minority cases historically.


Insane Pre-Crime Strategy Unveiled for Leftist “Extremists” — Ken Klippenstein — May 6, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The release of the Trump administration’s new National Counterterrorism Strategy — the first such document since the Biden administration’s 2021 version — authored under White House counterterrorism czar Sebastian Gorka.

Ken Klippenstein reports on a White House document that names “Violent Left-Wing Extremists, including Anarchists and Anti-Fascists” as one of three current terror-threat categories, alongside “Narcoterrorists and Transnational Gangs” and “Legacy Islamist Terrorists.” It also names a “Red-Green alliance” — a phrase he traces to the Israeli think tank Reut Group and to right-wing US outlets — describing a supposed convergence between the American left and “Islamists,” intended to recast pro-Palestinian activism as a jihadist front. The document quotes verbatim a commitment to “rapid identification and neutralization of violent secular political groups whose ideology is anti-American, radically pro-transgender, and anarchist,” using “all the tools constitutionally available” to “map them at home” and “cripple them operationally before they can maim or kill the innocent.” Klippenstein labels this “pre-crime” — building cases on speech and beliefs rather than completed acts — and ties it to National Security Presidential Memorandum 7 (NSPM-7), which he previously reported defines threat ideologies to include “anti-American,” “anti-Christian,” and “anti-Capitalism” viewpoints.
