The document’s own framing invokes the September 10, 2025 assassination of Charlie Kirk at Utah Valley University as justification, citing “the assassination of Charlie Kirk by a radical who espoused extreme transgender ideologies” as part of the rationale for the new transgender-targeting category. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent called the killing “a domestic 9/11” and pledged to deploy financial-mapping techniques developed against al Qaeda against domestic political networks. Klippenstein notes the document’s attempted inoculation — “Our counterterrorism powers will not be used to target our fellow Americans who simply disagree with us” — and observes that the operational text immediately contradicts the assurance.


Your enemies aren’t mentally ill. They apparently just want to kill you. — Auron MacIntyre, The Blaze — May 7, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Original commentary, with no specific triggering news event named. The piece sits within MacIntyre’s running post-liberal/Schmittian brief — that liberalism’s neutrality on ultimate questions is collapsing and an existential friend/enemy politics is returning.

The argument runs in three steps. First, MacIntyre claims modern Americans default to pathologizing political opponents because the materialist worldview has crowded out moral language: with no concept of evil, every divergent value system gets coded as a defective brain. Second, this pathologizing tendency is reinforced by liberalism’s foundational bargain — taking existential questions (Who is right about God? What is the ultimate good?) off the political table in exchange for economic cooperation and rising standards of living. Third, that bargain was always provisional and is now collapsing; the underlying value disagreements never went away, and Americans now lack the moral vocabulary to recognize them. The framing example is “if the progressive is a perfectly sane person and still wants to take your son if you do not chop off his genitals” — i.e., gender-affirming care for minors framed as a sane-person hatred rather than mistaken-belief disagreement. MacIntyre’s conclusion: when rational debate is no longer available because the moral premises don’t overlap, the only resolution is that one side wins and imposes its way of life on the other; in the worst case, this means violence.

But the analysis flags that the central framework rests on a strawman that misrepresents the actual usage pattern of mental-illness language in political discourse, and elides the question of which side is currently building institutional capacity for the “imposition” outcome MacIntyre describes — leaving the brief functionally as an apologia for one-sided political escalation while presenting itself as neutral diagnosis.


Antisemitism Is Getting More Violent — Josh Kaplan, Free Press — May 8, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the 2025 audit report from the ADL (Anti-Defamation League) released this week, which showed total U.S. antisemitic incidents dropped from 2024’s record high of 9,354 to 6,274 — the first decline in five years.

Kaplan, writing the FP (Free Press) global antisemitism beat, argues the headline number conceals a substitution effect rather than a genuine retreat. The decline is concentrated in one category — campus incidents — and reflects post-2024 administrator response to lawsuits, congressional hearings, and Trump-administration threats rather than a softening of underlying hostility; the ADL report itself notes campus incidents remain three times higher than 2021 levels. The category Kaplan flags as the actual signal: physical assaults, the only category that grew in 2025, reaching a 47-year high. He cross-references a Tel Aviv University report showing 2025 was the worst year for global antisemitic violence in three decades, with violence rising rapidly across Western countries. The interpretive move he draws: the substitution from speech-incidents to violence “puts a nail in the coffin” of the claim (which he attributes to the anti-Zionist left) that much of what’s labeled antisemitism is just protected criticism of Israel. Kaplan invokes the late UK chief rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks: “Antisemitism is a projection… caused not by Jews but by internal conflicts in the societies that give rise to it.” The bulk of the piece is then an unstructured incident roundup — Park East Synagogue protest with the Mamdani administration’s controversial spokesman response, a University of Michigan commencement-stage incident, the Georgetown Law student-petition campaign against an Israeli economist, a Queens swastika-tagging spree, an AI-generated TikTok rabbi network amassing 10M likes, a UK synagogue arson, Green Party candidates under antisemitism investigation, a Berlin “Kill all Jews” graffiti episode, Belgian charges against two Jews for performing ritual circumcisions, Venice Biennale protesters screaming “murderer” at the father of a Nova festival victim, and the Dam Square Remembrance Day defacement.

China and the structural dependencies the West has not priced

This week’s largest cluster organizes around three concentric layers of the China question, each filling in a piece the others leave implicit. The outer layer is macro: Tooze’s Chartbook 446 registers “Chinamaxxing” — his Anglosphere-coined term (the “-maxxing” suffix is online slang for over-investing in a position) for the current Western enthusiasm for Chinese consumer culture, technocratic governance, and online aesthetics — as the soft-power signature of post-hegemonic vacuum, while Douthat’s Interesting Times podcast with Ray Dalio supplies the same data from a hedge-fund-principal register: the US is following every prior reserve-currency empire’s debt-cycle decline, with Iran-war credibility risk as the Suez-equivalent crystallizing event. The Tooze-Dalio pairing is the synthesis composer’s most important pickup — same diagnostic data (debt overhang, eroded multilateral order, China as both transactional medium and savings-glut counterparty), opposite sociologies (Dalio reads it as a leadership-of-the-middle problem requiring a Plato-style “benevolent despot”; Tooze’s frame would treat it as a class-distributional problem about who absorbs the adjustment). Both pieces carry fundamental-flaw flags preserved below — Dalio for the financialization-causation slip (debt service crowds out investment without specifying whether the channel is cost-of-capital or demand/governance), and Tooze 446 for closing on a polycentric-soft-power frame that lets him sidestep the harder, testable Wang claim that CCP repression itself is suppressing Chinese cultural exports. The middle layer is sectoral and corporate-strategic: Tooze’s Chartbook 445 catalogues China’s anomalous lurch into agricultural deficit (sub-70% self-sufficiency, soybean imports driving 73-83% of Brazilian exports) and the Systemiq/CAU scenario in which Beijing applies its new-energy industrial-policy toolkit to ag, potentially collapsing US ag exports to China by 85%+ over 15-20 years; Pettis at Commonplace argues the WTO must shift from rules-enforcement to outcomes-targeting (constrain the surpluses themselves rather than enumerate the policies that produce them), with a fundamental-flaw flag for under-attributing demographics, comparative advantage, and commodity terms-of-trade to behavior his framework treats as policy-driven; Cain at Commonplace documents that Tim Cook didn’t merely depend on China’s supply chain but materially built China’s electronics manufacturing ecosystem ($275B in supplier upgrades 2016-2021, more than five times the entire CHIPS Act authorization), with the Apple-Alibaba censored-AI partnership as the kicker; Cass at Commonplace previews next week’s Trump-Xi Beijing summit by arguing the Busan ceasefire critics misread restraint as capitulation while the FCC quietly builds a multi-front containment architecture (drone and router authorization bars, lab-certification reciprocity, Chinese state-owned-carrier exclusion, foreign-adversary disclosure rule). The inner layer is the from-inside-China register the rest of the cluster lacks: Sun Liping’s WeChat essay on platform-driven destruction of small-city economic ecosystems (JD.com chain restaurants vs. local stalls, money flowing out of third-tier cities to Beijing/Shanghai/Hangzhou, the Lake Victoria Nile-perch analogy as ecosystem-collapse warning), and three CDT translations documenting the architecture of state-engineered atomization — Beijing’s pressure on Zambia to cancel RightsCon 2026 over Taiwanese delegate participation, Wade’s translation of an anonymous censored essay on the decade-long suffocation of a Beijing Normal University student magazine (Three Reviews, Three Proofs censorship + WeChat open-rate collapse + Douyin attention drain working jointly), and Wade’s translation of Peng Yuanwen and rural activist Zhou Jian on engineered urban-rural misperception (the dibao asset-test paradox: “you’re an individual until it’s time to share the financial burden, then you’re part of a family”). The cluster’s load-bearing convergence — across Tooze-Dalio macro, Cain-Pettis-Cass corporate/policy, Sun-Wade interior — is that the dependencies the West has not priced run in both directions: the US is structurally exposed to a Chinese industrial-policy turn it has no instrument to deter, and China’s domestic legitimacy is being eroded by platform-economic and atomization dynamics the CCP narrative apparatus is designed to suppress.


Chartbook 446: Soft power with Chinese characteristics? Notes on Chinamaxxing. — Adam Tooze, Chartbook — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a salon Tooze co-hosted in New York with Dan Wang (author of Breakneck) and Iza Ding on the recent Anglophone vogue for things Chinese, plus Ding’s Ideas Letter essay “the grinning defiance of Chinese soft power.”

Tooze coins “Chinamaxxing” — Anglophone-coded slang for over-investing in a position — to describe the current Western enthusiasm for Chinese consumer/online culture, technocratic governance, retro-authentic street life, wellness, internet humor, “cuteness” (Labubu dolls), and cross-cultural eros. He distills seven distinct vectors from the panel and notes the panelists agreed China itself is not in a vibrant cultural moment, so the West-side enthusiasm has to be explained by China’s economic rise, the sophistication of its consumer-internet layer, and contrast against Anglosphere malaise. Tooze accepts Ding’s point that Chinamaxxing falsifies the liberal premise that a CCP-controlled society cannot generate soft power, but registers skepticism (shared with the panel) that Beijing has the cultural-strategy apparatus to skillfully exploit it — no analogue to the CIA’s mid-century support for abstract expressionism. He places the moment in historical lineage (1960s “Mao-maxxing,” 1970s Hong Kong-kung-fu enthusiasm, the longer East Asian developmentalist admiration arc through Japan/Korea/Taiwan) to argue it’s overdetermined rather than singular. The piece closes on Dan Wang’s counterpoint — that Beijing’s repression is minimizing China’s cultural exports, and a freer China would be more influential — and pivots to the structural question: in a polycentric world without an American-style hegemonic footprint, what should the baseline expectation for cross-cultural influence even be? FUNDAMENTAL-FLAW FLAG: the closing pivot to “soft power beyond hegemony” lets Tooze avoid the harder testable question Wang actually raised — what’s the elasticity of Chinese cultural exports with respect to domestic political constraint? The polycentric-world framing is a distraction from a more specific empirical question Tooze has the apparatus to engage but doesn’t.


Chartbook 445: Is a “China shock” coming for the “big ag” food regime? — Adam Tooze, Chartbook — 2026-05-03 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Tooze’s own FT op-ed published the same weekend; a Systemiq report (“China’s Food Future”) commissioned by the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation in collaboration with China Agricultural University; Harriet Friedmann and Philip McMichael’s “food regimes” framework; Karl Polanyi’s “double movement” concept; ERS/proag.com data on Brazil-China-US soybean interdependence; Xi Jinping’s 2022 “food security” rhetorical frequency (mentioned every five days during the shock year).

Tooze’s setup is that global food trade operates within heavily regulated “food regimes” — production-and-consumption blocs structured by national policy and incumbent corporate interests, designed to prevent rather than enable dramatic trade-flow shifts. Within that frame, China’s trajectory since the 1990s is genuinely anomalous: starting from minor agricultural trade with a small surplus, China has become the world’s largest agricultural-import-deficit country with sub-70% self-sufficiency. The comparison cases (US: imports up but balanced trade; Mexico: shifted into surplus with US under NAFTA; EU: widening export surplus) underline how unusual China’s lurch into deficit is. Crucially, Tooze argues this is additive rather than substitutional: China hasn’t redistributed an existing diet, it’s transformed the national diet via a huge protein-consumption surge driven by meat and fish, while remaining largely self-sufficient in basic grains (with massive corn/rice/wheat stockpiles built via price subsidies). The deficit concentrates in animal-feed soybeans, where Chinese demand drives global dynamics — Brazil’s soybean export surge is 73-83% China-bound, with US production a secondary supplier. Tooze’s central question: in a “geopolitical environment of growing stress,” what happens if Beijing applies the multi-faceted industrial-policy toolkit (state-directed investment, R&D subsidies, demand-shaping, scale-and-cost compression) that worked spectacularly on new-energy industries to agriculture as well? The Systemiq/CAU report identifies the policy-shift signs between the 14th and 15th Five-Year Plans and projects Chinese imports from the US could fall by more than 85% on the 15-20-year industrial-policy timeline. Tooze is careful to frame this as scenario-exploration not prediction — the point is “how radically new the current configuration is” and how quickly Chinese industrial policy has historically reconfigured basic global parameters once activated.


How—and Why—to Save the World Trade Organization — Michael Pettis, Commonplace — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the WTO’s March 2026 Yaoundé, Cameroon meeting, which (per Reuters) confirmed the U.S. trade chief’s view that the organization has only a limited role in the emerging global trade reorganization.

Michael Pettis — Beijing-based economist (Peking University Guanghua School of Management) and Carnegie Endowment senior fellow whose decades-long framework treats global trade imbalances as the consequence of demand-suppressing industrial policies in surplus economies — diagnoses the WTO’s irrelevance as a structural mismatch between its rules and the economic mechanisms that actually produce trade distortions. The textbook purpose of trade is mutual welfare: countries expand exports to maximize the value of imports, shifting production to where it’s most efficient. But for decades, surplus economies (Japan, Germany, China, South Korea) have used exports to externalize weak domestic demand created by industrial policies that suppress household consumption — bank credit-allocation controls, repressed deposit/lending rates, restrictions on labor unionization or migration, production subsidies, infrastructure overbuilding. These industrial and financial policies are, in Pettis’s framing, far more effective at distorting trade than tariffs and quotas, but they’re the policies the WTO’s rules largely ignore. The Anglophone deficit countries (U.S., U.K., Canada) absorbed the imbalances via rising household and fiscal debt, stagnant wages, and deindustrialization — an arrangement that looked stable but, per Joan Robinson’s 1937 “beggar-thy-neighbor” warning (which Pettis cites as foundational), couldn’t last because surplus-recycling produces debt, asset bubbles, and unemployment in deficit countries that eventually become politically intolerable. Pettis’s prescription is to reform rather than abolish: the WTO should distinguish “benign” imbalances (advanced-economy savings funding productive investment in developing economies) from “harmful” ones (surplus recycling into demand-constrained deficit economies), prioritize welfare-enhancing outcomes over rules adherence, scrutinize persistent surpluses, coordinate exchange-rate policy, and consider distributional effects. He invokes Keynes’s 1944 Bretton Woods proposal — contain the surpluses themselves rather than try to enumerate every distorting policy — as the operational template. FUNDAMENTAL-FLAW FLAG: the framework treats current-account surpluses as fundamentally policy-driven (industrial policy → demand suppression → forced surplus) rather than as outcomes that can also reflect demographic structure (aging societies save), comparative advantage in tradable goods (Germany’s machine-tools sector), or commodity terms-of-trade (oil exporters). Mainstream economists who reject Pettis’s framework do so partly on this point — they think his model over-attributes savings-glut behavior to deliberate policy. Also: the operational implication (“constrain the surpluses themselves”) in the absence of a surplus-currency-appreciation mechanism collapses into “the WTO should sanction the U.S. tariff regime as a structural-imbalance corrective,” which isn’t really saving the institution so much as repurposing it.


Tim Cook Built Apple in China, but Beijing Owns the Keys — Geoffrey Cain, Commonplace — 2026-05-03 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Apple’s April 2026 announcement that Tim Cook will become executive chairman on September 1, with John Ternus succeeding him as CEO — and the broader question of what Cook’s 28-year supply-chain bet on China leaves his successor.

Geoffrey Cain — investigative journalist with Korea/China expertise, author of The Perfect Police State on Xinjiang surveillance — assembles a documentary case across multiple registers: Cook’s own statements (the March 2024 China Daily declaration that “there’s no supply chain in the world that’s more critical to us than China”; his July 2017 defense of removing dozens of VPN apps from the Chinese App Store; his December 2017 keynote at China’s World Internet Conference; the false-equivalence framing — “every country in the world decides their laws and regulations” — at the Fortune Global Forum), corporate-strategic facts (the closures of Apple’s California, Singapore, and Ireland plants; the buildup of Foxconn’s Zhengzhou “iPhone City” producing roughly half of global iPhones at peak; Patrick McGee’s Apple in China claim that Apple invested $275B upgrading Chinese suppliers between 2016-2021 — more than five times the entire CHIPS Act authorization), and quantified-exposure data (Strategy Risks ranking Apple second among 250 large American firms for China exposure; JP Morgan estimating ~95% of Apple products still assembled in mainland China and Taiwan; nearly half of Apple’s top 200 suppliers with facilities there). The piece’s central narrative arc is the contradiction between Cook’s statements (“forced labor is abhorrent … we would not tolerate it at Apple,” July 2020 to Congress) and reported behavior (the Washington Post’s November 2020 report that Apple paid Fierce Government Relations $90,000 to lobby on multiple bills including provisions of the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act). The closing pivot is the AI-development story: Apple Intelligence runs on American iPhones but not on Chinese ones because China’s Cyberspace Administration regulatory approval has not come; Apple is partnering with Alibaba (per The Information, February 2025) to build a censored version that will refuse to discuss the same topics — Tiananmen, Taiwan, Xinjiang, Communist Party authority — Cook himself has refused to discuss. Cain’s verdict: “the supply chain Cook built was once seen as Apple’s greatest achievement. Now it has become its chain.”


Beijing Summit Preview Edition — Oren Cass, Commonplace — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the upcoming Trump-Xi summit in Beijing, set to follow the October 2025 Busan ceasefire, and Cass’s accompanying May 8 New York Times op-ed arguing that the U.S. cannot accept a “grand bargain” balanced economic relationship with China because the asymmetry of the two systems guarantees the U.S. gets ripped off.

This is a Commonplace newsletter (Oren Cass’s editorial vehicle for American Compass, the post-liberal economic-nationalist think tank he founded) with the bulk of the analysis written by his colleague Daniel. The piece’s central move is to defend the October Busan ceasefire (which dropped China’s share of U.S. imports to 7.5% — erasing more than two decades of post-WTO-accession growth) against critics who read the cooling-off period as Trump going soft, and instead argue that “restraint” buys time for a multi-front structural decoupling that’s proceeding faster than headline coverage acknowledges. The piece focuses on a Federal Communications Commission (FCC) program less covered than tariffs or critical-minerals policy: December 2025’s bar on foreign-made (overwhelmingly Chinese) drones from receiving the equipment-authorization required to enter the U.S. market, March 2026’s parallel bar on foreign-made routers, rules adopted last year and a proposal advanced in April barring labs owned by foreign-adversary governments from testing U.S.-bound electronics (Chinese labs handled around 75% of U.S. electronics testing during peak globalization), an April 2026 notice of proposed rulemaking barring Chinese state-owned carriers from operating U.S. data centers and points of presence, and a January 2026 disclosure rule requiring nearly every license-holder in the U.S. tech, media, and telecom sectors to certify foreign-adversary control of as little as 10% ownership. The newsletter then loosely drifts into the standard Commonplace Friday-roundup format: a strong-jobs-report data bump (115K April payrolls, calibrated against the Veuger/Edelberg/Watson estimate that immigration enforcement has driven breakeven employment growth as low as 20-50K/month), Pettis-flagged commentary on global imbalances, a “compute tax” framing for AI revenue, Stanley Tools closing its Connecticut tape-measure plant, the Intel/Apple chip-fab rapprochement, and Spotify/IBM citations on AI-as-growth versus AI-as-headcount-cut.


A Legendary Investor on How to Prevent America’s Coming ‘Heart Attack’ — Ross Douthat (interviewing Ray Dalio), NYT (podcast transcript) — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: an “end of the American empire” mood Douthat says he has been feeling — driven by the stalemated Iran war, Trump’s strain on alliances, and the perception that China is biding its time waiting for American collapse — and an interest in pressure-testing that mood against Ray Dalio’s pattern-based historical model.

Dalio is the founder of Bridgewater Associates (one of the world’s largest hedge funds, built on systematic macro investing) and author of Principles for Dealing with the Changing World Order, the book that codified his “big cycle” framework. He brings three interlocking cycles to the conversation: a debt cycle (the US now spends about $7 trillion against $5 trillion in revenue, with debt at roughly six times income, a structurally unsustainable position visible across the past 500 years of reserve-currency empires), a domestic political cycle of irreconcilable wealth and values gaps, and an international cycle in which the post-1945 multilateral rules-based order has effectively collapsed into a power-rules-resolution regime. He develops the Suez analogy — Britain’s loss of Suez in 1956 crystallized a pre-existing financial decline — and applies it to the Iran war: a perceived US loss (Strait of Hormuz access, Iranian regime survival, nuclear materials disposition) would, on Asian leaders Dalio recently met, prompt the same defend-us-or-not reassessment that ended British primacy. He distinguishes the two functions of money — medium of exchange and storehold of wealth — and argues that while China’s renminbi is becoming a transactional medium, no fiat currency will be a credible storehold; gold has been the second-largest reserve currency for central banks for some time. The famous “heart attack” frame is Dalio’s reframe of Douthat’s “I’ve heard deficit warnings my whole life” objection: like arterial plaque, the buildup remains asymptomatic until it doesn’t, and the M.R.I. evidence is sufficient to warrant action regardless. On the technology side he stipulates AI as a triple-edged force — productivity that could mitigate debt, wealth-gap intensification approaching the first trillionaire, and harm-misuse risks. The closing prescription invokes Plato’s Republic on democratic decline and calls for a “strong leader of the middle” — a “benevolent despot” capable of forcing tax-and-spending compromise on a 60-percent-below-sixth-grade-reading-level electorate — toward a 3-percent-of-GDP deficit through some mix of taxes, cuts, and interest-rate suppression, while individuals diversify portfolios and hold 5-15 percent gold. Douthat probes the Japan-stagnation-as-soft-landing alternative; Dalio rebuts it on grounds that one-third of US debt is foreign-held and Japanese society absorbs depreciation differently than American society would. FUNDAMENTAL-FLAW FLAG: Dalio invokes a “debt service crowds out investment” mechanism without specifying whether the channel is a cost-of-capital effect (which the empirics of the last 20 years contradict — sovereign deficits ballooned alongside falling real rates and abundant capital) or a demand/governance channel (which they support); the slippage is doing significant work in the closing prescription, which slides from “we need fiscal adjustment” to “we need a Caesar.” Also: Dalio insists “this is much more like the ’30s” but won’t engage Douthat’s plausible point that wealthier societies absorb financial crises with smaller social-cohesion damage (1970s less severe than 1930s; 2008 aftermath less severe than 1970s) — the historical record actually supports a lower-magnitude prediction than Dalio’s frame implies.


Sun Liping: Money Is Hard to Earn — Where Does This Feeling Come From? — Sun Liping, WeChat (Sun Liping) — 2026-05-06 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: an anonymous online letter addressed to Liu Qiangdong (founder of JD.com, the Chinese e-commerce giant) about a meal at a JD-branded Qixian Xiaochu (七鲜小厨) chain restaurant, which the letter writer enjoyed but which made him feel worse the more he ate, because his friend’s small fast-food shop in a second-tier city had just had its takeout-platform recommendation weighting cut in half overnight.

Sun Liping (retired Tsinghua University sociologist, writing in Chinese on the WeChat account 老孙荐读, mirrored at szhgh.com) reads the letter as a parable of platform-economy displacement of local livelihoods. He extends it: in third-tier Chinese cities, money used to circulate locally — diners paid restaurant bosses who then ate, drank, and consumed locally — but now two outflow streams drain the cycle: online shopping (full payment goes to big-city head sellers) and takeout/ride-hailing/delivery (platforms take ~one-third per order, all flowing to Beijing/Shanghai/Hangzhou). The local economy “loses its lubrication … congealed and viscous.” Sun then introduces the piece’s central analogy: Lake Victoria in East Africa, where the 1950s introduction of the carnivorous Nile perch as a fishery-yield experiment caused the extinction of more than 200 endemic cichlid species; once the small fish were gone, the perch itself shrank and dwindled, and the whole ecosystem collapsed. The hinge: Sun concedes the standardized chain stores will likely replace street stalls, that this is “economic law,” and that progress can’t be stopped — but argues society is a complex, historically-evolved ecosystem with a fragile equilibrium, and that this round of AI-centered technological revolution may be the first transition in history where the food chain doesn’t grow richer but instead collapses. “Money is hard to earn” is, on his read, the first felt symptom of that structural shift, and it cannot be explained by the institutional/policy frames Chinese commentators (including Sun himself) usually deploy.


RightsCon 2026 in Zambia Cancelled Under Pressure from China — Cindy Carter, China Digital Times (discovery) — 2026-05-03 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the April 28-29 cancellation of RightsCon 2026 (the world’s largest digital-rights conference, organized by Access Now and held annually since 2011) in Lusaka, Zambia, with Access Now stating publicly that Chinese pressure was the cause.

Carter, writing for China Digital Times (CDT — a Berkeley-based aggregator translating censored Chinese discourse and tracking PRC influence operations), assembles a multi-source dossier on what happened. Zambia’s Ministry of Tourism and Service (MoTS) announced the “postponement” via state media on a national holiday with no prior consultation, then informally relayed (per Access Now) that the conference could proceed only if RightsCon excluded Taiwanese participants and moderated specific panels — Access Now’s “red line.” Carter cites parallel reporting from Wired (Vittoria Elliott and Zeyi Yang), Human Rights Watch, and Article 19’s Michael Caster at TechPolicy.press, all triangulating to the same chain: Chinese embassy displeasure at planned panels on PRC digital authoritarianism plus a Taiwanese delegation, Beijing’s pressure on Lusaka, and a Zambian government that had received $60 million from China to refurbish the very venue (Mulungushi Conference Center) plus a fresh cooperative-projects agreement signed a week earlier. Carter situates the incident inside a documented PRC pattern — pressuring Madagascar, Mauritius, and Seychelles to deny overflight to Taiwanese President Lai Ching-te en route to Eswatini; the 2025 forced closure of the IndieChina film festival in New York; censorship of a Bangkok art exhibition; the Thailand Uyghur deportations — framing RightsCon as a step-change in transnational reach, not an isolated event.


Translation: The Decade-long Death of a Campus Media Outlet (Part One) — Samuel Wade, China Digital Times (discovery) — 2026-05-06 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the April 2026 deregistration of Jingshi Xueren (“Capital Scholar”), a Beijing Normal University student-media WeChat account, prompting an essay posted to the WeChat account “Swimming Across by Moonlight,” subsequently censored, and archived at China Digital Times Chinese.

The translated essay — anonymized to “N University” and never naming the publication outright — narrates the slow erosion of a student journalism outlet across three phases. 2016-2018 (“Hotpot”) was the apex: a 48-page glossy print edition, a then-thriving WeChat public-account ecosystem, students self-directing investigations into delivery workers, retired athletes, and abolished academic departments — a “training ground for observing the world.” 2019-2022 (“Closure”) brought the eviction of nearby cultural institutions (the Shengshiqing academic bookstore, the East Gate snack street, Moxiang used bookstore), then COVID-era campus turnstiles and a Beijing Municipal Education Commission “semi-closed campus” policy that made off-campus newsgathering nearly impossible; the editor-in-chief recounts having to delete his own published article. 2023-2025 (“Suffocation”) was the structural kill: the outlet was folded into the school’s “official new media matrix,” WeChat’s open rate fell from ~8% (2016) to ~1% (2025), Douyin short-video consumption rose to ~200 clips/day per user, and the central “Three Reviews, Three Proofs” (三审三校) tiered-review censorship system landed across universities (cited examples: Shanxi University, Fuzhou University, Minzu University, Guizhou Normal University, Hubei Second Normal University). The translator’s framing — Samuel Wade for CDT — explicitly threads this to the broader decline of Chinese journalism, the detentions of investigative reporters Liu Hu and Wu Yingjiao, and the collapse of the journalism major (graduate-employment-rate ranked in the bottom quartile of all majors; 2021’s 19.42% in-field hire rate falling to 11.01% by 2025).


Translation: “Why Do Urban Chinese Have So Many Misconceptions About the Countryside?” (Part Two) — Samuel Wade, China Digital Times (discovery) — 2026-05-09 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a livestream conversation between journalist Peng Yuanwen (recent winner of the grassroots Journalists Home News Prize for his rural-pension reporting) and Zhou Jian, co-founder of the Beijing Gan’en Philanthropic Foundation working in rural poverty alleviation since the 2008 Sichuan earthquake — second of two parts on urban-Chinese misconceptions about rural life.

The translated dialogue methodically dismantles five urbanite assumptions about rural China. First, that elderly villagers are frugal-by-disposition and won’t spend windfalls — Zhou’s counter is that 200 yuan (~$30) gets saved for emergency hospital stays because it has to, but 2000 yuan would actually be spent. Second, that meat-only-at-weddings reflects taste preference rather than absolute-poverty desire-suppression. Third, that the dibao (低保, China’s minimum-income rural welfare program) provides adequate floor — Zhou shows the eligibility math (savings cap as low as 5,000 yuan / ~$735; children’s car ownership disqualifies parents) creates a logical paradox: “you’re an individual until it’s time to share the financial burden, then you’re part of a family.” Fourth, that urban ignorance of rural conditions is innocent — Zhou names “vested interests” directly, citing the silence of Beijing-based education-equity scholars whenever ending college-entrance-exam (gaokao) preferences for Beijing/Shanghai students comes up. Fifth, that “New Farmer” influencer videos depict rural life — Zhou points out the format consumes rather than portrays, with the camera on the urban benefactor handing a bag of rice rather than on the elder’s other 360 days. The piece closes on Zhou’s structural claim: the atomization separating rural elders, their migrant-worker children, and city-bound 996-mortgage-burdened young adults is engineered, not natural — “rural people’s invisibility isn’t because no one’s looking, it’s because people who are suffering are not allowed to be seen — because that would disrupt ‘harmony.’” Peng frames his own pension reporting and Zhou’s organizing as parallel projects to make these isolated populations recognize one another.

The energy-transition reversal accelerates — and the demand side wobbles

The cluster’s news-value this week is concentration: four substantive Heatmap pieces in a single week document a coordinated wind-energy reversal alongside a major demand-side data point. Kaufman’s reporting traces the supply-side siege — DoD permitting holdups freezing roughly 165 onshore wind projects, recycling the offshore-wind national-security playbook that federal courts already paused once; mounting blowback (House Democratic probes, a California regulatory inquiry, and a New York State Common Retirement Fund divestment review) against the $1B cash-for-quitting deal with TotalEnergies; and the SEC formally ending Biden-era climate disclosure rules while simultaneously cutting quarterly reporting to twice-yearly. Meyer’s Heatmap Pro exclusive supplies the demand-side surprise: at least 20 data center projects worth $41.7B and 3.5GW canceled in Q1 2026 after local opposition — a single-quarter record that puts 2026 on pace to exceed all of 2025 within the first five months. The Meyer data is the most analytically interesting because the buildout-resistance story complicates the AI-compute-demand-is-infinite narrative the rest of the discourse takes for granted (cross-reference Cowen’s “AGI Could Lower Interest Rates” in Theme 04 — both pieces are about whether the AI/compute story has the financial-market signature people assume). Kaufman’s secondary observation on the SEC double-move — that quarterly-reporting reduction could ironically help long-horizon decarb investment by relaxing managerial short-termism — is worth flagging as a cross-orientation finding: a Trump-administration deregulatory move with a possibly-positive transition-investment side effect.


Trump Halts Construction on Onshore Wind Turbines — Alexander C. Kaufman, Heatmap (publication) — 2026-05-04 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a Financial Times scoop that the Trump administration, through the Department of Defense, is halting routine military sign-off for onshore wind projects on private land — putting as many as 165 wind farms on indefinite pause since last August.

Kaufman, in his Heatmap AM newsletter, frames this as an extension of Trump’s December 2025 stop-work order on offshore wind, which courts paused within weeks. The Pentagon-radar review for onshore turbines is normally a formality completed in days; developers report it has become an open-ended block, including for projects far from any military base. The siege strategy, in Kaufman’s framing, is to make the regulatory environment “uninvestable” rather than to win on the merits — and developers are again expected to litigate. The piece bundles four other items (oil majors resisting Trump’s pressure to ramp Permian production amid the Strait of Hormuz crisis at $126/barrel; Ontario hitting a milestone on North America’s first small modular reactor, the GE Hitachi BWRX-300 at Darlington; the administration’s continued legal effort against New York City congestion pricing despite supportive polling; and NOAA approval of The Metals Company’s deep-sea mining application in the Clarion-Clipperton Zone).


Blowback Is Building Against Trump’s Cash-For-Quitting Offshore Wind Scheme — Alexander C. Kaufman, Heatmap (publication) — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the unraveling political and legal aftermath of the March 2026 deal in which French oil major TotalEnergies accepted roughly $1 billion to abandon two U.S. offshore wind leases, plus follow-on cases involving California’s first floating offshore wind project.

Kaufman’s lead-item reporting (with credit to Heatmap colleagues Emily Pontecorvo and Jael Holzman) traces three converging blowback vectors: House Democrats Huffman and Raskin opening a congressional investigation into whether the Total contract met the legal-settlement requirements of the federal coffer it was drawn from; California regulators probing the floating offshore wind cancellation; and the New York State Common Retirement Fund — a roughly $1.6 million stake but a signaling pension fund — publicly considering divestment over Total’s “backtracking” on transition commitments. Comptroller Thomas DiNapoli’s letter to Total CEO Patrick Pouyanné cites credible-transition-plan risk to the fund’s voting calculus. Kaufman notes France itself just energized a new floating offshore wind farm. The piece bundles separate items on Occidental’s repeatedly delayed Stratos direct-air-capture facility in West Texas (now indefinitely postponed for “non-process” component issues), expanded Trump sanctions on Cuba forcing Canadian miner Sherritt International out (shares down 41%), and a Volkswagen Q1 disclosure that its newest electric vehicles are 80% as profitable as their internal-combustion counterparts.


The SEC Stops Asking About Climate Risks — Alexander C. Kaufman, Heatmap (publication) — 2026-05-06 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the SEC’s (Securities and Exchange Commission) May 4 submission to the White House Office of Management and Budget of a proposal to formally rescind the 2024 Biden-era climate disclosure rule, plus a separate SEC proposal to switch publicly-traded companies from quarterly to twice-yearly reporting.

This is a Heatmap “AM” newsletter format — a numbered five-item news round-up. The lead item is the SEC double-move. The 2024 rule (already weakened during finalization to drop Scope 3 emissions, those from suppliers and end-users) had required public companies to disclose material climate risks plus, for large firms, Scope 1 (direct operations) and Scope 2 (purchased electricity) emissions. Kaufman, the Heatmap newsletter editor, reports the proposal is now at OMB review, citing Bloomberg’s pickup of a regulatory tracker. The reporting-frequency change — pushed by SEC chair Paul Atkins and quoted to the Financial Times as giving companies “regulatory flexibility” — was a Trump idea floated years ago. Kaufman flags an interesting cross-current: this deregulatory move, packaged as anti-ESG (environmental, social, and governance), could actually help climate action because long-horizon decarbonization bets are penalized by quarterly-reporting cycles. The remaining four items: (2) Wave-energy startup Panthalassa raises $140M Series B led by Peter Thiel for floating data centers using ocean-wave power off Oregon, valuing the company at ~$1B; competitor Aikido Technologies has launched floating platforms with data-center-in-ballast designs. (3) A Nature Climate Change paper from Fudan University and U.S. co-authors finds atmospheric microplastics absorb sunlight and amplify warming. (4) Geothermal startup Fervo Energy filed for a $1.3B IPO at a $6.5B valuation, with reviewed leases representing ~40GW (≈15% of installed U.S. solar capacity). (5) UAE state developer Masdar signed a 30GW solar plus 8GW battery deployment deal with Emirates Water and Electricity Company, with a 2035 target of 60% Abu Dhabi clean-energy share. Kicker: Norway is rolling out Europe’s first driverless self-driving bus (no onboard supervisor) in Stavanger.


Exclusive: Local Opposition to Data Centers Explodes in 2026 — Robinson Meyer, Heatmap (publication) — 2026-05-06 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the cancellation in February of the proposed 1-gigawatt, $13.5B Sentinel Grove Technology Park (“Project Jarvis”) near Port St. Lucie, Florida, after Governor Ron DeSantis proposed AI regulation in the state legislature; Meyer uses it as the lead example for a broader Heatmap Pro database survey.

Robinson Meyer — Heatmap co-founder, formerly Atlantic climate reporter — reports an original quantitative finding from Heatmap Pro’s national county-by-county survey: at least 20 data center projects were canceled after local pushback in Q1 2026, representing more than $41.7 billion in committed investment and at least 3.5 gigawatts of electricity demand. This breaks the previous single-quarter record set in Q4 2025. Roughly 100 new local data-center fights were added to the database in Q1, also a record. For context, all of 2025 saw roughly 25 cancellations nationwide; 2026 is on pace to exceed that within the first five months. Cumulatively, Heatmap Pro’s data shows at least $85 billion in canceled data center projects over the past three years. Meyer notes the cancellations are happening even when the proposed facilities are not running AI models, and even when developers preemptively address common objections (Project Jarvis’s developers offered self-contained water facilities and 60-foot setbacks from the road and still lost the planning-board vote). The methodology is described briefly: Heatmap Pro researchers periodically call every U.S. county to tally cancellations and new construction-limiting rules. The piece is a high-level summary of data normally restricted to Heatmap Pro subscribers. Note: the article presents cancellation counts and dollar figures without disclosing what fraction of proposed data centers were canceled (a denominator-free numerator), so the trend signal is real but its scale relative to total proposed pipeline is unstated.

Beyond the metropole: Africa, Russia, Brazil

This week’s periphery cluster does what the Anglophone roster otherwise can’t: it puts five concrete state-fragility and state-society-rupture cases into a single frame, none of which would have surfaced through the standard US-and-Europe commentariat. Appiah-Mensah on the April 25-27 Mali attacks identifies a JNIM-FLA hybrid jihadist-separatist convergence that simultaneously invalidates the Goita junta’s sovereignty narrative and the Russian Africa Corps security model that replaced ~20,000 international troops with 1,000-2,500 paramilitaries dependent on Malian units for perimeter security — and pairs directly with Aurelien’s Mali example in Theme 01 as cross-source confirmation of the hybrid-threat finding from independent vantage points. Ilo on Nigeria diagnoses a three-mechanism democratic erosion (weakened electoral act + mass governor defections to the ruling APC + judiciary captured to dismantle opposition party structures) that produces de facto one-party dominance long before the 2027 vote — a sub-national elite-capture model distinct from the mass-mobilization backsliding pattern of Hungary or Turkey. Okumu’s UNHCR-AI piece is the cluster’s sharpest cross-theme connection: a fast-cycle empirical case of the AI-augmentation-vs-replacement question Klein, Smith, and the broader AI cluster in Theme 04 are debating in the abstract, with the additional analytical move that the room-reading peer-navigator labor — performed largely by refugees themselves and producing measurable depression-trajectory costs — is structurally invisible to procedural-record analysis and therefore defundable once efficiency metrics certify the AI’s success. Sergeeva (Persuasion’s first appearance in this cluster) reads Putin’s spring 2026 mobile-internet shutdowns as the regime crossing a tolerance threshold by breaking the apolitical-consumerist bargain itself: an 8-point Putin approval drop, public criticism from non-political influencers like Victoria Bonya, and Putin himself walking back the rhetoric — though as the summary flags, the title’s finally-claim does more work than the body fully defends. Rojas closes the loop on the Western Hemisphere: the Trump-Lula rapprochement, brokered partly by oligarch Joesley Batista, is driven by US rare-earth dependency on China, but Lula’s domestic concessions to capital — the USA Rare Earth purchase of Serra Verde, the apparent abandonment of state-owned Terrabras, real-terms minimum wage stagnation — may have already cost him reelection against Flávio Bolsonaro.


Bamako under Siege: How Coordinated Attacks Exposed the Mali Government’s Fragility—and Tested the AES — Seth Appiah-Mensah, African Arguments — May 4, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The coordinated 25-27 April 2026 attacks across Mali by Jamaʿat Nuṣrat al-Islām wa-l Muslimīn (JNIM, the al-Qaeda Sahel franchise) and the Tuareg Azawad Liberation Front (FLA), including the reported fall of Kidal; an Africa Center for Strategic Studies assessment; FLA spokesman Mohamed Elmaouloud Ramadane’s BBC confirmation that the operation was pre-planned in alliance with JNIM; JNIM’s 28 April announcement of a total siege on Bamako.

Appiah-Mensah, an Africa-security analyst, frames the attacks as the culmination — not deviation — of a multi-year deteriorating trajectory under General Assimi Goita’s military junta, with militant-Islamist fatalities reportedly tripling since the 2021/2023 takeover. The strategically novel element is the JNIM-FLA convergence: ideologically opposed (jihadi versus Tuareg-separatist) but tactically aligned with a clean division of labor — JNIM provides asymmetric warfare and operational depth, FLA contributes local intelligence, mobility corridors, and territorial access. This breaks counterinsurgency’s foundational logic of separating jihadists from local grievances. The simultaneous strikes across ~1,500km — Bamako, Kati, Sévaré, Mopti, Gao, Kidal, plus aviation infrastructure — were a “demonstration of reach,” not a territorial campaign. Appiah-Mensah catalogues the junta’s structural failures: opening a second front against Tuaregs while jihadist threat grew; replacing ~20,000 international troops with ~1,000-2,500 Russian Africa Corps personnel dependent on Malian units for perimeter security; civilian alienation from heavy-handed tactics driving rural populations toward insurgents; and shrinking political space. He frames the Alliance of Sahel States (AES — Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger’s break from ECOWAS) as facing a defining moment: its absent coordinated response and “monstrous plot” rhetorical framing reveal it as political construct without functional defense capability. The piece closes with calls for re-engagement by ECOWAS, AU, UN, and the US — explicitly rejecting the disengagement-reduces-threat thesis.


Nigeria’s Road to Undemocratic Elections in 2027 — Udo Judo Ilo, African Arguments — May 5, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The recently amended Nigerian Electoral Act; defections of at least five opposition governors to the ruling All Progressives Congress (APC) over six months (the APC now controls about 31 of 36 states); the Independent National Electoral Commission’s (INEC) withdrawal of recognition for the main opposition party’s leadership based on a contested Court of Appeal interpretation; the Nigerian Bar Association’s rebuke of court interference in internal party affairs; reports of FCT-administered house-construction for Abuja judges under a senior ruling-party figure (Wike).

Ilo, a Nigerian governance analyst, argues the 2027 contest is being foreclosed before voting begins through three reinforcing channels. First, the National Assembly’s failure to mandate real-time electronic transmission of polling-unit results — despite years of civil-society demand following the 2023 election’s manipulation allegations — leaves the gap between local collation and final tabulation across Nigeria’s 176,000+ polling units exposed to interference. Second, the wave of opposition-governor defections to the APC reflects not ideological alignment but survival strategy: federal control over security agencies, anti-corruption bodies, and financial flows to states makes opposition-status materially costly, producing de facto one-party dominance long before ballots are counted. Third — and Ilo calls this the most dangerous — judicial weaponization. Courts have decisively reshaped internal-party leadership in the People’s Democratic Party (PDP), Labour Party, and African Democratic Congress (ADC) through injunctions arriving at politically convenient moments. The construction of houses for judges under a key APC political figure’s supervision raises direct patronage concerns. Ilo situates Nigeria within a global pattern of gradual democratic erosion through legal ambiguity, institutional capture, and quiet opposition-neutralization rather than dramatic coup, and warns international observers against accepting procedural compliance as evidence of genuine competitiveness. The closing frame: with inflation above 30%, 40%+ poverty, and persistent insecurity, accountability requires meaningful electoral choice — and Nigerians may reach 2027 without one.


The Machine Cannot Hold — Moses Okumu, African Arguments — May 7, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: UNHCR’s September 2025 AI Approach document deploying algorithmic systems for Refugee Status Determination (RSD), early-warning displacement forecasting, and online-threat detection; UNHCR Innovation’s subsequent piece on AI analysis of refugees’ Telegram and Facebook content; a 2025 Cambridge Prisms: Global Mental Health study on depression trajectories in unpaid versus paid peer refugee helpers; the author’s 2025 study of urban displaced youth in Kampala’s informal settlements.

Okumu, a Kampala-based researcher who built the Tushirikiane peer-navigation program for refugee youth in Uganda, opens with a scene: in 2019, a 24-year-old refugee-serving-organization worker named Phiona walks into the YARID office and reads the room — light, intake-form language, staff body language, whether the researcher is checking his phone — in under 90 seconds, deciding whether to place sex-worker and LGBTQ youth into a study in a country whose laws (the 2023 Anti-Homosexuality Act) criminalize both. She stays. Across six years and 45 peer-reviewed papers naming research-PIs, her name appears in none, because peer review has no authorship category for the person who made the research possible. Okumu’s central argument is that this room-reading, trust-brokering, neighborhood-knowledge labor — overwhelmingly performed by refugees themselves, often unpaid, with documented depression-trajectory costs — is the actual nervous system of humanitarian systems, and is structurally invisible to UNHCR’s AI Approach because it cannot be digitized into procedural records. He concedes RSD-throughput is a real problem (years in legal limbo cause documented harm) but insists protection is not a throughput problem. The empirical anchor: in his 2025 Kampala study, even participants with the highest digital health literacy had suboptimal awareness of and access to sexual health services — the navigators, not the apps or SMS or validated instruments, were the variable that translated digital access into uptake. His prediction: faster AI-driven decisions will produce appearance of improved protection; navigators will still be in the actual room doing the work the algorithm can’t see; eventually a budget-cutting program manager will note the AI is doing the processing and ask what these community workers are being paid for. He calls this not malicious but “what optimization does when the thing being optimized is care,” and asks UNHCR to name the labor before scaling makes it invisible on paper and defundable in practice.


Russians Are Finally Angry About Censorship — Ivetta Sergeeva, Persuasion — May 9, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the spring 2026 wave of mobile-internet blackouts across major Russian cities (officially justified as protection against Ukrainian drone attacks), disruptions to Telegram and WhatsApp, and pressure on VPNs that have begun reaching Moscow itself, breaking ATMs, banking apps, taxi services, and even officially approved “white-list” websites.

Sergeeva (a Senior Research Associate at George Washington University’s Elliott School and co-founder of OutRush, a longitudinal survey of Russian post-2022 emigrants) argues that the Kremlin has misunderstood the social bargain it spent two decades building. The unusual visibility of public anger is the load-bearing observation: in April 2026, Putin’s approval dropped below 80% for the first time since fall 2022 (per Levada), losing 8 percentage points since September 2025; even apolitical influencers like Instagram celebrity Victoria Bonya posted a viral appeal to Putin on internet shutdowns; Putin himself reportedly told officials to “explain restrictions better” and lawmakers “not to get stuck on prohibitions.” Sergeeva’s analytical move is to distinguish political censorship (which Russians have lived with since the 2022 Meta ban — VPNs are everyday infrastructure, illegal Instagram still drives 48% of influencer income) from infrastructure breakage. The post-2014 social contract was: be apolitical and consumerist, and the state will preserve everyday convenience; the new shutdowns break the second half of the bargain. She quantifies the digital economy stakes: 133 million Russian internet users, Telegram with 61 million daily Russian users, Avito (Russia’s Craigslist equivalent) at 72 million monthly users and 230 million listings, online schools earning 168 billion rubles in 2024 (16 billion in “psychology” alone), Russian gaming spend at 170-200 billion rubles. She frames this through Alexei Yurchak’s late-Soviet concept of vnenakhodimost — being inside the system but mentally elsewhere — arguing that today’s internet performs that escape function, so breaking it invades the fragile private zones where Russians have learned to hide from politics. The MAX state messenger app (mandatory pre-installation since 2025, integrated with government services, suspected of surveillance use) is positioned as the regime’s authoritarian replacement plan. The hinge of the column comes when Sergeeva contrasts Putin’s analog generation (he famously called the internet a “CIA project” in 2014) with a digitally integrated society, calling this “the collision between two Russias.” She closes with a Yurchak-flavored prediction: in autocracies, anger has no democratic outlet, so it expresses itself either through emigration (already an ongoing trickle) or through the kind of sudden, retrospectively-obvious illegitimation collapse the late USSR experienced — “society may also change once again while its rulers are ‘sleeping,’ until a seemingly stable system suddenly discovers that society has no need for it.” The title’s finally-timing claim is doing more work than the body fully defends — the celebrity-influencer behavior and Putin’s rhetorical adjustments are also consistent with cyclical irritation rather than threshold-crossing delegitimation, and Russia-watcher predictions of imminent illegitimation collapse have a poor historical track record.


Why Lula and Trump Made Up — Juan David Rojas, Compact — May 8, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Thursday state visit between Trump and Brazilian president Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, following the unwinding of last August’s 50% US tariff on Brazilian products (originally imposed as retaliation for Brazil’s Supreme Court convicting and imprisoning former president Jair Bolsonaro for his role in the post-2022-election coup attempt) and ongoing US-Brazilian negotiations over rare-earth minerals.

Rojas, a Compact and American Affairs contributor focused on the Iberian Peninsula and Latin America, traces the arc: Trump’s August 2025 tariffs were lobbied for by Eduardo Bolsonaro (Jair’s youngest son, dispatched to Washington), backfired politically by allowing Lula to recover from a record-low approval rating by holding firm, drove a Brazil-China trade boom, and were unilaterally dropped by Trump three months later. The journalist Glenn Greenwald has reported that Lula charmed Trump partly by invoking their shared experience of legal jeopardy (Lula was jailed two years on a São Paulo apartment-bribery charge widely viewed as politicized lawfare). The hinge of the piece is Brazil’s strategic asset: it holds the world’s second-largest rare-earth reserves but currently exports almost nothing because — like the United States and unlike China — it lacks extraction and refining capacity. Last month USA Rare Earth’s $2.8 billion purchase of Goiás-based Serra Verde Group (Brazil’s only operating rare-earth mine) handed effective control of fledgling production to a foreign multinational, and Lula’s government simultaneously appeared to back away from creating Terrabras, the planned state-owned extraction company. Hours before Lula met Trump, Brazil’s lower house passed a unanimous regulatory framework creating a council with veto power over foreign mining acquisitions and tax credits for domestic refining — though whether the bill survives the Senate is open. On Venezuelan oil, Lula notably conceded Trump’s intervention “had been a success,” with Brazilian oligarch Joesley Batista (who reportedly brokered the Trump-Lula meeting) positioned to enter Venezuela’s post-Maduro oil sector. The closing argument: rapprochement has politically benefited the opposition; Lula’s approval has slumped to the low-to-mid 40s as the public punishes him for capitulations to the central bank, foreign NGOs, the São Paulo financial sector (“Faria Lima”), transgender activists, and “climate fundamentalists”; he’s now polling tied with Flávio Bolsonaro, who promises Milei-style shock therapy and tighter Trump alignment. Worst, the real-terms minimum wage has stagnated since Lula returned to office.

The Ozempic era and the contested body

The cluster organizes around what counts as a “real” cause of bodily and mental dysfunction — and what counts as legitimate intervention. Klein and Belluz supply the surprise empirical anchor: GLP-1 drugs are showing roughly a 20% weight-independent cardiovascular risk reduction, comparable to statins’ 29%, which implies the willpower/lifestyle/diet-and-exercise frame for chronic conditions is structurally obsolete in roughly the way it became obsolete for hypertension once statins existed. Goldberg uses The Devil Wears Prada 2’s box-office success to defend women’s professional-identity-formation against a tradwife-and-AI-displacement backlash that is being ratified inside the corporate retrenchment (Deloitte halving paid family leave; Palantir’s Karp openly celebrating AI’s disruption of “highly educated, often female voters”). Bohler’s Compact indictment argues that accreditation-mandated DEI in U.S. medical education has lowered admissions standards by race (AAMC data: accepted Black, Hispanic, and American Indian matriculants below the average MCAT for rejected white applicants) and then masked the resulting downstream performance gaps by abolishing the rulers — Step 1 conversion to pass/fail explicitly to “minimize racial demographic differences,” AOA disaffiliations, the >50% UCLA clinical-exam failure rate. deBoer pushes back on the progressive cliche that economic insecurity drives depression and anxiety, arguing the empirical case is weak (Hippocrates, Burton’s 1621 Anatomy of Melancholy, Nigeria reporting lower depression than the U.S., Twenge-era adolescent decline during 2014–2019 falling unemployment) and the “solve poverty first” frame defers real psychiatric investment indefinitely. The cluster’s underlying disagreement is who gets to define legitimate suffering — biomedical (Klein/Belluz on GLP-1s; deBoer on psychiatry as a brain-organ disease), institutional (Bohler on professional standards as the substrate of medical credibility), or political (the tradwife/anti-merit/anti-medication discourse Goldberg and deBoer both push back against). Bohler is on the institutional-conservative side of this disagreement; deBoer is on the biomedical-realist side; both push back against the same opponent — a discourse that prefers political reframing of measurable outcomes — from different angles.


We’re at the Dawn of the Ozempic Era — and It’s Really Weird — Ezra Klein × Julia Belluz, NYT (podcast transcript) — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a KFF poll finding one in eight U.S. adults is currently taking a GLP-1 drug (semaglutide marketed as Ozempic/Wegovy, tirzepatide as Zepbound, with retatrutide and other multi-receptor agonists in trials), and an emerging body of clinical findings that the drugs’ benefits extend far beyond weight loss into cardiovascular protection, inflammation modulation, and possibly addiction reduction.

The conversation is structured in five movements. First: mechanism — GLP-1 is a gut-and-brain hormone, and the drugs work by reaching the brain’s GLP-1 system through the blood-brain barrier and dialing down appetite, partly by acting on the brain region that signals “there’s a toxin in circulation,” which is why the most common side effect is nausea (“the wonder drug we’ve invented makes your brain slightly think it’s being poisoned all the time”). Second: the demolition of the willpower frame — Belluz argues that “common obesity” arises from more than 1,000 genetic variants almost all expressed in the brain, and Klein extends this with personal testimony (he lost 50-60 pounds at 16, has battled food desire ever since, but has no analogous craving for cigarettes or alcohol; the people who fight the chocolate cake aren’t lacking willpower the others have). Third: the weight-independent benefits — diabetes-trial cardiovascular harm-screening unexpectedly found a 20% reduction in cardiac events, comparable to statins (~29%), with parallel signals for liver, kidney, and possibly inflammation-mediated disease; researcher Dan Drucker’s three-bucket model is weight loss, inflammation modulation, and direct organ-signaling. Fourth: Klein’s own use — he tried low-dose tirzepatide and found it “revelatory” but anhedonic, “like being a gambler who loves slots and pulling the thing and getting the three cherries, and then nothing lights up,” supporting the picture that the drugs are doing something to the brain’s reward system, not just appetite. Fifth: the political-cultural mess — telemedicine and Chinese-compounded peptide gray markets (retatrutide is the current frontier, popularized by bodybuilding/longevity TikTok), the food-environment-vs-individual-choice question that Belluz wants to answer with paternalism (school-lunch reform, junk-food advertising bans, supermarket land-use policy on the French model) and Klein wants to answer with realism about voter resistance to that paternalism (Bloomberg’s failed soda tax, the COVID vaccine backlash that made RFK Jr. HHS Secretary), and a shared concern about MAHA’s hollowness — its rhetoric pointed at the food environment but in practice cut SNAP, killed local-produce-to-school programs, and took on no industry. The episode closes on a Buddhist note about the “great insight that desire and craving are the root of suffering,” with both speakers ambivalent about whether this generation of drugs and the broader optimization-culture moment will end well.


A Movie About Women Who Love Their Work? Groundbreaking. — Michelle Goldberg, NYT — 2026-05-04 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the box-office success of “The Devil Wears Prada 2” — second-largest worldwide opening of 2026 — and what Goldberg reads as its countercultural insistence that careers can be sources of genuine female meaning, set against a wider corporate-and-cultural retrenchment from the post-2010s “girlboss” moment.

Goldberg traces the arc from the original 2006 film (an early augur of #girlboss optimism, with Anne Hathaway’s Andy choosing the gold ring of a newspaper job over a sulking boyfriend) through the ideal’s collapse: two highly qualified women losing the presidency to Trump, Ruth Bader Ginsburg refusing to retire and costing Democrats a Supreme Court seat, Sheryl Sandberg’s Lean In feminism discredited as Facebook curdled, the boutique women’s co-working chain The Wing imploding. The opposing cultural pole is now ascendant: tradwife influencers, manosphere podcasters mocking women’s “email jobs,” Gen Z writer Freya India’s book Girls® lamenting that work “became an end in itself” for her generation, and the “I do not dream of labor” left-wing meme that converges with conservative critique. Corporate America, ever trend-sensitive, has followed suit — Deloitte halving paid family leave, return-to-office mandates hitting working mothers hardest, DEI programs quietly buried, and Palantir CEO Alex Karp openly celebrating that AI will disrupt the power of “highly educated, often female voters.” The Devil Wears Prada 2 is set inside this collapse: Andy’s newspaper has fired her by text, AI is hollowing out cultural institutions, and the men in charge of media are tech-bro philistines. The hinge of Goldberg’s column comes when she insists this isn’t depressing in the film: Andy and Miranda Priestly (Streep’s diabolical fashion editor, who muses about missing her children but says “Boy, I love working — don’t you?”) are both visibly fulfilled. The column closes on the rejoinder Goldberg wants to make to “I do not dream of labor”: some women do, in fact, dream of labor — the kind that comes with mastery, agency, and glamour — and the cultural permission to want that is what’s being withdrawn.


Medicine Without Merit — Forrest Bohler, Compact — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: his own four-year experience inside an unnamed US medical school — including a publicly tracked policy regime in which the Liaison Committee on Medical Education (LCME, the accreditor for US MD programs) embedded “mission-appropriate diversity outcomes” into Standard 3.3 in 2009, the Accreditation Council for Graduate Medical Education (ACGME) imposed parallel requirements on residency programs, and post-2023 institutions began renaming rather than dismantling DEI offices after the Supreme Court’s Students for Fair Admissions ruling.

Bohler, an incoming Penn plastic surgery resident, opens with his own initial rejection from his state medical school despite strong credentials and an admissions officer’s explanation that he didn’t fit “the demographic the school was prioritizing.” He then marshals Association of American Medical Colleges (AAMC) data showing accepted Black, Hispanic, and American Indian applicants matriculating with average MCAT scores below the average for white applicants who were rejected — and walks the same gap through GPA, clinical clerkship grades, USMLE Step 1, and residency attrition (URiM orthopedic surgery residents leave training programs at roughly four times the white rate; for Black residents specifically, more than seven times). He documents the policy machinery — LCME Standard 3.3 (2009), ACGME parallel mandates, the 2024 American Medical Association House of Delegates Resolution 309 endorsing disaffiliation from the Alpha Omega Alpha (AOA) honor society, the conversion of USMLE Step 1 to pass/fail explicitly to “minimize racial demographic differences” — and traces what he describes as a self-reinforcing cycle: disparities introduced upstream get rediscovered downstream, attributed to ongoing discrimination, and used to justify further DEI expansion. He cites the David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLA’s Justice Department finding of civil-rights violations and reported >50% failure rates on standardized clinical exams (vs. 5% national average) as a leading indicator of system-level decay. The hinge from data to lived texture is a series of vignettes: a faculty member confusing the Tuskegee Syphilis Study with the Tuskegee Airmen with no correction; residents joking after the Charlie Kirk assassination about hoping the responding surgeon was “a pregnant female surgeon of color about to get an abortion”; eponym deductions on anatomy exams.

The piece’s main argumentative weakness is selection bias on the qualitative anecdotes — readers cannot distinguish “this is the ambient norm” from “Bohler observed multiple shocking incidents.” But the quantitative spine (AAMC matriculation data, accreditation language, AOA disaffiliations, UCLA performance data) is checkable and the framework — that delayed downstream costs hide upstream policy decisions — has predictive content.


Please Stop Making Reductive Claims About Economic Hardship and Mental Health — Freddie deBoer, deBoer — 2026-05-04 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a viral Substack Note (55,000 likes) asserting a tight causal link between economic hardship and rising mental illness, and the broader progressive public-health discourse around it.

deBoer concedes upfront that economic stress contributes to psychic suffering and that he himself favors redistribution, then turns to attack the specific claim that poverty and inequality are the primary driver of clinical depression and anxiety. His evidence operates on three levels: historical (Hippocratic melancholia, Burton’s 1621 Anatomy of Melancholy, and the consistent identification of these conditions across Persian, Chinese, and Indian medical traditions show the conditions predate modern capitalism); biographical (Lincoln, Churchill, Styron, Robin Williams, Cobain — wealthy or powerful people with severe mental illness); and epidemiological (the WHO World Mental Health Survey shows Nigeria reporting lower depression than the U.S. despite 1/63rd the per-capita income, and Nordic welfare states have some of the highest antidepressant prescription rates). He invokes twin studies showing ~40-50% heritability for depression and ~80% for schizophrenia, then pivots to the Twenge data on adolescents: U.S. mental health worsened during 2014-2019 even as median incomes rose and unemployment fell to fifty-year lows. The hinge of the piece is a policy argument: if we sell the public on “fix inequality, fix mental illness,” we will both demoralize ourselves when the second metric fails to track the first AND risk defunding dedicated psychiatric infrastructure (drug research, therapy access, hospitals). He closes by accusing the cliche of doing “category erosion” — blurring the line between ordinary unhappy feelings and pathological brain disorders, which is precisely what his anti-antipsychiatry project is meant to fight.

The economy people actually live in

A cluster of pieces this week trades narrative for numbers, each correcting a common framing with empirical material the political discourse has not yet absorbed. Yglesias on “fixed incomes” demonstrates the trope describes 1946-1975 conditions and gets the contemporary picture backwards: today’s seniors are unusually inflation-protected (automatic Social Security cost-of-living adjustments since 1975, equity-heavy portfolios that price inflation in fast), while working-age private-sector workers have lost the cost-of-living-adjustment clauses that used to be union-standard and now have to bargain individually. Yglesias on teacher merit pay names a wonky-policy issue with both massive electoral upside (Broockman/Kalla find the centrist position generates a bigger vote-share boost for Democrats than girls’-sports moderation, asylum moderation, or transition surgeries for minors) and supportive empirical evidence (no studies find performance pay backfires; debate runs between enthusiasts and null-finders). Smith reframes Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez’s “you can’t earn a billion dollars” as an implicit indictment of all big business — almost every major American company was founded by a billionaire — and counters with a Knightian-uncertainty windfall-tax frame that, as the analysis block notes, makes a stronger pro-redistribution case than AOC’s. This extends Smith’s prior-week “millionaires-against-billionaires” argument that the May 2 synthesis logged as part of the wealth-concentration cluster; the AOC line is the data point Smith was waiting for. Cowen surfaces three NBER findings the political discourse has not yet absorbed: ICE intensity in the second Trump administration reduced undocumented work without producing labor-market spillover for U.S.-born workers — who are net harmed in immigrant-heavy sectors; extreme global poverty’s 1990-2015 decline was primarily within-cohort household-churn-driven, not long-trapped intergenerational escape; and residential exposure boosts cross-class marriage but not interracial marriage — segregation accounts for more than a third of class-based marital sorting but less than 5% of racial sorting. The Cowen sentences-to-ponder finding implies racial-integration discourse is barking up the wrong lever (the implicit Straussian read documented in the analysis block: if the spatial channel is small, the policy lever that targets the spatial channel cannot do most of the work the policy discourse asks it to do). Cowen’s Australia social-media-ban update closes the cluster: only one in four 14-15-year-olds compliant four months in, with effective enforcement requiring more invasive government internet monitoring — a policy result that pairs uncomfortably with the surveillance-architecture findings the regressive-enforcement carry-forward has been tracking.


Seniors aren’t living on “fixed incomes” — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — May 5, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: A Mark Kelly tweet (Senator Kelly, D-AZ) deploying the standard “seniors on fixed incomes” frame to argue inflation hits retirees hardest.

Yglesias makes a tight empirical correction. In the post-war decades, the trope held: Social Security cost-of-living increases were ad hoc and unpredictable, private-sector unionization was high enough that cost-of-living-adjustment (COLA) clauses were common in working-age contracts, and middle-class stock ownership was rare, so retirees disproportionately held nominal-dollar war bonds and defined-benefit pensions that inflation eroded. Three structural changes have inverted the picture. Since 1975, Social Security has had an automatic annual COLA, fully protecting the roughly 25 percent of seniors who depend on it for nearly all income. Affluent retirees are mostly in equities, where professional traders and algorithms price inflation in fast — a healthy 401(k) doesn’t require any owner action to track inflation. And working-age private-sector workers have largely lost COLA clauses, so they have to bargain individually for nominal raises, a costly process documented in recent work by Joao Guerreiro, Jonathon Hazell, Chen Lian, and Christina Patterson showing that wage-bargaining effort is itself a real cost of inflation. The remaining “fixed income” archetype — affluent enough that Social Security doesn’t dominate, but invested overwhelmingly in long-duration bonds — exists but is small and not particularly vulnerable. Closes with the observation that 28 percent of large 3+ bedroom homes are owned by empty-nesters and that boomer downsizing would be a fine outcome.


We should pay more for the best teachers — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — May 4, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: A new paper by political scientists David Broockman (Berkeley) and Josh Kalla (Yale) testing the electoral effect of Democratic candidates moving to the center on a range of issues, with Yglesias zooming in on one item from the survey: teacher performance pay.

The empirical finding driving the column is striking. Broockman and Kalla offered respondents a “liberal” position (hard to fire teachers, pay tied to seniority), a “centrist” position (hard to fire, but better teachers get paid more), and a “conservative” position (regular employment with merit pay and easier firing). 60 percent of voters take the conservative position, and a Democrat moving to the centrist version gets a bigger vote-share boost than from any cultural-issue moderation tested except affirmative action and racial targeting of small-business loans — bigger than girls’-sports moderation, asylum moderation, or transition surgeries for minors. Crucially, Democratic primary voters also overwhelmingly reject pure-seniority pay, so it’s a rare position that works in primary AND general. On the merits, Yglesias surveys the empirical literature: a meta-analysis finds an average positive effect of 0.043 standard deviations on student test scores, with no studies finding the policy backfires; the debate is between enthusiasts and null-finders. The Obama-era Race to the Top program failed because states submitted compliant grant applications without meaningfully changing evaluation systems. Successful examples exist — Dallas’s 2013 system (test scores plus observations plus student ratings, with bonuses for skilled teachers in high-needs schools), DC Public Schools’ system (which raised the floor by getting low-scoring teachers to either improve or quit), and rural Andhra Pradesh, India. Yglesias concedes teacher quality has modest impact compared to peer effects and out-of-school factors, but argues this is no reason to keep paying via pure seniority.


Do billionaires earn their money? — Noah Smith, Noahpinion — May 9, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
