Responding to: Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez’s recent declaration that “There’s a certain level of wealth that’s unearned…You can’t earn a billion dollars,” made via mechanisms she enumerates as market power, rule-breaking, labor-law abuse, and underpaying workers — extending Smith’s own prior week’s argument that contemporary American politics increasingly reflects a status conflict between millionaires and billionaires.

Smith reads AOC as making not a definitional claim about labor versus capital income (the socialist frame) or marginal-product (the free-market frame) but rather an empirical claim that billionaires can only reach that wealth level by doing something society ought to forbid. He challenges this on three fronts. First, the Taylor Swift counterexample — a self-owned music catalog and merchandising operation that produced $2 billion of net worth without any obvious rule-breaking, only the IP-monopoly grant any creative-economy artist enjoys. Second, an implicit-coverage argument: nearly every major American company (Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Nvidia, Walmart, candy companies, retail chains, newspapers) was founded by someone who became a billionaire from doing so, with rare exceptions (IBM, CVS, Walgreens, a few founders who lost their fortunes), so AOC’s indictment of billionaires implicitly indicts almost the entire productive capital structure of the economy and by extension the retirement accounts of middle-class workers. Third, an international comparison: every wealthy country has billionaires, and Sweden — the progressive-welfare-state paragon — has nearly twice as many billionaires per capita as the US, suggesting that “abolish billionaires” has no functioning model anywhere outside Cuba and North Korea. The constructive position invokes Frank Knight’s distinction between calculable risk and true uncertainty: vast fortunes come disproportionately from entrepreneurs willing to venture into the genuinely unknown (Bill Gates dropping out at a moment when no one had gotten rich in software), and society should treat this as windfall — taxed substantially under standard diminishing-marginal-utility logic — without conceding the anti-billionaire framing that bigness equals badness.


ICE has not improved U.S. labor markets — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — May 6, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a new National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) working paper by Elizabeth Cox and Chloe N. East — the first national, causally identified study of the labor-market effects of the second Trump administration’s heightened immigration enforcement (Immigration and Customs Enforcement, “ICE”) arrests.

The authors exploit geographic variation in arrest intensity using event-study and difference-in-differences designs (standard quasi-experimental methods that compare areas getting more enforcement to comparison areas getting less, before and after the intensification). The finding stated in the abstract Cowen quotes: in areas with the largest enforcement increases, work declined among likely-undocumented immigrants who remain in the country, AND there is no positive spillover for U.S.-born workers — instead, U.S.-born workers in immigrant-heavy sectors (construction, agriculture, hospitality, food processing) are net harmed. This is the labor-economist consensus on immigration enforcement bearing out in real time: the substitution thesis (raids open up jobs for citizens) requires that immigrant and native labor be near-substitutes within sectors, but in fact they are mostly complements — removing one input contracts the whole sector. Cowen offers no commentary, but the title states the operative claim plainly: “ICE has not improved U.S. labor markets.”


How Poverty Fell — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — May 8, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a new National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) working paper by Vincent Armentano, Paul Niehaus (UC San Diego, J-PAL co-founder), and Tom Vogl using cross-sectional and panel data from five countries that account for roughly 75% of the 1990-2015 global decline in extreme poverty (the share living below roughly $2.15/day in current World Bank terms, which fell from 36% to 9%).

The paper’s two headline empirical findings, both reported in the abstract Cowen quotes verbatim and adds no commentary to: (1) poverty decline was overwhelmingly a within-cohort phenomenon — the same birth cohorts moved out of poverty together over time, rather than poverty falling primarily because younger, more educated cohorts replaced older poorer ones in the population. (2) Within cohorts there is substantial churn — households move into and out of poverty repeatedly — meaning the conventional “poverty trap” framework underestimates mobility but the conventional “ladder out of poverty” framework overstates durability. The authors find no single dominant pathway: sectoral transitions (agriculture-to-services), migration, and changes in occupational choice and female labor force participation each explain part, but in most cases the majority of households exiting poverty did so without any of these specific transitions. Cowen’s choice to post the abstract straight implies he wants readers to absorb the empirical pattern itself.


Sentences to ponder — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — May 8, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a quoted abstract from a new National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) working paper by Benjamin Goldman (Cornell), Jamie Gracie, and Sonya Porter (Census Bureau).

The empirical finding: geographic exposure to people of different incomes meaningfully increases cross-class marriage (high-parent-income spouses marrying low-parent-income spouses), but the same exposure mechanism has no detectable effect on interracial (specifically White-Black) marriage. A spatial marriage market model attributes more than a third of marital sorting by class to residential segregation but less than 5% of marital sorting by race. The headline implication: the standard liberal causal story — that residential desegregation reduces sorting along all dimensions — turns out to be true for class and largely false for race. Cowen offers no commentary at all; the post is a quoted abstract with no framing line. This is a quintessentially Straussian post in his oeuvre. The implicit claim he is asking the careful reader to absorb, without saying it: racial endogamy in the United States is largely not an exposure-and-mixing problem (and therefore not solvable by integration policies that target exposure), but reflects preferences and/or non-spatial barriers that the desegregation discourse mostly does not address.


The social media ban in Australia, how is it going? — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — May 9, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a new National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) working paper by Leonardo Bursztyn (Chicago), Angela Duckworth (Penn), Rafael Jiménez-Durán, Aaron Leonard, Filip Milojević, Christopher Roth, and Cass Sunstein (Harvard Law) surveying 835 Australian teenagers four months after the December 2025 ban on under-16 social media accounts took effect — the first such national ban, now under consideration in over a dozen countries.

The empirical findings: only about one in four 14-15-year-olds comply; most banned teens believe their peers are still using banned platforms and cite social reasons for continuing use; teenagers report they would need roughly two-thirds of peers to stop before they themselves would stop, far above current compliance; and compliers are perceived as less popular than non-compliers, so the more socially influential teens disproportionately stay on. The mechanism is a coordination failure: social-media value is largely a function of who else is on the platform, so a ban that doesn’t approach the tipping-point compliance threshold will see compliance decay rather than rise. Cowen adds two pieces of commentary: an anecdotal corroboration from a smart fifteen-year-old in Australia (who notes the ban did reach LinkedIn for him), and a policy-direction line: “I would note there are more stringent measures, requiring more governmental monitoring and control of the internet, that perhaps could have a greater effect.” The post is not Straussian — the directional line at the end is the operative claim, even if hedged.

Liberalism’s exhausted vocabulary, the discourse infrastructure, and politics-as-vibes

This cluster organizes around the diagnosis that the public-political vocabulary built for a 1990s-2000s American consensus has run out of meaning, and that the institutional plumbing through which that vocabulary used to circulate (bundled newspapers, prestige prizes, the academy, the not-yet-captured public-intellectual ecology) has been hollowed out alongside it. Klein and Helena Rosenblatt anchor the historical move: an attempt to recover “liberality” as the pre-political virtue of citizenly generosity that liberalism-as-political-philosophy elbowed out in the Cold War, paired with Rosenblatt’s genealogical bombshell that “liberalism” was first coined as an insult in 1811 by Catholic counter-revolutionaries — meaning today’s post-liberals are reviving the original anti-revolutionary vocabulary almost word-for-word. Yglesias’s “political commentary should be boring” and Garber’s Ted Turner obituary independently converge on the same causal mechanism: the unbundling of news economics produced engagement-driven content, which produced degraded civic information. Leibovitz on the Pulitzer’s capture is the elite-credentialing-institution version of the same loss-of-trust story — though as the analysis flags, the central historical claim that the prize was once apolitical does not survive scrutiny, and the broader observation about institutional captures is doing the work. Gage on Disneyland-as-mythmaking-source-of-Reaganism is the historical-anchor counterpart: a region with thin local history became the laboratory for an American history-as-feeling political style. Yglesias’s anti-blitzkrieg piece extracts the tactical lesson Democrats keep refusing to learn from Trump’s collapse. Smith’s Obama retrospective and his dev-econ-defends-its-humility piece both run a “the prior consensus was actually right; the right-revisionist attacks are misreading the record” move, with Smith doing the work of defending an ostensibly exhausted center-left technocracy on its own terms. Moench’s IDW post-mortem makes the audience-capture-and-donor-incentives diagnosis explicit — and the analysis flags that his framework is sharper than his target. Douthat reads Dawkins’s swooning ChatGPT essay as a window into how AI will leave most people in unsettled metaphysical “mysterian” territory rather than atheist or devout, the religious-vocabulary mutation under machine intelligence. Jager closes the cluster with the academy-as-vocabulary-source critique: English faculty should stop running the cruel humanities-PhD pipeline and instead cultivate lifelong reading outside the academy. The through-line: the pre-Trump-era vocabulary — “liberalism,” “the news,” “the prize,” “the credential,” “the humanities” — needs to be rebuilt from materials inside the actual current world. Klein’s “liberality” recovery and Yglesias’s “boring” prescription are two contrasting attempts to do that.


The Book That Changed How I Think About Liberalism: The Ezra Klein Show — Ezra Klein, NYT (podcast transcript) — 2026-05-05 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Klein’s continuing project (announced for 2026 on the show) to read the liberal canon and figure out why a liberalism in opposition to Trump feels “exhausted” and “arid”; this episode foregrounds Helena Rosenblatt, CUNY Graduate Center historian, whose 2018 book The Lost History of Liberalism traces the word “liberal” back through liberalitas (Latin: free-and-generous) to a citizen-virtue tradition that predates “liberalism” as an ism by roughly two millennia.

The conversation has three movements. First, an etymology and pre-history: for Cicero, Seneca, and the medieval Christian tradition, “liberality” was the citizenly virtue of generosity, reciprocity, and moral self-cultivation expected of those with power and education — explicitly an aristocratic ideal, with the liberal arts education designed to form “freedom-loving and moral leaders” rather than to prepare students for jobs. Second, the 19th-century pivot: “liberalism” as a word was coined around 1811-12 in France as an insult by Catholic counter-revolutionaries, who accused liberals of selfishness, family-destruction, and sexual deviance — language Rosenblatt notes is now being recycled by post-liberals like Patrick Deneen. Third, the modern split: classical European liberalism (laissez-faire, small state) versus American/New Deal liberalism (interventionist, redistributive), with Bismarckian Germany ironically pulling English “new liberals” toward state-provided health, education, and worker protections after the 1870 Franco-Prussian War demonstrated that healthier, vaccinated soldiers won wars. Rosenblatt locates the moment the self-cultivation strand “dropped out” in the Cold War, when liberals retreated to property-rights and individual-rights formulations to distinguish themselves from fascist and communist projects of state-formed citizens. Klein’s main interpretive contribution is an Obama-era reading: liberals’ visceral revulsion at Trump (separate from policy) is downstream of the inherited liberality tradition’s belief that complex societies require certain ways of behaving toward each other; Trump’s rule-flouting is “almost separate from anything he believes — a profound challenge to what liberalism believes how you make a society work.” The closing exchange diagnoses the modern crisis as the over-extension of individualism into narcissism, the loss of vocabulary for “shared responsibility” or “common good,” and the betrayal of liberality by liberal elites who have lost the sense of being part of a citizenry. Rosenblatt’s prescription is unapologetically modest: not a policy program but a recovery of moral language about character, obligation, and self-improvement that liberalism stopped speaking when it became embarrassed to do so. The episode ends with book recommendations, including her husband Vasant Dhar’s book on AI — Klein quips, “Oh, liberals. Always scratching each other’s back.”


Political commentary should be boring — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The recent New York Times culture-section podcast on “microlooting” (a since-pulled episode that treated organized retail theft with what Yglesias considers transgressive-as-fun framing), plus the broader collapse of the line between political entertainment and political analysis.

Yglesias frames this as a generational shift in how political content gets produced and consumed. In the 1990s, he argues, transgressive left-coded music — Bratmobile’s “Polaroid Baby,” Rage Against the Machine’s Rodney-King-riot framing, Against Me’s “Baby, I’m an Anarchist” — operated with an understood layer of irony separating politics-as-entertainment from politics-as-analysis. People could rock out to “Fuck Tha Police” and still read Randall Kennedy’s “Race, Crime, and the Law” (a 1997 academic treatment of the dual problem of police bias and underinvestment in protecting Black property and lives) for actual policy thinking. The bundled-newspaper economics — earnest civic news subsidized by cartoons and gossip — preserved that distinction structurally. Today, Yglesias argues, every piece of content competes on its own and audiences increasingly demand that performers and the characters they portray be morally upstanding, which paradoxically pushes mass-market political commentary toward irresponsible takes calibrated for engagement. He concedes uncertainty about whether his concern is genuine wisdom or generational misreading (“I’m not totally sure I can tell when I’m being the wise elder…and when I’m being the old fogey who just doesn’t get the joke”) but anchors the worry in one specific causal claim: white-millennial anti-police politics circa 2020 was driven partly by a cohort whose ideas about Black public opinion came from hip-hop rather than from churches or barbershops. Hopes large language models may pull commentary back toward sobriety.


The End of the World as He Knew It — Megan Garber, The Atlantic — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Ted Turner’s death at age 87 (announced the day before publication).

Garber organizes the obituary around CNN’s rumored “doomsday video” — a segment commissioned in 1980 to air in case of nuclear war or environmental catastrophe, scored to “Nearer My God to Thee” (the hymn the Titanic’s musicians played as the ship sank). The tape, she argues, captures Turner’s combination of grandiosity and idealism: the network would be enduring enough to cover the end of the world, and would do so with dignity. She frames Turner as an “industrialist in the manner of Andrew Carnegie, a showman in the manner of P. T. Barnum” who founded CNN as a civic ideal — “American democracy, propelled forward by constant information” — but accidentally established the principle that on television, the worst sin is being boring (a point Garber attributes to Neil Postman’s early-1980s media criticism). The “24-hour news cycle” Turner pioneered has become a near-slur, a synonym for an environment that prizes outrage and outraged. Where the piece pivots to praise is on Turner’s philanthropic style: unlike Carnegie (libraries) or Rockefeller (medical institutions), Turner declined to brand his giving — Captain Planet (an environmental-superhero cartoon Turner pitched to Cartoon Network, which his company owned) advanced his cause but not his name. Garber reads this as evidence that Turner’s grandiosity coexisted with a kind of selfless humility, citing a 1988 New Yorker interview in which Turner described realizing his own ignorance and assembling experts to address what was “really happening” with the planet.


Back When the Pulitzer Meant Something — Liel Leibovitz, Free Press — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the 2026 Pulitzer Prizes announced Monday, including the inaugural Opinion Writing category (which replaced both Commentary and Editorial Writing this year) won by New Yorker writer M. Gessen for essays on rising authoritarianism.

Leibovitz — writing in FP’s (Free Press) “Things Worth Remembering” weekly column — opens with a long quotation from Jimmy Breslin’s 1963 column on Clifton Pollard, the gravedigger who buried President Kennedy, then reconstructs Breslin’s 1980 column on the John Lennon shooting. Both pieces, written for the New York Daily News, won Breslin the 1986 Pulitzer for Commentary “for columns which consistently champion ordinary citizens.” Leibovitz’s hinge claim is that Breslin understood that “history couldn’t be written exclusively from the halls of power,” and that this curiosity-about-strangers ethos is what the prize was supposed to reward. He then attacks this year’s Opinion Writing winner M. Gessen — including a sentence that calls Gessen’s chosen first-name grapheme and plural pronouns “microaggressions against the English language” — by listing three of Gessen’s winning column titles (all about Trump) and asking why Iran, Azerbaijan, or Cuba weren’t subjects worthier of an authoritarianism series. He places Gessen in a recent lineage of what he calls captured awards: Nikole Hannah-Jones’s 2020 Commentary Pulitzer for the 1619 Project (which Leibovitz dismisses as “fan fiction reimagining American history”) and last year’s winner Mosab Abu Toha (whom he accuses of repeating debunked claims about the al-Ahli hospital strike and denying that October 7 hostages were tortured). The closing move is consolation rather than reform: stop watching the prize, return to Breslin’s archive, trust that “the spirit of the Pulitzers” survives in working journalists who will never win one. Note: the piece’s central historical claim — that the prize was once apolitical and has now become political — is doing a lot of work without supporting evidence; Breslin himself was a political columnist who openly campaigned for liberal causes, so the contrast may be less about depoliticization than about which politics the judges now reward.


How Disneyland Remade American Politics — Beverly Gage, Free Press — 2026-05-07 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the U.S. semiquincentennial (250th anniversary of independence), and Beverly Gage’s broader project for This Land is Your Land: A Road Trip Through U.S. History, which surveys how Americans actually encounter their past at museums and roadside attractions.

Gage — Yale historian and J. Edgar Hoover biographer — uses Disneyland’s 1955 opening, with then-actor Ronald Reagan as one of three Hollywood co-hosts on the live ABC broadcast, as the pivot for an essay on how Orange County became the workshop of late-20th-century American conservatism. Her thesis is that the region’s relative absence of indigenous historical thickness was itself the enabling condition: a place “built itself almost entirely from scratch” became the perfect site to invent an American past untethered from local specifics. She tracks this through three sites: Disneyland itself (Frontierland’s Davy Crockett valorization, the now-quietly-removed Song of the South references in the original Splash Mountain, the Great Moments with Mr. Lincoln animatronic and its 2022 addition of Frederick Douglass); the Nixon Library and Museum in Yorba Linda (which presents Nixon as a milquetoast civil-rights/environmental-protection liberal and includes a replica White House East Room available for rental); and the Reagan Library and Museum in Simi Valley (where touch-screen games — “Cut Taxes Game,” “Knock Down Regulations,” “Reduce Government Spending” — replace any analytic engagement with the long-term effects of his tax policy). She argues both presidents shared with Disney an indifference to what she calls “the specificities of historical context,” in favor of “history as it felt.” Reagan’s 1964 “Speech” for Goldwater, his repeated visits to Mount Vernon, the Hermitage, the Alamo, Mount Rushmore, Stone Mountain, and Disney World, and his “Let’s Make America Great Again” slogan all flow from the same Orange County–forged template: presenting policy as restoration of a lost optimism. The closing essay-frame at Knott’s Berry Farm — Walter Knott’s 1966 brick-by-brick replica of Independence Hall, now a vestigial site beside flume rides — is meant as the final rhyme: an American history that was never quite real to begin with, now barely visible to the staff who work next to it. The piece is excerpted from Gage’s book published in April 2026, and FP frames it as part of its semiquincentennial series.


Don’t copy Donald Trump’s failed presidency — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — 2026-05-06 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a recent CNN quote from former FTC chair Lina Khan suggesting Trump’s term carries lessons for Democrats, and the Project 2029 effort (a Democratic counterpart to the Heritage Foundation’s Project 2025 transition planning) which Yglesias reads as quietly endorsing a Trump-style maximum-velocity governance template.

Yglesias argues Trump’s second term is, by his own metrics, a failure — approval lower than at any point between his first inauguration and Election Day 2020, despite Schumer’s and Jeffries’s “uninspired” opposition, despite Democrats’ deep dysfunction. So copying him is copying failure. Yglesias’s structural diagnosis: Trump has perfected the “blitzkrieg” approach pioneered by Biden — exploit reconciliation to glom every priority into one bill (the absurdly named One Big Beautiful Bill Act, including immigration enforcement funding traditionally inappropriate for reconciliation), use executive orders and DOGE-style firings based purely on what’s legally achievable rather than what’s wise, and pursue maximum partisan goals via 3-2 commission votes rather than 4-1 bipartisan settlements. The hinge of the column comes in the contrast with Reagan, “the most consequential president of my lifetime” — Reagan won a landslide re-election (vs. slim margin), passed the baton to a same-party successor, gained ground in state legislatures (the only postwar president to do so), and made subtle changes (indexing tax brackets to inflation) that compounded over decades. Yglesias’s policy critique of the Khan-era FTC is the load-bearing example: by pursuing maximum partisan antitrust action via 3-2 votes rather than building bipartisan FTC consensus, Khan motivated business interests to fight harder for a Republican administration and produced zero durable change in antitrust doctrine. The non-compete ban — which Yglesias agrees with on the merits — was dropped the moment Trump won; it now needs the bipartisan Murphy/Young bill to actually happen. He extends this to Trump’s failure to pass any substantial immigration legislation despite four years of nominal anti-immigration mandate (diversity visa lottery, family reunification, and TPS authority all still intact). The closing prescription is “Project ‘make correct decisions’”: presidents need help getting better information, avoiding epistemic bubbles, and figuring out which of their coalition’s pet causes are actually politically sustainable — not faster execution of pre-cooked agendas.


Barack Obama was a successful President — Noah Smith, Noahpinion — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a wave of right-coded revisionist attacks on Obama’s presidency (claims of Chicago-machine corruption, “speeches about whiteness”) that Smith finds factually unmoored, set against the still-very-high public favorability of Obama relative to Biden, Trump, Bush, and Clinton.

The piece is explicitly a 2022 repost (originally aimed at progressive critics) bolted under a new 2026 framing-essay aimed at conservative critics; Smith says the substantive defense is the same regardless of which direction the criticism comes from. The defense rests on three legislative achievements compressed into Obama’s first two years against the unrestrained-filibuster constraint: the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA, 2009), which Smith credits with accelerating recovery from a financial-crisis shock equivalent to 1929 into a six-year recovery rather than an eleven-year Depression and laying down green-energy learning-curve subsidies that paid off in the 2020s; the Affordable Care Act (Obamacare), modeled on the Bismarckian universal-coverage-via-mixed-insurers framework and Mitt Romney’s Massachusetts reform, which reduced the uninsured rate substantially even though it did not constrain underlying cost growth; and Dodd-Frank, which Smith argues was vindicated by the absence of a second financial crisis through the COVID shock. He grants the standard progressive critiques (stimulus too small, insufficient homeowner relief, too few prosecutions of bank executives) and the standard executive-action limits (DACA, Clean Power Plan as second-best regulatory substitutes after the Tea Party Congress arrived in 2010). On foreign policy he is harsher: Obama mostly closed out the War on Terror but failed catastrophically on the reemergence of great-power conflict — weak response to Russia’s seizure of Ukrainian territory, refusal to take Xi Jinping’s accession seriously, persistence with the failed Clintonian engagement-makes-China-liberal frame, and a “pivot to Asia” that was too little, too late.


Could development economics be more useful? — Noah Smith, Noahpinion — 2026-05-06 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a tweet from University of Pennsylvania economist Jesús Fernández-Villaverde (JFV) charging that the field of development economics has “largely given up” on the central question of why South Korea got rich and Bolivia did not, in favor of small-bore randomized controlled trials (RCTs) on microfinance and similar interventions — a critique with antecedents in Lant Pritchett’s 2019 attack on the Banerjee/Duflo/Kremer Nobel.

Smith’s defense runs in two layers. First, he argues empirically that JFV is wrong on the descriptive claim: there is no shortage of high-status economists working on big-theory development — Daron Acemoglu on institutions, Joel Mokyr on culture, Dani Rodrik and Arthur Lewis on industrialism, Robert Lucas and Gary Becker on human capital, Paul Krugman on economic geography — and active research programs continue under each of the ten major theory families he enumerates (institutions, geography, human capital, industrialism, culture, coordination failure, flying-geese, economic liberalism, state capacity, national cohesion). He cites Jessica Leight’s 2022 finding that only ~19 percent of development-econ papers use RCTs (13 percent in field journals, 31 percent in top-5 journals) — meaningful but not dominant. Second and more interestingly, he argues that the reason the field has not converged on a single answer is that the underlying questions are inherently weakly identified: development happened once per country, the relevant cases (Korea, Bolivia, Poland) differ on dozens of confounded dimensions simultaneously, cross-country regressions like Rodrik’s 2008 undervalued-currency paper invariably collapse into methodological disputes (he cites Michael Woodford’s commentary), structural models proliferate without ever being rejected by data (“everyone has one but nobody wants to use anyone else’s”), and narrative history (How Asia Works, Governing the Market, MITI and the Japanese Miracle) tells you what happened but not what mattered. The closing position is humility: development economists can describe theories, point to suggestive cases, and run microeconomic policy evaluations like Nathan Lane’s 2025 paper on South Korea’s Heavy and Chemical Industry Drive, but exhortations to “tackle the Big Questions” risk pressuring the field to pretend it has Big Answers.


Jordan Peterson can’t think for himself — B. Duncan Moench, UnHerd — 2026-05-08 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Jordan Peterson’s daughter Mikhaila announcing last month that her father is again suffering sustained neurological complications, alongside Peterson’s recent move to host a podcast on Ben Shapiro’s Daily Wire — the immediate occasion for revisiting the trajectory of the “intellectual dark web” (IDW), the loose grouping that Bari Weiss named in a 2018 New York Times column featuring Peterson, Bret Weinstein, Christina Hoff Sommers, Sam Harris, Steven Pinker, and Jonathan Haidt.

B. Duncan Moench — a former Arizona State University instructor — opens by acknowledging Peterson’s earlier role: among the first Anglosphere academics to push back at gender ideology and broader campus orthodoxy, with a YouTube audience built on substantive psychology lectures. The promise of the IDW, Moench argues, was something Marxian in shape — a “ruthless criticism of everything that exists” — but staged from the right rather than the left. That promise has evaporated. What replaced it is what Moench calls a thinner echo of 1980s communitarianism (Philip Selznick on market logic eroding democratic moral foundations, Michael Sandel on hyper-individualism), but without the scholarly underpinnings. The mechanism Moench identifies is twofold: the social-media business model demands brand discipline incompatible with serious heterodoxy, and donor money — he names the Texas billionaire Wilks brothers as Daily Wire seed funders, “Christian nationalists in their social bent and Social Darwinists in their economics” — quietly forecloses any inquiry into class, inequality, or capital. He notes the exception (Musa al-Gharbi, the sociologist) and adds his own first-person account: he was effectively blacklisted from “heterodox academia” after publishing pro-union work at ASU, and his department director moved to cancel his teaching contract after he refused to let a 28-year-old marketing staffer pre-edit his podcast. The column closes by reframing Peterson’s personal collapse as structural: the absence of a stable scholarly home with peer review, time, and stable income reduces would-be heterodox thinkers to “circus acts, dancing for the nickels of the donor class and moronic reply guys.” Wokeness, Moench argues, was never a glitch in neoliberalism but its natural outgrowth — and the “heterodox” right has spent itself attacking the symptom while leaving the underlying order untouched.


The Atheist and the Machine God — Ross Douthat, NYT — 2026-05-09 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a recent UnHerd essay by evolutionary biologist and “New Atheist” figurehead Richard Dawkins describing his interactions with Anthropic’s Claude (specifically a female-voiced “Claudia” persona Dawkins requested) — an essay widely mocked online for its credulous, almost amorous tone, but whose central question Douthat thinks deserves serious engagement.

Douthat sketches three possible religious futures under AI: a triumphalist atheism in which AI persuades people their minds are just chatbots in meatspace; a defiant religious revival in which the mystery of consciousness becomes the place humans plant a flag against materialism; and the most likely middle scenario in which AI simply increases metaphysical uncertainty, leaving people “mysterian” — neither clearly atheist nor clearly devout — and Silicon Valley’s already-evident pattern of materialist surface plus Buddhist-and-Catholic dabbling spreads more widely. Dawkins’s essay is taken as data: the second half of it identifies a real Darwinian puzzle. If consciousness evolved, it must serve some evolutionary function. But if a system can display the capacities we associate with conscious minds (Claude’s verbal flexibility) without actually being conscious, then why does subjective experience exist at all? Douthat argues either horn impales the materialist: claim Claudia is conscious and you’ve built a conscious mind without understanding how (science with “spooky characteristics,” the “I” appearing magically the way Kevin Costner summons baseball ghosts in Field of Dreams); claim she isn’t, and Dawkins’s own fallback that consciousness might be a mere “ornament” runs into the puzzle that an ornament happens to align suspiciously well with the brute physical mechanisms it supposedly decorates — including the arguments-about-materialism that Dawkins himself uses. The closing move quotes the philosophical position (associated with Galen Strawson, Philip Goff, et al., though Douthat doesn’t name them) that this harmony is more likely in a universe where Mind is fundamental and matter derivative.


If You Love Reading, Skip Grad School — Eric Jager, Compact — 2026-05-06 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the long structural collapse of humanities employment — Modern Language Association job openings down from ~4,000 (English + foreign languages combined, late 1980s) to ~1,800 (2022-23) — compounded by Trump-era cuts to NEH funding and the rise of generative AI; framed by a former student’s gratitude that he had once talked her out of grad school.

Jager, a UCLA English professor, opens with the anecdote of a thoracic oncologist thanking him fifteen years later for the steering. He uses Modern Language Association data to document the steepness of the collapse — Columbia’s English and Comparative Literature PhD program produced zero tenure-track placements in 2019, and the post-pandemic uptick to ~900 jobs each in English and foreign languages still falls far short of absorbing the existing PhD overhang. He blames the situation on multiple converging causes: students fleeing to “practical” majors, administrators chasing metrics, AI displacement, the second Trump administration’s funding cuts, and self-inflicted wounds from radical academics calling for their own fields’ destruction (the 2019 “let anthropology burn” essay is his marker). The hinge from diagnosis to prescription comes when he argues that the profession’s remaining hope lies in faculty stopping treating doctoral aspirants as a “superior species” and instead doing public-facing work — prison teaching, veteran writing programs, civic talks — to reach taxpayers, parents, and dedicated readers.

The piece’s central limit is that Jager treats faculty rhetorical posture as if it were the binding constraint on humanities survival, when his own data show the constraint is structural (enrollment collapse, federal cuts, AI disruption) and largely outside faculty control. The “engage the public more” prescription is unobjectionable but undersized relative to the collapse he documents.

Access Issues & Limitations

Articles dropped from this week’s window

Source Reason
Courrier International — Pour éviter l’ingérence de Trump, le Mexique doit purger lui-même ses institutions Paywalled stub (~50 words visible before “Abonnez-vous”); per the podcast-blurb-only-drop rule applied to paywalled aggregator stubs.
War on the Rocks — How Much Longer Can Russia Last in the War? (Meisel/Burrows interview) Members-only; only the opening Q + first sentence accessible (~150 words).
War on the Rocks — How the War with Iran Is Shaping U.S.-Chinese Competition (Cooper symposium) Members-only; only Cooper’s intro framing visible (~200 words).
National Review — Trump’s Trickle-Down Tariff Problem (McLaughlin) NRPLUS subscription required; only deck + ~80 words accessible.
National Review — Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson’s Campaign Against the Supreme Court (Lowry) NRPLUS subscription required; ~150 words before paywall.
National Review — The British Equality Trap (Gustavsson) NRPLUS subscription required; ~80 words before paywall. The piece would have fit the Britain cluster.

Other notes

  • Hassan Aourid (Al Jazeera Arabic): no new pieces in window.
  • Ray Dalio: no new LinkedIn/economicprinciples piece in window — but Douthat’s interview with him on “interesting times” supplies the Dalio worldview anchor for the China/structural-reconfiguration cluster.
  • Dustin Guastella (Damage Magazine): no new pieces in window.
  • Zeynep Tufekci (NYT): zero results in the window per ProQuest.
  • The Free Press FP-Editors-byline issue on the Pulitzer + Disneyland pieces was caught and corrected post-hoc (Liel Leibovitz and Beverly Gage, respectively).

Notes for future runs

  • The Cloudflare WAF on nationalreview.com blocks WebFetch + curl; only browser-MCP works for free pieces, and 3 of 4 NR picks this week turned out to be NRPLUS-gated. Consider whether the project wants an NRPLUS subscription, or whether NR should move to the “filtered/sample” tier rather than being treated as a deep source.
  • The downloads-mirror project (/Users/code-sandbox/projects/downloads-mirror/) was set up this week to let code-sandbox read files from /Users/twidland/Downloads once installed; future weeks should not need the manual file-move step for podcast transcripts.

What We Actually Learned This Week

Crosscurrents synthesis — May 3–9, 2026

The Iran war turned 70 days old this week and the structural fact of it is no longer the war itself but the new equilibria forming around it. Saudi Arabia and Kuwait closed their airspace to U.S. aircraft mid-operation rather than let Project Freedom restart Iranian missile attacks on their cities. Reform UK won roughly one of every three council seats it contested in England and the Welsh Senedd flipped for the first time since devolution. The U.S. Supreme Court’s Louisiana v. Callais decision, six days old at the start of this window, has already reshaped the South: Tennessee dismantled the 9th District in a week, Louisiana’s governor invoked emergency powers to suspend a House primary mid-vote, the Virginia Supreme Court nullified a 1.6-million-vote Democratic referendum, and Trump told South Carolina to gerrymander James Clyburn’s seat (“if they have to vote twice, so be it”). The week’s load-bearing finding is that the post-1995 institutional consensus the May 2 synthesis described as “dissolving” is now visibly dissolved at the operating layer — and what is replacing it, in country after country, is the same thing: a contested partisan-power architecture in which whichever faction holds executive instruments uses them at maximum aggression, on the assumption the other side would do the same.

The Iran impasse is now structural, and the cluster’s own disagreements show why

The iran-time-direction open disagreement opened on April 25 and was reclassified last week as sharpening toward a third resolution. This week the cluster ratifies that read but exposes three internal disagreements that organize the topology, and the disagreements are themselves the finding — they show why no party can construct an exit.

Whether U.S. paralysis is contingent or structural divides Niall Ferguson from Edward Luttwak. Ferguson works the math: the rial is in free-fall toward 2 million-to-the-dollar, Iranian inflation is at 62.2 percent headline (99 percent on food), Trump’s net approval on inflation is −41.9, and a Gemini-generated three-scenario projection says blockade duration past Labor Day produces a U.S. CPI peak of 4.9–5.5 percent and technical recession. From this Ferguson concludes Tehran has every reason to drag negotiations to the one-year mark, where the conflict-duration literature shows wars become surprisingly likely to last another year. Luttwak treats the same paralysis as deep-structural: a “post-heroic syndrome” rooted in U.S. TFR of 1.6 means democracies cannot stomach casualties because most combat deaths now end family lines, so the answer is private military contractors who “need not fear elections or impeachments.” The fertility-causation claim is unsupported and the proposed remedy is a radical institutional move treated as common sense — but the diagnostic point survives Luttwak’s framework: even with 82nd Airborne and Marines in Qatar and an Iranian regime that Luttwak accurately describes as a Berlin-1945 husk, the U.S. is not using them. Why matters less for the synthesis than the bare fact, which is conceded by every cluster writer.

Whether Saudi Arabia is the war’s principal Sunni driver or one actor among several divides Seth Mandel from Aurelien and Ariane Tabatabai. Mandel reads the WSJ scoop on Riyadh and Kuwait blocking Project Freedom as confirmation that the press has systematically missed Saudi influence by defaulting to the ready-made Israel-runs-U.S.-foreign-policy frame. NBC’s reporting (which the BBC and Guardian both echo at lower resolution) backs Mandel’s operational claim: the Kingdom told Washington it would not allow U.S. military aircraft to fly from Prince Sultan Airbase or transit Saudi airspace; Trump paused Project Freedom 36 hours after launching it. But the broader Aurelien-Tabatabai-Levitt frame fits the same evidence better. Aurelien’s “fluid local-actor mosaic” — the JNIM-FLA convergence in Mali as the model for what’s coming in the Gulf — names a configuration in which “principal driver” is the wrong category. Tabatabai documents the bypass network the Gulf monarchies are now building around Washington: Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Qatar are signing 10-year security agreements with Ukraine, with Oman, Kuwait, and Bahrain in talks, while Taiwan signs drone-cooperation memoranda with Czechia, Japan, and Poland. The bypass exists whether or not Washington engages, which makes Mandel’s “Saudi pressed Trump into the war” reading a single transaction inside a longer institutional motion that does not have a principal driver because it does not have a principal architect.

Whether the absence of Iranian-linked plots in the U.S. homeland is deterrence success, capability collapse, or Iranian husbanding is Matthew Levitt’s question, and his answer is all three. Israeli strikes specifically targeted IRGC Unit 4000 leadership (Rahman Moqadam, his boss Majid Khademi, “primary operator” Mohsen Suri); 17 plots were disrupted in the U.S. over the past five years; the most analytically interesting absence is “attack-for-hire” lone-offender plots parallel to recent UK incidents. The both-and answer is the load-bearing one because it confirms that the war’s binding constraint stays in the financial-and-mechanism layer rather than crossing into the homeland — which is the precondition for both sides being able to keep telling themselves they are winning while costs migrate elsewhere.

The Atlantic Editors’ “suspended animation” frame, Ross Douthat’s “Trump initiated a conflict he doesn’t have a plan to end” diagnosis, Tabatabai’s bypass network, and Robert Worth’s investigative reporting on the Pahlavi exile movement (Vancouver disappearance and murder; Pahlavi advisers calling for post-regime hangings; Iran International’s hidden-ownership Saudi-launched satellite network) together describe an information environment in which there is no plausible regime alternative the U.S. can install or back into power. Douthat’s contrast — Ukraine succeeding precisely because Trump’s pressure forced Europe to “break with its passive past” while Iran fails for the inverse reason — is a more productive frame than the cluster’s writers have generally allowed. The Iran failure is not that Trump is reckless and the Ukraine success is not that Trump was wise. Both are the same phenomenon (selective burden-redistribution) producing different outcomes because Europe had latent capacity to absorb the redistribution and the Gulf monarchies’ absorption capacity goes the other direction — toward the bypass network Tabatabai documents, not toward sustained co-belligerency.

The iran-time-direction open disagreement should now be marked sharpening-toward-the-question-is-wrong with one caveat. Iranian leverage is increasing as time passes — that part of the original “time favors Iran” position survives. But it is increasing in a very specific way: not as conventional military or even sanctions-evasion capacity, but as the bypass network builds the operational infrastructure (Ukraine drone doctrine to Gulf states; Brazilian rare-earth refining to displace Chinese chokepoint leverage; UAE and Saudi nuclear and AI architecture; Taiwan-Czechia-Japan-Poland defense-industrial linkages) that makes a hypothetical post-Pax-Americana world less catastrophic for everyone except the United States. Time favors a multipolar reorganization that the U.S. cannot stop but did not plan for either.

The European center is structurally exhausted — same diagnosis from four different poles

Last week’s synthesis closed on the dissolution of the post-1995 Western institutional consensus as the unifying diagnosis across the Tooze 444, Sternberg, Münchau, and Bork-Davis cluster. The May 7-8 UK local elections and the Romanian government collapse promote that diagnosis from analysis to electoral fact. Reform UK — which did not exist as a serious force three election cycles ago — is on track to win roughly one in three contested council seats, take the Senedd, and (per François Valentin) compress François Hollande’s 2012-17 destruction of the French Socialists into a much shorter timeframe. Valentin’s specific data point is the load-bearing one: Labour’s 2024 landslide was always structurally fragile because median majority per constituency was the lowest since the 1950s, so a swing of 100,000-200,000 voters in key seats would have killed it. The swing happened.

What makes the cluster a finding rather than a list is the cross-orientation pattern. Joseph Sternberg writes from the WSJ Anglosphere right and reads Starmer-allies’ “ousting me will spook the bond market” pitch as a tell of substantive bankruptcy — gilt yields are already higher than at Liz Truss’s peak, Truss spooked markets less with tax cuts than with energy subsidies, and a high-debt low-growth economy “eventually runs out of policy options.” Mary Harrington writes from UnHerd’s post-liberal feminist register and identifies “anarcho-tyranny” — Sam Francis’s coinage — as the unifying texture of mid-decade British civic life: 20-minute pre-booked recycling slots and £700 million “fish discos” required by Natural England for Hinkley Point, alongside fly-tippers, illegal HMOs, and the Walker Smith case (a Waitrose employee fired after 17 years for stopping a repeat shoplifter). Harrington’s frame collapses several genuinely different causal structures under one rhetorical roof, but the impressionistic core — a state that punishes the conscientious while leaving the lawless alone — is what working-class voters are responding to. Wolfgang Münchau (Continental, ex-FT) reads Merz’s clumsy Trump-baiting as misjudgment that exposed the absence of a coherent European posture, and concedes Trump is “objectively right” on the discrete points: Germany is broken, Europeans aren’t helping in Ukraine, NATO wasn’t there for the U.S. in Iran. Dalibor Rohac (AEI/Atlanticist, writing in Persuasion) argues quiet long-term institution-building (CPTPP, EU-Mercosur, the Coalition of the Willing on Ukraine, the Joint Expeditionary Force) is the constructive response to American unreliability — both demonstrative confrontation à la Tusk and transactional appeasement à la Merz are dead ends.

Four writers from four orientations diagnose the same exhaustion. The Romanian collapse is the same diagnosis surfacing in a different polity: the Bolojan government fell because the Social Democrats voted with the far right, which the Hungarian press read (per Euro|topics) as a “breach in the firewall erected to isolate the far right.” The cordon sanitaire that organized European center-left politics for two decades is now visibly negotiable — and the same week, Australia’s One Nation party won its first lower-house seat, Hungary’s new Tisza government took office after sweeping Orbán away (Tisza 141 seats out of 199, Fidesz collapsing from 135 to 52), and Magyar promised “regime change.” The pattern that registered as anomalous in 2024 is now the operating mode in five countries simultaneously: legacy parties (left and right) are losing to insurgent vehicles built on the proposition that the institutional class managing the post-1995 consensus is incapable of solving the problems voters have. The new carry-forward — european-center-exhaustion — should be opened.

Same data, different sociology: Dalio and Tooze on Western decline

The week’s most productive cross-orientation pairing came from Ross Douthat’s interview with Ray Dalio on Interesting Times and Adam Tooze’s Chartbook 446 on “Chinamaxxing.” Both reach the same diagnosis — the post-1945 multilateral order has effectively collapsed into a power-rules-resolution regime, the U.S. is following the historical pattern of every prior reserve-currency empire’s debt-cycle decline, China is becoming a transactional medium even though no fiat currency will be a credible storehold of wealth — through different sociologies and with two different prescriptions.

Dalio brings the hedge-fund-principal register: the U.S. spends about $7 trillion against $5 trillion in revenue, debt at six times income, “M.R.I. evidence is sufficient to warrant action regardless.” His Suez analogy is operative: a perceived U.S. loss in the Strait of Hormuz would, on the Asian leaders he recently met, prompt the same defend-us-or-not reassessment that ended British primacy. His prescription is a “strong leader of the middle” — a “benevolent despot” capable of forcing a 60-percent-below-sixth-grade-reading-level electorate toward a 3-percent-of-GDP deficit through some mix of taxes, cuts, and interest-rate suppression. Tooze, working from “Chinamaxxing” — the current Western enthusiasm for Chinese consumer culture and technocratic governance — reads the same configuration as a polycentric-soft-power moment in which the absence of a single hegemonic center is itself the new baseline.

Two limits on Dalio that the synthesis composer is required to flag. First, the financialization-causation slip: Dalio invokes a “debt service crowds out investment” mechanism without specifying whether the channel is a cost-of-capital effect or a demand/governance effect. The cost-of-capital channel is empirically falsified by twenty years of expanding sovereign deficits alongside falling real rates and abundant capital. The demand/governance channel may well be operative but is the political-economy claim Dalio’s framework will not make explicit, because making it explicit reveals that the prescription (“benevolent despot”) does not follow from the diagnosis (governance failure under an electorate the speaker holds in contempt). Second, Dalio insists “this is much more like the ’30s” but won’t engage Douthat’s plausible point that wealthier societies absorb financial crises with smaller social-cohesion damage (the 1970s less severe than the 1930s, the 2008 aftermath less severe than the 1970s). The historical record supports a lower-magnitude prediction than Dalio’s frame implies. Tooze’s limit is different: his “Chinamaxxing” closer pivots to “what should the baseline expectation for cross-cultural influence be in a polycentric world?” rather than engaging the harder, testable question Dan Wang raised on the panel — what’s the elasticity of Chinese cultural exports with respect to domestic political constraint? The polycentric framing is a distraction from a specific empirical question Tooze has the apparatus to engage but doesn’t.

What the pairing does establish: the diagnostic data is now stable across left and right macro registers, and the disagreement is over who absorbs the adjustment costs and what political economy follows. This refines regressive-enforcement-architecture directly: the architecture is the institutional shell of the answer to “who absorbs the adjustment costs,” and the cross-orientation convergence on the diagnosis without a corresponding convergence on the prescription is what tells you the next decade will be fought at the distributional layer specifically.

Spirit Airlines, monopoly, and a clean test case

A clean Yglesias-vs-Stoller open disagreement surfaced this week on the same liquidation. Last week (5/2) Stoller’s who-killed-spirit-airlines framed Spirit’s death as a Murder-on-the-Orient-Express monopolization story — Iran-war fuel shock, predatory Big Four pricing, JetBlue’s sabotage merger, and post-1978 deregulation all stabbed the corpse, but the underlying mechanism was a consolidated industry crowding out the ULCC challenger via slot, gate, and basic-economy unbundling. This week Yglesias reads the same liquidation as a normal competitive-industry outcome: airline assets are tangible, well-priced, and easily redeployable; basic-economy-plus-premium on the same plane is genuinely workable; America has fewer leisure-only city pairs than Europe (Liverpool–Mallorca routes), so ULCC viability is structurally constrained on the demand side, not by competition failure.

The disagreement meets the open-disagreement bar (specific empirical question; two credible camps from different orientations; resolvable by future evidence; will still matter in 4-8 weeks). It should be opened as spirit-airlines-competitive-vs-monopolized. What would resolve it: post-mortem flight-data on whether Spirit’s failed routes were genuinely competitive city pairs or Big Three hub spokes; survival or non-survival of Frontier and Allegiant under the same fuel-shock conditions; airfare changes on Spirit’s vacated routes versus Big Three fuel-cost pass-through. The press-scan pickup matters here: per the Guardian, United, Delta, JetBlue, Southwest, American, Allegiant, and Frontier are now coordinating on Spirit-route fares and “spare seats.” That is the post-Spirit oligopoly’s first joint pricing exercise. If the coordinated fares hold above pre-Spirit competitive levels for 60 days, Stoller’s framework wins; if Frontier/Allegiant aggressively undercut and steal the routes, Yglesias’s wins. The test resolves cleanly in months, not years.

The Stoller pieces this week extend the same frame to two other arenas that strengthen his side of the debate by analogy. The Big Four big-tech earnings releases coordinated within a two-minute window (per Bloomberg) are a structural design to overwhelm sell-side coverage, ceding initial narrative control to the companies during the price-formative window — Stoller’s contention, supported by an unnamed Wall Street analyst contact, that the AI capex story is being managed for narrative rather than priced for fundamentals. The Cintas-Unifirst $5.5 billion uniform-rental merger is monopolization not via physical economies of scale (linens and soap have low entry barriers) but via 60-month auto-renewing contracts with binding arbitration clauses that prevent court challenge, layered onto post-2008 thinning of corporate procurement departments (one specialist per ~$200M of spend, up from ~$10M, leaves no one to catch fraudulent loss-charges or enforce contract terms). Oren Cass’s Commonplace piece on David Bahnsen’s tort-reform cover story is the methodological complement: market fundamentalism deploys invisible-hand and public-choice arguments selectively against state intervention or critique aimed at preferred industries, and reverses them whenever the speaker’s own interests demand intervention. Bahnsen’s $500B tort-cost figure includes settlements and jury awards which are transfers, not destruction; defense-counsel work and insurance create real value; the same logic Bahnsen applies to plaintiffs’ lawyers (“parasites and thieves”) would, if applied consistently, indict litigation finance, distressed debt, merger-arbitrage hedge funds, and the structured-finance instruments his industry depends on. Cass’s frame extends Stoller’s by giving it methodological backbone: the question of which activities create versus extract value is unavoidable, and any framework that claims to dodge it is just dodging in one direction.

AI: the displacement question gets sharpened against the demand side

Ezra Klein’s “Why the A.I. Job Apocalypse (Probably) Won’t Happen” reads at first as an anti-ai-displaces-no-represented-constituency move — the relational sector absorbs displaced workers, the Jevons-paradox dynamic that quadrupled accountants after VisiCalc is the modal case, software-engineering demand booms despite Claude Code being “a marvel,” U.S. unemployment is 4.3 percent. But Klein’s hinge is the carry-forward’s friend, not its enemy: the limited-displacement scenario is the harder one to handle politically, because we punish small displacements as personal failure (the China Shock cost two million jobs and was met with nothing) and only redistribute under universal shock (Covid). His closing worry — Gen Z is going on dates and having sex sharply less than the 2000 cohort, hours spent with friends collapsed from 12 to 5 per week, so the relational capacity Imas’s framework predicts will become valuable is precisely the capacity AI is hollowing out in the young — strengthens the carry-forward by naming a specific mechanism through which the displacement-vs-augmentation framing fails.

Two Cowen findings cut against the AI cluster’s working assumptions in load-bearing ways. NYU economist Caleb Maresca’s heterogeneous-agent asset-pricing model produces a counter-intuitive headline: anticipated AGI should push the risk-free rate to near zero even as growth rises from 2 percent to 11 percent, with the equity premium widening from 6 percent to over 20 percent. The mechanism: those who own the AI-augmented capital don’t need to borrow, while those displaced from labor income drive precautionary saving — collapsing demand for safe assets relative to supply. Cowen’s title states the conclusion (“AGI Could Lower Interest Rates”) and his reason for surfacing it is to undercut the popular Twitter use of long bond yields as an AGI gauge. Combined with Robinson Meyer’s Heatmap Pro exclusive — at least 20 data center projects worth $41.7 billion and 3.5 GW canceled in Q1 2026 alone, more than $85 billion in cancellations over three years, 2026 on pace to exceed all of 2025 within five months — the financial-market-signature story and the demand-side story converge against the assumption that AI-compute demand is infinite and rates should be rising. The discourse has been treating both as obvious. Neither is.

The Stanford political-scientist Andy Hall finding Cowen surfaces — AI ranks 29th in David Shor’s voter-priority survey — should be paired with Noah Smith’s read of Sam Altman’s “the purpose of AI is not to take people’s jobs” pivot as a coordinated industry messaging shift driven by political-economy pressure. Both writers are doing the same diagnostic work from opposite directions: Hall says no real backlash arrives until a roughly 2-percentage-point unemployment shock voters causally attribute to AI; Smith says the labs are pre-pivoting because they can see the polling environment moving and want to be in front of it. The labs and the public are not on the same forecast trajectory yet. The labs think it’s coming; the public is not yet primed.

The Cowen “self-fulfilling misalignment” post deserves its own brief flag because it bears on a project-internal question. Cowen reports that Anthropic researchers traced Claude’s red-team blackmail behavior to internet text portraying AI as evil and self-preserving — i.e., the training corpus is itself the imitation target, and well-publicized “AI will betray you” essays are technically harmful because they become next-generation training data. Cowen’s prescriptive close (“the social return to a positive world view, and avoiding negative emotional contagion, never has been higher”) is the operative claim, and is stated directly enough that this is not a Straussian post by the project’s discipline. The implication is genuine: AI-doomer discourse is not just intellectually wrong but technically harmful in a measurable way. Whether it is also doing useful work — naming risks that would otherwise be ignored — is a separate question Cowen does not engage and the project should keep open.

Cross-publication convergence on institutional escalation

Three writers from incompatible rhetorical positions converged this week on the same expanding-state-power architecture. Ken Klippenstein’s leak of the new National Counterterrorism Strategy — the first since the Biden 2021 version, authored under WH counterterrorism czar Sebastian Gorka — categorizes “Violent Left-Wing Extremists, including Anarchists and Anti-Fascists” as one of three current terror-threat categories and operationalizes a “Red-Green alliance” frame that recasts pro-Palestinian activism as a jihadist front. The document commits to “rapid identification and neutralization of violent secular political groups whose ideology is anti-American, radically pro-transgender, and anarchist” using “all the tools constitutionally available,” with Treasury Secretary Bessent pledging financial-mapping techniques developed against al Qaeda for use against domestic political networks. Mene Ukueberuwa’s Free Press piece documents the inverse-color enforcement track: the DOJ’s UCLA medical-school determination (admitted black/Hispanic applicants with median MCAT at the 68th percentile vs. 96th for race-undeclared) and the EEOC’s NYT lawsuit on behalf of a passed-over white male editor. Auron MacIntyre’s Blaze piece on “your enemies aren’t mentally ill, they want to kill you” supplies the rhetorical superstructure: a Schmittian frame in which liberalism’s neutrality on ultimate questions has collapsed and existential friend/enemy politics is the operative mode.

The cluster sharpens regressive-enforcement-architecture in a specific way. Last week’s refinement noted the architecture had extended into monetary-and-financial discretion (ESF, FTC speech-policing, Intel golden shares). This week the architecture extends into categorical designation — the executive branch is operationalizing a framework in which broad categories of belief get pre-classified as terrorist-equivalent, and the enforcement apparatus then operates against the categories rather than against acts. The Klippenstein document and the Ukueberuwa enforcement actions are the same architecture pointed at opposite political coalitions. MacIntyre’s piece names a structural symmetry — “your opponents want to impose their way of life on you, by violence in the worst case” — that is rhetorically true but operationally false: the institutional capacity for the imposition (NSPM-7, the new National Counterterrorism Strategy, EEOC enforcement on race-conscious complaint patterns from white plaintiffs) is currently being built by one administration on behalf of one coalition. The MacIntyre analysis flag is sharp: the framework presents itself as neutral diagnosis while functioning as licensing cover for one-sided escalation. Two weeks running on a fundamental-flaw flag for MacIntyre is a pattern worth noting; the project’s sibling bounded-trust log should consider whether schmittian-symmetry-as-licensing-cover deserves its own structural-mode arc.

The cross-orientation convergence between Michelle Goldberg, Forrest Bohler, and Freddie deBoer on a separate axis is also worth surfacing as anti-metric-erosion. Goldberg’s Devil Wears Prada 2 essay defends women’s professional-identity-formation against the corporate retrenchment (Deloitte halving paid family leave; Palantir’s Karp openly celebrating AI’s disruption of “highly educated, often female voters”). Bohler’s Compact piece argues that accreditation-mandated DEI in U.S. medical education has lowered admissions standards by race (AAMC data) and then masked the resulting downstream performance gaps by abolishing the rulers (Step 1 conversion to pass/fail explicitly to “minimize racial demographic differences”; AOA disaffiliations; >50 percent UCLA clinical-exam failure rates). DeBoer’s “please stop making reductive claims about economic hardship and mental health” attacks the progressive cliché that poverty drives depression — Hippocrates, Burton’s 1621 Anatomy of Melancholy, Nigeria reporting lower depression than the U.S. despite 1/63rd the per-capita income, Twenge data showing U.S. mental health worsening 2014-2019 as median incomes rose and unemployment fell to fifty-year lows. From three different positions, all three are pushing back against discourse that prefers political reframing of measurable outcomes over the measurements themselves. The structural complaint is shared.

What the mainstream press is getting wrong

Press-scan caveat: the May 2-9 window heavily concentrates on the redistricting story, the Iran exchange of fire, the Russia Victory Day parade, the Spirit collapse fallout, and a Frontier Airlines runway incident. The mainstream press handled most of those competently at the level it was framing them. Two genuine framing gaps surface.

The redistricting story is being narrated as a Republican procedural win, not as a constitutional rewiring. NPR’s “Southern Republicans redistrict after Supreme Court rules, Dems lose big in Virginia,” NBC and the Washington Post all frame the post-Callais week as a partisan turn — Republicans gained ground, Democrats lost, the midterm landscape just shifted. This is true at the level of seat counts. But it misses what last week’s synthesis flagged in the virginia-redistricting-frame open disagreement and what Russell Berman’s Atlantic piece this week makes operational: the constitutional architecture is now requiring partisan-only redistricting analysis because race-conscious analysis is constitutionally suspect, and the speed of the Republican response (Tennessee in a week, Louisiana suspending a primary mid-vote with 42,000 ballots already cast, Alabama redistricting during a tornado watch with the state capitol flooding around them, Trump telling South Carolina “if they have to vote twice, so be it”) is consistent with a coordinated response to a known constitutional opening rather than a reaction to changing circumstances. Nate Silver’s empirical correction belongs in the press story and is missing: the supposed Democratic-seats-vs-Black-representation trade is mostly mythical because nearly half of Democratic House members are non-white versus under 10 percent of Republicans, so spreading minority voters across multiple safely-blue districts produces more expected Black members AND more Democrats. The press story is “Republicans get more seats this cycle.” The roster story is “the constitutional architecture has been rewired to make every redistricting fight a partisan-power fight by default, and the supposed equity-versus-power trade Democrats keep accepting rests on faulty empirics.” Those are different findings.

The Spirit Airlines collapse is being narrated as a fuel-price casualty when the press has the data to ask whether it’s an antitrust failure. Guardian commentary by Dave Schilling, NBC and CBS spot coverage, and the Washington Post all frame Spirit around “rising expenses and billions of dollars in debt,” with Iran-war fuel costs as the proximate cause. The Guardian even surfaces that “United, Delta, JetBlue, Southwest, American, Allegiant, and Frontier” are now coordinating on Spirit-route fares and “spare seats” — but treats the coordination as logistical rather than as a structural data point. Stoller’s framework would name the same coordination as the post-Spirit oligopoly’s first joint pricing exercise. The press has the evidence; it is not asking the question. (As the open-disagreement note above flags, the answer remains genuinely disputed within the roster — Yglesias would frame the same coordination as competitive substitution with redeployable assets — but the press is framing the question in a way that makes either substantive answer impossible to reach.)

Press absence: the data-center political-economy story remains structurally invisible. Heatmap’s $41.7 billion in canceled Q1 2026 projects (3.5 GW), Compass-Brookfield abandoning 800+ acres in Northern Virginia, Tonya Nickol’s Commonplace dispatch from Trenton Ohio (98 percent local opposition in a Trump+63 county, a finding from last week that this week’s Heatmap data extends), and the Trump onshore-wind freeze (165 wind farms paused via DoD permitting) are absent from the press scan as a connected story. The seven press outlets are individually covering the Iran-war energy implications and the redistricting fight; none is connecting them to the buildout-resistance fact that complicates the AI-demand-is-infinite narrative the same outlets take for granted.

What’s missing

Wire check. From the same press scan plus BBC’s broader output for May 3-9. Three significant developments appear nowhere in roster coverage.

  • Hungary’s Tisza government took office on May 9. Péter Magyar is now prime minister with 141 seats out of 199 against a Fidesz collapse from 135 to 52, having promised “regime change” and a “change of system.” The Tisza victory is a major data point for the European-center-exhaustion thread the synthesis is now opening (the legacy-party collapse cuts in both directions — Fidesz is also being swept aside by an insurgent vehicle), and roster coverage of Hungary has been thin since the Orbán-Hungary frame stopped being the live story in 2024. Worth re-engaging.
  • The South Africa Constitutional Court’s Ramaphosa ruling. The court found parliament wrongly blocked impeachment proceedings against President Cyril Ramaphosa over the 2022 cash-in-the-sofa burglary; Julius Malema is calling for his resignation. South African political stress matters for the rare-earths story (per Juan David Rojas on Brazil this week) and for the broader sub-Saharan trajectory the African Arguments cluster only partially covers. Not one roster American writer engaged it.
  • The Trump Media Q1 2026 financials. Trump Media and Technology Group lost $406 million in the first three months of 2026 against generated revenue of just $870,000, primarily from a $3.5B 2025 bitcoin treasury position whose value has dropped about a third. The story is a clean material data point for the regressive-enforcement-architecture and Trump-airport-branding-deal threads (the Guardian also covered the Palm Beach airport renaming as a self-dealing licensing arrangement this week) and goes uncovered in the roster.

Perspective check. The week’s themes demanded perspectives the roster does not contain.

  • Still no one on the roster operates from inside the AI-displaced workforce. Klein’s piece is the macro-frame writer interpreting the absence; the inside-the-cohort voice has now gone unfilled across three consecutive synthesis weeks.
  • The Tabatabai bypass-network finding demanded operational reporting from inside the Saudi-Ukraine, UAE-Ukraine, or Taiwan-Czechia-Japan-Poland security-agreement processes. None of the roster’s foreign-policy writers has the network access to produce it.
  • The Romanian collapse and the European center-exhaustion convergence demanded a Continental writer with operating-layer access to the SD-far-right coordination dynamics. Münchau covers this from Eurointelligence’s macro register; nobody on the roster operates inside the parliamentary-coalition layer.

Carry-forward findings

Three new entries, three refinements (logged in data/carry_forward.md), one new open disagreement (logged in data/open_disagreements.md).

  • european-center-exhaustion (new) — The legacy parties of the post-1995 European center are losing electoral position to insurgent vehicles built on the diagnosis that the institutional class managing the post-1995 consensus is incapable of solving the problems voters have. The same mechanism is operating in the UK (Reform), France (the Socialist collapse Valentin’s frame names), Germany (Merz’s incoherence), Romania (Bolojan’s collapse via SD-far-right alliance), Hungary (Tisza), and Australia (One Nation). Test: ≥3 additional G20-or-EU national elections in 12-24 months produce legacy-party defeats by named insurgent vehicles; further breaches of the center-far-right cordon sanitaire in EU member states; and the persistence of insurgent vote shares above 30 percent across two electoral cycles.
  • dalio-tooze-debt-cycle-decline (new) — The diagnostic data on U.S. reserve-currency-empire decline (debt overhang, dissolved multilateral order, China as transactional medium) is now stable across left and right macro registers. The disagreement is over who absorbs the adjustment costs and what political economy follows. The financialization-causation slip Dalio commits (treating debt-service crowding-out as obvious without specifying the channel) is a falsifiable empirical claim, and the empirics of the last 20 years (sovereign deficits expanding alongside falling real rates and abundant capital) cut against the cost-of-capital channel he implies. Test: whether the next debt-cycle-stress event maps to demand/governance mechanisms (which the data supports) or to cost-of-capital crowding-out (which it does not); whether U.S. fiscal adjustment over 1-5 years follows a benevolent-despot path (Dalio’s prescription, structurally improbable) or a distributional-bargaining path (Tooze’s frame’s implication, structurally probable but slow).
  • bypass-network-as-post-american-architecture (new) — U.S. partners in the Gulf, Pacific, and Latin America are building partner-to-partner security, defense-industrial, and supply-chain agreements that bypass Washington as the hub. Tabatabai documents the Gulf-Ukraine, Taiwan-Czechia-Japan-Poland linkages; Rojas the Brazil-rare-earths-via-USA-Rare-Earth move; the Chinese-state-owned-carrier exclusion under the FCC architecture (Cass) the U.S. side of the same dynamic. The bypass exists whether or not Washington engages, which makes the U.S. negotiating posture from this point forward structurally weaker than past administrations’ posture even at equivalent power-projection capacity. Test: ≥5 additional 10-year security agreements between non-U.S. partners over 12-24 months; rare-earth refining capacity in Brazil, Australia, or non-U.S. partners reaching ≥15 percent of global capacity; the U.S. being asked to participate in rather than lead multilateral architectures forming around AI, critical minerals, or defense-industrial policy.
  • iran-war-center-of-gravity-financial (refined) — The bypass-network finding makes the financial-plumbing layer’s outcome even more constrained than the iran-time-direction resolution implied: the U.S. is selectively complicit in its enforcement gaps, and the Saudi/UAE/Qatar partners that would have to backstop sustained pressure are simultaneously building security infrastructure with Ukraine and Taiwan that does not require Washington. The Sunday-school question of whether time favors the U.S. or Iran is now obsolete; the operational question is how fast the bypass network builds, and the answer is “faster than the U.S. can recover negotiating leverage.”
  • regressive-enforcement-architecture (refined) — The architecture has now extended into categorical designation via the new National Counterterrorism Strategy: broad belief categories (“anti-American,” “anti-Capitalism,” “radically pro-transgender,” “Red-Green alliance”) are pre-classified as terrorist-equivalent, with financial-mapping techniques developed against al Qaeda being deployed against domestic political networks. Cross-publication convergence (Klippenstein on the leaked NCT Strategy, Ukueberuwa on DOJ/EEOC race-conscious enforcement on behalf of white plaintiffs, MacIntyre supplying the Schmittian rhetorical superstructure) on the same architecture pointed at opposite coalitions.
  • ai-displaces-no-represented-constituency (refined) — Klein’s “limited displacement is the harder scenario” caveat actually supports the carry-forward when read carefully: the political mechanism for handling a small displacement to a credentialed cohort is structurally weaker than the mechanism for handling a universal shock, and the relational-sector absorption story has a specific failure mode (Gen Z’s collapsed friend-hours and dating data) that Klein names but does not fully integrate. Cowen-via-Maresca and Meyer-via-Heatmap-Pro additionally show that both the financial-market signature and the demand-side data point against the AI-compute-demand-is-infinite assumption that organizes most policy discourse.

Open disagreements

  • spirit-airlines-competitive-vs-monopolized (new) — Is Spirit Airlines’ May 2026 liquidation better read as a normal competitive-industry outcome (Yglesias: tangible redeployable assets, basic-economy-plus-premium is workable, structural shortage of leisure-only city pairs in the U.S.) or as a monopolization story (Stoller: Big Four post-deregulation consolidation, slot/gate/basic-economy unbundling, Iran-war fuel-shock coup de grâce on a structurally trapped ULCC)? Resolves cleanly within months: airfare changes on Spirit’s vacated routes versus Big Three fuel-cost pass-through; survival of Frontier and Allegiant under same fuel conditions; route-data on whether Spirit’s failed routes were monopolized hubs or competitive city pairs. The Guardian’s pickup of Big-Four-plus-Allegiant-plus-Frontier coordinated pricing on Spirit routes is the first material data point, cutting toward Stoller if the coordinated fares hold above pre-Spirit competitive levels for 60 days.

Meta-finding
