To summarize, under the original map, we’d expect Susquehanna to elect 3 Democrats and 1.4 Black representatives. Under New Map A, those numbers become 5 Democrats and 1.2 Black members. Under New Map B, it’s 4.9 Democrats and 1.6 Black representatives (more than before).

There’s not really a trade-off to speak of. Democrats are taking advantage of the fact that 1) the propensity to elect Black members to Congress scales up fairly linearly with the share of Black voters, but 2) the effects of partisanship on Black representation are somewhat nonlinear. Even a slightly blue district will usually vote Democratic for Congress, and Democratic candidates are much more likely to be Black or belong to other minority groups.

I know Susquehanna is a hypothetical state, and it’s beyond my pay grade to speculate what sort of legal scrutiny these maps would face. But the math works. We aren’t adding or removing any voters from Susquehanna; we’re just sorting them differently, which is what any redistricting process does.

I don’t like misshapen, misrepresentative, noncompetitive districts any more than the rest of you do, but they’re the inevitable, prisoner’s dilemma-type consequence of having essentially no enforceable constraints on redistricting in federal law. And as I’ve stressed repeatedly, Democrats are not at any sort of intrinsic disadvantage in an unregulated redistricting process; they actually control trifectas in states representing more seats in the U.S. House than Republicans, and might pick up another couple of trifectas in November. They just have to be willing to aggressively abide by the rules of the game, which involve fewer tradeoffs between Democratic representation and minority representation than you might assume.

1

So excluding nonvoting delegates like Eleanor Holmes Norton.

2

Using logistic regression.

3

The probability of electing a Democrat is also modeled using a logistic regression based on Harris vote margin in 2024. So yes, we’re embedding a logistic regression within another logistic regression. 🤯

4

Though I left the raw Harris vote margin in the model anyway in addition to the S-Curve. Usually I’m in favor of retaining parameters in a model if they’re directionally and logically correct, even if they don’t achieve proper significance.

Claude’s Response

The model is a useful corrective to a real misperception inside Democratic legislative offices, and Silver’s central empirical observation — that the Black-representation contribution of a district is roughly linear in Black share but highly nonlinear in partisan lean — is well-supported by his charts. The Susquehanna example is the load-bearing one: it shows that the choice between “pack one majority-Black district” and “spread Black voters across three lean-blue districts” is not zero-sum if you grant that lean-blue districts reliably elect Democrats and Democratic primaries reliably produce minority candidates at high rates.

The two analytic vulnerabilities he glides past are worth naming. First, the Section 2 retrogression doctrine that Callais weakened existed precisely because of the worry that white-majority Democratic primaries in lean-blue districts would consistently nominate white candidates over Black ones — Silver’s S-curve assumes the cross-sectional 2024 distribution of Democratic nominees generalizes to a counterfactual world where many more districts are 15-25 percent Black rather than 50 percent. That’s an extrapolation, not a measurement. Second, the model treats partisan-electing-a-Democrat as exogenous to the redrawn map; in practice, deliberately spreading Black voters into formerly white-suburban districts shifts the median voter of the Democratic primary, and Silver doesn’t model whether the resulting Democratic nominee mix continues to look like the current 50-percent-minority caucus.

That said, Silver’s headline claim is more defensible than the dovish reading would suggest, because he’s specifically arguing about blue-state maps where the alternative is simply not engaging in partisan redistricting. The Maryland-and-Illinois Democratic resistance to maximally aggressive maps on Black-representation grounds is a real political fact that’s costing seats; calling that resistance empirically miscalibrated is a substantive contribution.

Back to summary

Virginia Democrats Get Their Comeuppance

The Editors · 2026-05-08 · Original →

POLITICS & POLICY

Virginia Democrats Get Their Comeuppance

By THE EDITORS
May 8, 2026 5:24 PM

Schadenfreude isn’t the most elevated emotion, but it’s impossible not to revel in it after the comeuppance of Virginia Democrats.

They blew by any number of legal obstacles to draw perhaps the most absurdly lopsided congressional map in the country, and congratulated themselves for their audacity.

They took a congressional map that favored Democrats 6–5 and made it one that would favor Democrats 10–1 — in a purplish state where Republicans held all the statewide offices as recently as last year.

This required amending Virginia’s constitution in a misleadingly worded, lavishly funded referendum that passed by a narrow margin a few weeks ago.

Virginia had previously amended its constitution to make an independent commission responsible for drawing district lines. To bypass the commission and impose the new heavily gerrymandered lines, Democrats had to reamend the constitution and do it on an expedited basis to produce their four new Democratic seats by the 2026 midterms.

They duly violated various rules to get their result and assumed that once a ballot measure had been passed by the voters, nothing would be done to reverse it. It was a perfect plan — except for the possibility that the Virginia Supreme Court might do its job, and sure enough, it just did.

The court ruled that the amendment is rendered invalid by Democrats’ failure to abide by the constitution’s strictures. To ensure that all amendments are the product of extensive deliberation, the constitution says that the General Assembly has to pass a constitutional amendment; then there has to be an intervening election for the General Assembly; and after that, the General Assembly has to pass the same amendment. Only then does the amendment go to voters.

In their zeal, Democrats used a special session of the General Assembly to initially pass the amendment in late October last year, even though early voting in the General Assembly elections had already started. This denied those voters knowledge of the constitutional amendment; the whole idea is to afford voters the opportunity to pass judgment on assembly members for votes for or against a proposed amendment. And so Democrats had frustrated one of the purposes of the amendment process.

This isn’t even a close call. Of course, there is much weeping and gnashing of teeth on the left.

Why do Republicans get to redistrict in their states, and Democrats can’t in Virginia? Well, if Republicans similarly violate their state constitutions, their efforts should get blocked, too. But there’s no getting around the fact that the Virginia Constitution determines how the Virginia Constitution is amended; there’s no break-glass provision if Democrats want to match or exceed the gerrymanders in Republican states.

How dare the Virginia Supreme Court decide this question after Virginia went to the trouble of holding a referendum, and after the public voted? This is especially rich given that Democrats opposed the court’s deciding the question before the vote. Indeed, their lawyers told the court that it had to wait until after the vote to rule — and won that argument at the time. They hoped that the justices would be too cowardly to overturn a ballot measure that had already been voted on, and they lost their big gamble.

This chapter in Virginia political history has been low and unedifying, but at the end of the day, the law prevailed.

THE EDITORS comprise the senior editorial staff of the National Review magazine and website.

Claude’s Response

This is a tightly-argued procedural editorial, and on its narrow legal point it’s hard to dispute: the constitutional text NR describes does say what it says, and the early-voting overlap on the special session does appear to have undercut the deliberation requirement. The schadenfreude is foregrounded but the underlying claim — that the court applied the rule and Democrats lost a gamble of their own choosing — is correctly identified as the operative finding.

What the editorial leaves unsaid is the larger frame in which all of this is happening. Texas Republicans started the mid-decade redistricting war by drawing aggressive new maps explicitly to bank House seats before 2026; Virginia Democrats responded by trying to amend their state constitution at speed to retaliate; the Virginia Supreme Court has now blocked the response while the original Texas action remains in effect. NR is correct that the Virginia constitution governs Virginia, but the celebration of “the law prevailing” reads differently when the predictable downstream effect is to lock in Republican gerrymanders nationally while the most plausible Democratic counter-route is foreclosed. The editorial would be more honest if it acknowledged the asymmetry rather than dismissing it as whining.

The other thing missing from a partisan editorial of this length is any treatment of why the Virginia constitution was structured this way — the deliberation requirement was inserted to stop exactly the kind of rushed partisan amendment Democrats attempted, and that’s a legitimately conservative procedural commitment NR could have foregrounded as substantive vindication of small-c constitutional caution rather than mere comeuppance.

Back to summary

Graham Platner Is No Nazi

Michelle Goldberg · 2026-05-08 · Original →

I have a confession: I would not have recognized the skull that the Democratic Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner had tattooed on his chest as a Nazi symbol. I knew that a skull and crossbones formed part of the insignia for the Nazi SS, but had never really clocked the image’s particularities — its three-quarter profile, the bones at the side of the skull instead of underneath — that gave it meaning even when stripped of all other Nazi context. So when Platner insists that he and his military buddies, drunk on shore leave, picked it off a tattoo parlor wall without recognizing it, it’s easy for me to believe him.

I also can’t quite blame those who detest Platner’s left-wing politics for refusing to give him the benefit of the doubt. They have an easy line of attack, and naturally they’re going to use it. Still, it’s worth drawing a distinction between what is true and what makes an easy political narrative.

Platner’s haters are embracing a distorted conception of him — and his following — built on a fundamental misunderstanding of his appeal. Some, appalled by Platner’s rise, have convinced themselves that many voters are drawn to him because of his supposed antisemitism, and that anyone who denies this is being mendacious. Platner, wrote Philip Klein in National Review, “had one thing going for him, which is that he gained attention for his antisemitism. That is a ticket to success in the modern Democratic Party.”

This argument is premised on a deliberate misreading of both Platner and his progressive fans. We know about many of the outré and occasionally disturbing views Platner once held because years of his Reddit posts have come to light. He’s apologized for some of them, saying that he made them at a time in his life when he was lost, deeply angry and suffering from PTSD from his military service in Iraq and Afghanistan. But aside from a single post, written when he was a bartender, suggesting that Black people were bad tippers, there is nothing in these messages that indicates an interest in racial hierarchies or anti-Jewish conspiracy theories.

On the contrary, he is consistently, militantly antifascist. If people “expect to fight fascism without a good semiautomatic rifle, they ought to do some reading of history,” he wrote in 2018. In another, he wrote that since leaving the military, he’d become a “vegetable growing, psychedelic taking socialist,” but added, “Still got the guns though, I don’t trust the fascists to act politely.” He referred to himself, jokingly, as an “antifa supersoldier,” and was involved with the Socialist Rifle Association, a left-wing alternative to the National Rifle Association. He lionized the men who volunteered to fight fascists in the Spanish Civil War.

These views appear consistent through much of his life. In high school, he was voted “most likely to start a revolution,” and his yearbook picture shows him holding a sign that says, “Free Kosovo Chechnya Kashmir Palestine Kurdistan Tibet.”

It’s worth pausing on the inclusion of “Palestine” here. Israel’s defenders often accuse pro-Palestinian activists of singling Israel out and ignoring human rights abuses elsewhere. But Platner, from a young age, seems to have seen the Palestinians as one oppressed people among many. His condemnation of Israel, unlike that of some of the country’s right-wing enemies, has nothing to do with its Jewish character, but with its killing and dispossession of a subject population, a stance shared by many left-wing Jews.

Israel’s champions may not want to hear it, but there’s a difference between those who revile Israel because it’s Jewish, and those who oppose it because they believe states should grant equal rights and liberties to all those under their dominion. People in the second camp are usually reliable champions of the multiracial democracy that has allowed Jews to thrive in America.

For some conservatives, there’s no contradiction between seeing Platner as both a leftist and a Nazi, since they think that Nazis were socialists. That’s mostly a canard, but even if you believe it, virtually no left-wing person does. In other words, the notion of a socialist antifa Nazi might make sense if you don’t take Platner’s politics seriously. But Platner appears to take them very seriously indeed.

I’ll admit I find it hard to believe that Platner didn’t know what his tattoo represented before last fall, when he had it covered up. An anonymous acquaintance told journalists that he jokingly called it “my Totenkopf,” and his former political director has said that Platner told her it could be “problematic.” My best guess about what happened is that he got the tattoo, later learned what it resembled, and didn’t take it seriously enough until it became a political liability. You might argue that alone is disqualifying. But to suggest that Platner harbors secret Nazi sympathies is to misjudge not just him, but his voters, many of whom are gravitating to him precisely because they see him as someone willing to fight fascism.

Of course, some of the right’s Platner discourse is simple trolling. Conservatives are angry at liberals who call people on the right Nazis and are relishing the chance to turn the tables. Attacking liberal Platner defenders on X, the writer Matt Shapiro was admirably frank about this. It’s absurd, he wrote, for liberals to “expect their right-wing opponents to be honest” about Platner’s tattoo given that “they have an entire industry” that levels “accusations of racism and Nazism in order to destroy the lives of their ideological opponents.”

As an illustration, he cites progressives who accused Elon Musk of giving a Nazi salute at Donald Trump’s inauguration rally. This seems to me an odd example, given the unending stream of white nationalist slop that Musk posts online. Still, I appreciate the acknowledgment that much of this debate is premised on dishonesty and revenge. Conservatives think they’ve been treated unfairly and are now seizing the opportunity to do the same to their foes.

It’s unfortunate that Platner gives them this opportunity. That, not any covert affection for Hitler, is the biggest problem with his candidacy.

Word count: 1067

Claude’s Response

The column’s strongest move is forcing the question of what evidence we’d accept about a person’s politics. Goldberg lays out years of consistent, contemporaneous antifascist self-description from someone who didn’t know he’d later run for office, then asks the reader to weigh that against a piece of skin art picked in a tattoo parlor. That’s a real argument, not just affect, and it’s the kind of evidentiary discipline the genre often skips. Her point that “those who oppose Israel because they believe states should grant equal rights” are categorically different from those who hate it for being Jewish is also genuinely useful — it does productive work distinguishing positions that the rhetorical machinery on both sides routinely conflates.

But the column dodges the strongest version of the charge. Nobody serious claims Platner is a Nazi; the more careful Republican attack is that a guy in his thirties spent multi-year stretches with a Totenkopf on his chest, was apparently warned by his political director that it could be “problematic,” and only covered it up when his Senate run made it costly — which suggests either remarkable obtuseness about iconography (implausible for someone steeped in WWII history) or a tolerance for ambiguity that voters might reasonably read as a character flag. Goldberg’s “my best guess” formulation — he got it, learned what it was, didn’t take it seriously enough until it was a liability — actually concedes this version of the case while still labeling it a misjudgment of “his voters.” That’s a slide. The voter point and the candidate point are separable.

The column is also weaker than it could be on the Reddit posts themselves. Goldberg mentions but barely engages the “Black people are bad tippers” post and “some of the outré and occasionally disturbing views” Platner has apologized for, both of which a fairer treatment would have to actually quote and adjudicate. The decision to wave at them as PTSD-era lapses while spending paragraphs on his Spanish Civil War lionization gives the piece a thumb-on-the-scale quality that undermines the evidentiary discipline she models elsewhere.

The Musk-salute aside (“the unending stream of white nationalist slop that Musk posts online”) is the giveaway that Goldberg is writing for an in-group. The whole point of the column is that we should evaluate political character on contemporaneous evidence rather than gestures and tattoos — but she stipulates the Musk case in a parenthetical without any of the same evidentiary work. Either the standard applies to both or it applies to neither.

Back to summary

‘Reverse Racism’ in Employment and Admissions Is Still Racism

Mene Ukueberuwa · 2026-05-08 · Original →

President Donald Trump’s second term may go down in history as the moment Washington decided that white people have civil rights under the same laws designed to protect racial minorities. It’s been clear for at least a decade that whites often face discrimination in hiring—sometimes barely concealed, sometimes openly celebrated. Investment banks announce plans to divide up job offers by race and gender, and tech companies issue updates on how much they increased minority leadership.

The Trump administration is addressing the most egregious instances of this trend, taking up discrimination cases on behalf of white workers and applicants the same way the government has done for other groups for the past 60 years. They are also ensuring that white and Asian applicants aren’t illegally penalized in college admissions. And so far, they’re doing it in a way that seeks to apply the law impartially rather than stoke further racial tensions.

This week has featured two major actions in this federal campaign. The Justice Department announced Wednesday that it had determined that the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA), is discriminating against white and Asian medical school applicants by deliberately admitting less-qualified blacks and Hispanics.

The Trump administration claims that UCLA’s admissions committee discussed how to conceal its discrimination by using “holistic” admissions criteria to offset the lower MCAT scores of black and Hispanic applicants. As a result, admitted blacks and Hispanics in 2023 had median test scores equivalent to the 68th percentile of all test-takers, compared with 96th percentile for applicants who declined to state their race on a survey (as whites and Asians typically do). The Justice Department’s finding is the first time that it has sought to enforce the Supreme Court’s 2023 ban on racial preferences at a medical school.

The administration is also suing The New York Times Company for allegedly passing over a white male editor for a promotion. The lawsuit filed Tuesday by the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) claims that the man was excluded from the last round of interviews for the role of deputy real estate editor last year, despite his years of experience as an editor on that topic. None of the finalists were white men, the suit alleges. The role was eventually given to a black woman, Monica Burton, who was an editor at Eater, had no experience covering real estate, and who interviewers rated lower than two other finalists.

Discrimination is hard to prove in court, and rightly so. It’s easy for a jilted employee to blame his race, and countless suits are filed by people who sniff racism where none existed, or who cynically try to shake down their employers despite knowing the process was fair. To hold the Times liable, the EEOC will likely need something firm, such as an email or quote from the hiring manager suggesting that race was determinative.

Yet until last year, that would not have been enough for many white male complainants. Until the Supreme Court struck them down last June, appellate court rulings had required members of a “majority” group not only to prove discrimination in their own case but also to show that their employer had engaged in a pattern of discrimination, a standard never required for minority complaints.

It’s as if the appeals courts were saying, We know whites are racist, so just tell us what happened and we’ll rule in your favor. But if you want to claim a white guy got the shaft . . . you’re gonna need a pricier lawyer. (The double standard applied in 20 states, though notably not in New York, where the Times case was filed.) The Supreme Court’s June ruling was unanimous, because the principle at issue is simple and hard to dispute: Discrimination is wrong and illegal regardless of which group is the target.

Together with last week’s ruling against race-based Congressional districts, the Supreme Court’s employment-law decision helped strip out antiwhite discrimination that had become embedded in law. The lawsuit against the Times—which has denied the claims and called the suit “politically motivated”—and others like it take the natural next step in combating discriminatory habits and policies that often began as good-natured outreach to minority communities, but quickly hardened into an explicit preference.

The claim that whites are brutally and universally victimized in the corporate world today is easy to find on the online right. “I think the oppression of white men in contemporary America is worse than what black slaves went through in America,” wrote one anonymous account known as Bumbadum in an X post that kicked off a couple of days of fierce debate. That type of claim is harmful. In the same way that black race hustlers stir up anger among their followers, their white counterparts stoke needless resentment toward minorities when they exaggerate the scale of antiwhite racism.

But real cases of antiwhite discrimination exist, often encouraged by misguided corporate policies. The Trump administration is pursuing this one as part of its campaign against diversity, equity, and inclusion policies in the corporate world. The Trump Justice Department has similarly launched criminal investigations into possible civil-rights violations against whites. The department’s Civil Rights Division is currently probing claims of discrimination in Los Angeles public schools, Minnesota state government, and among several federal contractors.

Instead of fuming about the supposed oppression of whites, the Justice Department and EEOC have vigorously but soberly fought discrimination precisely where the evidence suggests it really exists. “I don’t even use the term reverse racism,” said Harmeet Dhillon in her confirmation hearings to lead the Justice Department’s Civil Rights Division. “It’s just racism to discriminate on the basis of race.”

There may be examples in which the administration has overstated its case, or launched an investigation in haste. And their attitude could certainly change in a moment. But in general, the administration is striking a crucial balance that progressives recently haven’t cared to: fighting against racism, regardless of the targeted group, without inflaming more of it.

Claude’s Response

The legal substantive point — that the unanimous Supreme Court ruling closed an asymmetric pleading standard that had genuinely existed in twenty states — is correct and worth surfacing. Title VII (the federal employment-discrimination statute) was always race-neutral on its face; the appellate-court doctrine requiring “majority” plaintiffs to prove a pattern of discrimination beyond their individual case really was a judicially created asymmetry, and getting rid of it is consistent with the statute’s text. Ukueberuwa is also correct that there’s a real legal-realist constituency on the right that wants to push the racial-grievance analysis well past where the law actually goes (the “Bumbadum” character he cites is the strawman version, but a steelman version with similar conclusions exists in more respectable venues). The Dhillon “it’s just racism” framing is the strongest version of the administration’s position and the one most consistent with what most courts have said since SFFA v. Harvard.

But the piece treats the UCLA and NYT cases as if the DOJ’s framing of them were established fact, and that’s the load-bearing weakness. The MCAT-percentile gap (68th vs 96th) is presented without engagement with the considerable empirical literature on how holistic admissions programs actually work — including that medical school admissions weight clinical experience, research, interview performance, and letters of recommendation alongside MCAT scores, and that “holistic review” was an established framework long before 2023. Whether UCLA’s specific committee discussions crossed the SFFA line or stayed inside the holistic-review framework SCOTUS explicitly left intact is a fact-dependent question the column doesn’t engage. Similarly, the NYT case is presented in EEOC framing — a white male editor with real-estate experience was excluded from the final round, the role went to a black woman with no real-estate experience — but the column doesn’t engage the NYT’s response that the suit is “politically motivated” or examine whether the EEOC will actually meet its evidentiary burden (which Ukueberuwa correctly notes will require “an email or quote from the hiring manager”). The piece is treating preliminary administrative actions as if they were already proven.

The “crucial balance” closing claim — that the administration is fighting racism without inflaming it — is the one that requires the most skepticism. The same week the DOJ announced these actions, the broader Trump civil-rights policy includes investigations of Los Angeles public schools, Minnesota state government, and “several federal contractors” — a pattern that on a different framing reads as expansive federal intervention into private and state employment practices, of exactly the kind the conservative legal movement has historically opposed. Ukueberuwa wants to read this pattern as principled symmetry; an equally available reading is that it’s a redirection of civil-rights enforcement infrastructure toward a different protected class, with the same prosecutorial-discretion problems that progressive critics have always raised about civil-rights litigation. The column doesn’t engage this counter-frame at all. It also elides what may be the most consequential institutional question: whether the same DOJ Civil Rights Division will continue to bring cases on behalf of black and Hispanic plaintiffs at the same rate it brought them under previous administrations. That’s the actual test of “balance,” and it isn’t addressed.

Finally, the rhetorical structure of the piece — concede the strawmen (“real cases of antiwhite discrimination exist,” “Bumbadum is wrong”), then claim the administration is occupying the moderate position — is the standard FP move on contested terrain, and it works on a reader who is already inclined to see the post-SFFA enforcement environment as a normalization rather than a politicization. A reader who’s skeptical of the framing won’t be moved, because the column hasn’t addressed the actual contested questions — whether the holistic-review framework was being abused or correctly applied at UCLA, whether the EEOC has the goods on the NYT, and whether the Civil Rights Division’s enforcement portfolio is still symmetric across racial groups. The piece is competent advocacy in the FP register, but it’s advocacy, not analysis.

Back to summary

Insane Pre-Crime Strategy Unveiled for Leftist “Extremists"

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-05-06 · Original →

Sebastian Gorka

Subscriptions are declining; PLEASE become a paid subscriber so I can keep doing this work

The White House declared war on the American people today, labeling its political opponents as terrorists, including “Left-wing extremists.” The new label also claims that there are “deepening alliances” between “the far-left and Islamists” — or pro-Palestinian protesters.

The language is contained in the White House’s newly released National Counterterrorism Strategy. It is the first National Strategy to be unveiled since 2021, when the Biden administration issued its document. The Strategy identifies the “left-wing,” “anti-Fascists,” “Anarchists” and “radically pro-transgender” ideologies as threats equivalent to jihadi groups like al Qaeda and ISIS, or narco-traffickers.

The Strategy is the brainchild of White House counterterrorism czar Sebastian Gorka, an eccentric figure I have reported on, who last year hinted at terrorism charges being levied for political opponents of the administration. The document makes clear he got his wish. Gorka called the Strategy “my life’s work,” and apparently waxed so poetic in previous drafts that his superiors told him (by his own account): “Cut it down, Gorka!”

“Currently we face three major types of terror groups,” the Strategy says, listing “Narcoterrorists and Transnational Gangs,” “Legacy Islamist Terrorists,” and “Violent Left-Wing Extremists, including Anarchists and Anti-Fascists.”

Strategy

2.52MB ∙ PDF file

Download

Download

“Counterterrorism” itself is a propaganda term, sanitizing the actual practice: pre-crime, which aims to build cases against people for what they might do, most ominously based on speech or beliefs. (I’ve written about the Bureau’s pre-crime push at length.) The Strategy doesn’t bother hiding it. It promises to “Identify terror actors and plots before they happen” and to use “law enforcement tools to cripple them operationally before they can maim or kill the innocent.”

The Strategy also hints at a crackdown on pro-Palestinian groups. In a section laying out “five functional aspects of the current CT environment” beyond the previously named three categories, it warns of “New and deepening alliances between the far-left and Islamists, i.e., the ‘Red-Green’ alliance” — a phrase borrowed from conservative discourse to suggest a conspiratorial alignment between the American left and radical Islam.

The “Red-Green alliance” term has been pushed by Israeli think tanks like the Reut Group — which defines the term as “the nexus between radical progressive groups to Islamists organizations” — and picked up by right-wing U.S. news outlets. The framing is designed to recast pro-Palestinian activism as a front for jihadism.

The Strategy proposes employing the same tactics used to map out jihadi networks like al Qaeda against Americans here at home, promising “rapid identification and neutralization” of the supposed threat. This is exactly the technique that Gorka and other top Trump officials like Kash Patel cut their teeth on during the post-9/11 global war on terror — experience that I previously warned they would lean heavily on.

Per the document:

“In addition to cartels and Islamist terror groups, our national CT [counterterrorism] activities will also prioritize the rapid identification and neutralization of violent secular political groups whose ideology is anti-American, radically pro-transgender, and anarchist. We will use all the tools constitutionally available to us to map them at home, identify their membership, map their ties to international organizations like Antifa, and use law enforcement tools to cripple them operationally before they can maim or kill the innocent.”

National Security Presidential Memorandum 7 (NSPM-7) defines the threat as those espousing “anti-American,” “anti-Christian” and “anti-Capitalism” viewpoints. The new National Strategy uniquely identifies “radically pro-transgender ideologies” as terrorists, adding yet another threat group to the federal government’s targeting.

As I reported last year, the FBI in response to Charlie Kirk’s murder was preparing a war on what it considers transgender “extremism,” based on my sources. That report was subject to endless fact-checks asking how I knew this — something I can’t answer without burning my sources — and whether I was spreading panic in the transgender community. The counterterrorism strategy affirms my reporting, explicitly pointing to “the assassination of Charlie Kirk by a radical who espoused extreme transgender ideologies” as part of its justification for the new targeting.

The Strategy is, in important respects, Charlie Kirk’s strategy. Within hours of his September 10, 2025 murder at Utah Valley University, White House, Justice, and Homeland Security officials scrambled to draft a sweeping domestic crackdown, as I previously reported. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent called the killing “a domestic 9/11,” vowing his department would now do to American political networks what it once did to al Qaeda’s in going after their finances.

Charlie Kirk was not just an ally of the new terror hunters. Trump reportedly considered the 31-year-old Turning Point USA co-founder part of his “extended family,” in his words, with a direct line to the Oval Office that few others enjoyed. The President credited Kirk with delivering young voters in 2024, posthumously awarded him the Presidential Medal of Freedom on what would have been his 32nd birthday, and used his February 2026 State of the Union to mourn “my great friend Charlie Kirk,” with Kirk’s widow Erika seated in the chamber as a special guest.

Kirk’s death resulted in the issuance of NSPM-7, on which much of the new counterterrorism Strategy is built.

The new Strategy also leans heavily on grievances about the “weaponization” of counterterrorism powers under prior administrations to justify a sweeping expansion of those same powers. It cites past FBI controversies — surveillance of “conservative Catholics attending traditional mass in Virginia,” “parents standing up for their children at school board meetings,” and investigations of “Members of Congress, or President Trump and his associates” — as the rationale for what it calls a previous “radical shift” in U.S. counterterrorism.

In other words: abuses of counterterrorism authorities against the right have now become the grounds for unleashing those same authorities against the left.

The Strategy anticipates this reversal and tries to inoculate against it. “Our counterterrorism powers will not be used to target our fellow Americans who simply disagree with us,” it insists, before going on to describe a regime that does precisely that — provided the disagreement is categorized as “extremism.”

Except that’s exactly what it does!

The strategy includes a foreword by President Trump that emphasizes its unparalleled focus on the homeland: “my Administration has put an unprecedented focus on dismantling threats to the American homeland in our Hemisphere.”

The global war on terror has come home.

Leave a comment

— Edited by William M. Arkin

Claude’s Response

The piece is investigative-report register: Klippenstein’s job here is primarily to surface the document and quote it. The reporting holds up because the most inflammatory framings — “left-wing extremists,” “radically pro-transgender ideologies,” “rapid identification and neutralization,” “Red-Green alliance” — are direct quotations from the strategy itself, not Klippenstein’s gloss. A reader who would otherwise dismiss the framing as activist hyperbole has to contend with the fact that the document’s own text uses these formulations. That is the column’s primary contribution and it is load-bearing.

The “pre-crime” frame (a Minority Report reference) is Klippenstein’s editorial coinage and carries normative weight that should be tested against what the document actually authorizes. Counterterrorism investigations have always involved pre-attack disruption — that is the doctrine, not an aberration. What is novel here is the target: the move from al-Qaeda-style transnational networks to domestic political ideologies defined by viewpoint (“anti-American,” “anti-Christian,” “anti-Capitalism” per NSPM-7), and the explicit promise to use the same intelligence-mapping techniques developed against jihadi cells against US political networks. Klippenstein is right that this is a categorical shift, and right that the document’s protective language (“not… target our fellow Americans who simply disagree with us”) is performative — the operative text immediately defines disagreement as “extremism” when it is left-coded. The pre-crime label is therefore not pure rhetoric; it tracks a real feature of the document. But the framing also imports a Philip K. Dick gloss that does some work the underlying material doesn’t quite support — the strategy is closer to “viewpoint-based threat assessment” than to literal predictive arrest, and a reader interested in pushing back on the policy would be better armed by the quoted text than by the Minority Report reference.

The strongest analytical move in the piece is Klippenstein’s observation that “abuses of counterterrorism authorities against the right have now become the grounds for unleashing those same authorities against the left.” The cited justifications inside the document — surveillance of conservative Catholics in Virginia, parents at school board meetings, investigations of Trump associates — are presented as evidence that the previous counterterror apparatus was politically captured, and therefore the new apparatus needs the same powers pointed in the opposite direction. That logic, if accepted, generates a permanent ratchet: each administration cites the previous one’s abuses as the rationale for its own. Klippenstein names this dynamic crisply.

What’s weak: the column doesn’t engage seriously with the question of how the document’s authors would respond to its own framing — for example, whether they would dispute that “anti-American” and “anti-Christian” are viewpoint categories rather than threat indicators, or how the strategy distinguishes “ideology” from “violent activity.” A reader who wants to understand whether this document is more dangerous than ordinary executive-branch counterterrorism boilerplate (much of which has historically named left-coded targets too — see the FBI’s COINTELPRO history) would benefit from a sharper comparative claim. The column gets to the right outrage but doesn’t fully establish the comparative weight, which the document’s actual implementation will determine.

Back to summary

Your enemies aren’t mentally ill. They apparently just want to kill you.

Auron MacIntyre · 2026-05-07 · Original →

Ignoring the realities of good and evil in favor of science and mental health has left us unable to understand those who would do us harm.

In our modern world, it is very common to conflate motives we do not understand with some form of mental illness. This is understandable when it comes to horrific crime. A serial killer is so violent and twisted that it is hard for us to comprehend his actions, and there is certainly a fair amount of mental illness that is a factor.

Today, however, people often use this explanation when it comes to political disagreements. Abortion, hatred of Christians, the mutilation of children — these beliefs are so horrible that they can only possibly be explained by a malfunctioning brain. But that is not the only explanation. The other option is that some people have a very different set of values that drive them to pursue goals we view as evil.

The average American would like to avoid this truth, because it comes with an unnerving conclusion: Your political enemies are not crazy; they are sane people who hate you and want to hurt you.

As sophisticated and modern people, we tend to avoid language that implies some form of objective metaphysical truth. Evil is a concept that conjures up images of old churches, judgmental Sunday-school teachers, and medieval peasants trying to explain a drought.

We have the technology and understanding to explain everything in the world around us. Explanations that call on unseen forces or divine intervention are no longer required. In this thoroughly materialistic view, humans are simply animals with more sophisticated brains. Any undesirable behavior, therefore, can be understood as a malfunction of that brain.

There is no evil; there is only mental illness.

Modern Americans are universalists in a very strange sense. We assume that our values, beliefs, and customs are the default for all humans everywhere. Americans believe that all our assumptions about the way life ought to be lived are arrived at through individual human rationality, so if another person has a functioning brain, he will, of course, come to the same conclusion.

When we are confronted with someone who has very different understandings or goals, it is assumed that something has happened to his faculties of reason.

We try to argue with these people in hopes that rational debate will help them see the error of their ways. If debate does not work, we begin diagnosing the dissenting individual with all kinds of pathologies that explain why he doesn’t see the world the way we do. The explanation for divergent morals or goals is always a defective mental process, never a genuine difference in how we view the world.

The obsession with material explanations accounts for half of this phenomenon, but the other half is explained by our desperate need to avoid real conflict.

One of the major selling points of liberalism was the reduced need for violent conflict by removing existential questions from the political arena. Who is right about God? What is the ultimate good? To what end should we orient our society? These are all important but dangerous questions. The answers are exclusive and all-encompassing.

These are the things that people are willing to kill and die for, so better to make them off-limits and focus on something everyone can agree on — making more money and increasing the standard of living.

The desire to end the terrible wars that were fought over religion or identity is entirely understandable, and it is difficult to argue that the focus on economic cooperation did not produce significant benefits, but this was always a trade-off that could never last.

We may like to pretend that we are too advanced and sophisticated to get hung up on these primitive concerns, but under the surface, they continue to define us. Those conflicts do not disappear, and when they re-emerge, you have stripped your civilization of the tools needed to identify and address them, which is exactly what we are seeing now.

Americans no longer have the moral language to discuss good and evil, so they simply apply a clinical diagnosis to their opponents instead. Believing a radical leftist is mentally ill is far easier than addressing the alternative. If the progressive is a perfectly sane person and still wants to take your son if you do not chop off his genitals, then the calculus for what must be done changes radically.

Differences of opinion can be navigated as long as two parties share the same moral assumptions and the same goals. But if there is a fundamental difference in what is considered good or evil — if two groups seek radically opposed outcomes — then no amount of rational debate will ever resolve the core issue.

If our differences are not based on mental illness but fundamental beliefs about right and wrong, then a disturbing conclusion emerges. When rational debate is no longer an option, then the only solution left is the same one our unsophisticated ancestors came to for centuries — one group must win and the other must lose.

In the best scenario, this means an exercise of power from the winning side that allows it to rule over the other, forcing its way of life upon the defeated foe. In the worst scenario, this means violence and war until one side is no longer willing to fight to defend its way of life.

In either case, this is a return to an existential understanding of politics. The stakes are no longer higher taxes or lower taxes, but survival.

It should be said that mental illness is real and can be a factor in some political trends, but the idea that half of Americans are crazy because they do not share your political views is absurd. The truth is much darker: We are at least two societies, with mutually exclusive understandings of morality and purpose, trapped in one country.

The theoretical neutrality of the liberal system allowed this drift to occur under the surface, but the differences have become too extreme to ignore. Both sides have their own internally consistent understanding of the world, but they are entirely incompatible with each other.

One side is going to win, one side is going to lose, and the winning side is going to impose its way of life on the other. There is no way to avoid this reality, and obscuring the truth with comforting fictions about mental illness only ensures that you will be the side that loses.

Claude’s Response

MacIntyre’s piece is structurally Schmittian — Carl Schmitt being the German jurist whose 1932 “The Concept of the Political” defined politics as the friend/enemy distinction and whose work has become foundational reading on the post-liberal right. The Schmittian frame does some real work: it’s true that the liberal procedural bargain depends on a willingness to bracket ultimate questions, and it’s true that when that bracketing breaks down, conflicts get sharper. The piece is right that pathologizing opponents (the “Republicans are mentally ill” or “Trump voters have authoritarian personalities” genres) is intellectually lazy and that taking opponents’ values seriously as values is a more honest engagement.

The central claim does not survive scrutiny. MacIntyre’s framing — that Americans uniformly default to pathologizing political opponents as a category, and that this is the dominant cultural mode — collapses two distinct things: (1) the aforementioned lazy left-coded discourse about authoritarian personalities and Trump-derangement-syndrome diagnoses, and (2) actual psychological research on radicalization and mass-shooter motivation, where mental-illness profiles ARE genuinely contested. More importantly, the piece’s own example — “the progressive is a perfectly sane person and still wants to take your son if you do not chop off his genitals” — is itself a paradigm case of the kind of pathologizing rhetorical move he’s denouncing, just reversed. The actual substantive disagreement on adolescent gender care involves clinicians and parents weighing puberty-blocker evidence, social-transition outcomes, and detransition rates — there are sane disagreements within that frame, and MacIntyre doesn’t engage with any of them. He instead translates the disagreement into a willingness-to-mutilate-children frame designed to make the opposing side appear monstrous. This is exactly the move the piece formally rejects.

The bigger problem is the symmetry the piece quietly assumes. MacIntyre’s brief is presented as neutral observation: “We are at least two societies, with mutually exclusive understandings of morality and purpose, trapped in one country,” with violence the implicit endpoint. But the operational situation is asymmetric. The institutional moves toward Schmittian friend/enemy politics — pressuring the FCC against opposition broadcasters, deploying the National Guard inside US cities against domestic political opponents, gerrymandering Black-majority districts out of existence under cover of a 6-3 SCOTUS ruling, the active stripping of state autonomy through emergency powers (see Berman’s piece this week on Louisiana suspending an in-progress primary) — are coming overwhelmingly from one direction. The progressive equivalent (NYC mayoral candidate Mamdani’s tax plans, district attorneys with lenient bail policies, university DEI offices) is doing nothing comparable in institutional weight. By framing the “one side wins, one side loses, the winning side imposes its way of life” outcome as an inevitable structural feature of value disagreement, MacIntyre is functionally licensing the actual escalation his coalition is conducting while presenting himself as a detached observer of mutual incomprehension.

Fundamental-flaw flag: the piece’s central claim — that the dominant American mode is pathologizing political opponents as mentally ill — is not just unsupported but contradicted by the rhetorical move the piece itself uses (framing trans-care advocates as wanting to “take your son” and “chop off his genitals”). The framework is also asymmetrically applied: MacIntyre invokes Schmitt’s friend/enemy logic to license the “one side wins, one side imposes” outcome without engaging with which side is currently doing the imposing. The piece reads as ideological cover for institutional escalation dressed as neutral diagnosis, in the same family as last week’s MacIntyre column whose 70-year-monopoly-on-political-violence claim collapsed under documentary scrutiny. Per-theme writers and the synthesis composer should treat the central frame as advocacy, not analysis.

Back to summary

Antisemitism Is Getting More Violent

Josh Kaplan · 2026-05-08 · Original →

For those of us following the story of growing antisemitism across the world, this week’s Anti-Defamation League (ADL) 2025 audit report came as something of a shock. For the first time in five years, the U.S.’s foremost antisemitism watchdog reported a decline in the total number of incidents.

On the face of it, we should celebrate this news. But it also feels counterintuitive, particularly after a year in which we saw the killing of Jews across America, from Washington, D.C., to Boulder, Colorado. And the truth is, we’re not turning a corner on the rising tide of Jew-hatred. While individual incidents of antisemitism may be falling, the prejudice is getting more violent. Let me explain.

In 2024, the ADL recorded 9,354 incidents of anti-Jewish hate, its highest ever total. In 2025, this number dropped to 6,274. That can largely be explained by the cratering of antisemitic incidents on college campuses. 2024 was the zenith of the protest movement that swept across American universities. Since then, a combination of lawsuits, Trump administration threats, and congressional hearings seem to have drummed into the heads of college administrators a desire to take the problem of antisemitism seriously. That’s not to say this problem is gone; as the ADL notes in its report: “Incidents on college campuses remained almost three times higher in 2025 than in 2021.”

However, what seems to be following the wave of college protests is even more troubling. The only category of antisemitism to have grown in the past year is physical assaults, with violence against Jews in the U.S. reaching a 47-year high. This tells us a few things. First, it puts a nail in the coffin of the idea, often parroted on the anti-Zionist left, that much of what gets called antisemitism is criticism of Israel or speech protected by the First Amendment. Second, the headline claim that “antisemitic incidents decreased significantly” is rendered less meaningful when more Jews than ever are being assaulted and killed. According to a report from Tel Aviv University published earlier this year, 2025 was the worst year for antisemitic violence across the world in three decades. Those researchers found that “in every Western country, the total number of incidents remained dozens of percentage points higher than in 2022” and that violence was increasing rapidly in most of the countries they looked at.

Against a backdrop of rising political violence in America, it’s perhaps no surprise that violence is finding its way towards Jewish targets. Time and time again, history has proven that the way a society treats Jews is a reflection of the health of its civil life. As the late UK chief rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks said: “Antisemitism is a projection, and is caused not by Jews but by internal conflicts in the societies that give rise to it.”

UNITED STATES

Protesters Surround Manhattan Synagogue Hosting Israeli Real Estate Expo

Over 100 protesters surrounded Park East Synagogue on Manhattan’s Upper East Side on Tuesday as it hosted an Israeli real estate event, chanting “There is only one solution, intifada revolution” and “We don’t want a two-state, we want ’48.” A spokesman for Mayor Zohran Mamdani publicly condemned the expo, calling West Bank property sales “illegal under international law.” The ADL said he “had a responsibility to de-escalate” and “did the opposite.” It was the second protest at the same synagogue in six months.

University of Michigan Faculty Chair Praises Pro-Palestinian Protesters at Commencement

Faculty Senate Chair Derek Peterson told graduates at a May 2 campus-wide commencement to “sing for the pro-Palestinian student activists, who have over these past two years opened our hearts to the injustice and inhumanity of Israel’s war in Gaza.” University president Domenico Grasso apologized the same day, saying Peterson “deviated from the remarks he had shared” and that the comments were “hurtful and insensitive to many members of our community.”

Georgetown Law Students Force Out Jewish Pro-Israel Speaker

A student petition with 282 signatures successfully prevented Morton Schapiro—a pro-Israel Jewish economist and former university president—from delivering the Georgetown University Law Center commencement address on May 17. The petition called for his removal, saying Schapiro “holds controversial, Zionist, and harmful opinions.” Georgetown replaced him with David Cole, the American Civil Liberty Union’s former legal director who in 2023 condemned the ADL and Louis D. Brandeis Center for Human Rights Under Law for their calling to investigate pro-Palestinian student groups.

Four Suspects Sought After Antisemitic Vandalism Across Queens

Four suspects allegedly spray-painted swastikas and antisemitic slogans on two synagogues, two private homes, and a car across the Rego Park and Forest Hills neighborhoods of Queens, New York, on May 4. At one synagogue, the swastika was painted directly over a plaque memorializing Holocaust victims. City Council Speaker Julie Menin, who visited the sites, said congregants and families “were met with terrifying signals of hatred and threats of violence.” The NYPD Hate Crimes Task Force is investigating.

Network of 49 AI-Generated Fake Rabbis Amasses 10 Million Likes Pushing Antisemitic Conspiracy Theories on TikTok

The Combat Antisemitism Movement (CAM) released a new report Tuesday identifying a coordinated network of 49 TikTok accounts using AI-generated rabbi avatars to package classic antisemitic tropes as “insider Jewish financial wisdom,” amassing more than 950,000 followers and over 10 million likes. CAM called TikTok “complicit in accelerating radicalization” and noted the operation follows a similar network it exposed on Instagram in March.

Antisemitic Graffiti at Maryland Elementary School

Antisemitic graffiti was discovered on the exterior of Greenwood Elementary School in Brookeville, Maryland, on Saturday, prompting condemnation from Governor Wes Moore and a police investigation. The Jewish Community Relations Council of Greater Washington called for a zero-tolerance antisemitism policy, calling this incident only the latest in a “deeply troubling pattern” across Montgomery County’s public schools—citing anti-Israel messaging at a school culture night and threats against Jewish students. The district is already under a federal Title VI investigation for antisemitism and has had four educators placed on administrative leave for sharing antisemitic content on social media.

UNITED KINGDOM

Counterterrorism Police Investigate Synagogue Arson

Counterterrorism police investigated an arson attack at the former East London Central Synagogue in Whitechapel on Tuesday, after an unknown suspect set fire to the building’s gates early in the morning. The fire was quickly extinguished and there were no injuries. The synagogue closed in 2020, but earlier this year a Muslim group put down a deposit to purchase the building and convert it into a mosque and education center. The Metropolitan Police announced a new dedicated 100-officer unit the following day to protect Jewish communities across London.

Green Party Candidates Face Antisemitism Probes

Ahead of nationwide elections on Thursday, the Labour Party released a dossier on Green candidates with extremist social media histories, and a Daily Mail investigation also revealed that 30 Green candidates are under internal party investigation for antisemitic posts—including one who promoted an October 7 “false flag” theory and another who compared Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu to Adolf Hitler.

EUROPE

Israeli Restaurant and Berlin Synagogue Targeted

Billal Aloge, a Syrian Kurdish Muslim who opened Israeli restaurant Jaffa in the western Germany city of Freiburg to foster Jewish-Arab coexistence, had his food truck vandalized twice in three days—paint thrown across the exterior, Israeli symbols defaced with antisemitic slogans, and side mirror broken—totaling approximately €30,000 in damage. This comes soon after a synagogue in the eastern city of Cottbus was defaced with a swastika for the second time in four days, and buildings in the Pankow borough of Berlin were spray-painted with “Kill all Jews” and “Only a dead Jew is a good Jew.”

Belgium Charges Two Jewish Men for Performing Ritual Circumcisions

The Antwerp Public Prosecutor’s Office announced it will charge two Jewish men for performing circumcisions, which Belgian law requires be done by licensed medical professionals. Israeli foreign minister Gideon Sa’ar called it “a scarlet letter on Belgian society,” saying Belgium had joined “a short and shameful list, together with Ireland, of countries that use criminal law to prosecute Jews for practicing Judaism.” Belgian foreign minister Maxime Prévot dismissed the condemnation, saying the proceedings were initiated by members of the Jewish community themselves.

Protesters in Venice Scream “Murderer” at Father of Nova Massacre Victim

In advance of the 61st Venice Biennale, pro-Palestinian protesters berated Eyal Waldman upon learning he was Israeli. Waldman is co-founder of Mellanox Technologies and one of Israel’s most prominent advocates for peace and coexistence with the Palestinian people. He has employed Palestinian engineers and donated $360,000 to construct an oncology ward at a Gaza hospital. The protesters called him a murderer and shouted obscenities at him. Waldman’s daughter Danielle and her boyfriend were killed by Hamas at the Nova music festival on October 7.

Dutch WWII Memorial Defaced with Graffiti and Blood-Red Paint on Remembrance Day

Vandals splashed red paint and spray-painted “genocide” across Amsterdam’s National Monument in Dam Square—the country’s central site for honoring World War II victims—on Monday, Dutch National Remembrance Day. Amsterdam mayor Femke Halsema called it “an incredibly cowardly deed.” Police are searching for three suspects who fled on bicycles; the same monument was defaced during a pro-Palestinian demonstration last August.

Claude’s Response

The substitution-effect argument — that aggregate incident counts can fall while severity rises and that this is a worse equilibrium, not a better one — is genuinely important, and Kaplan is right to lead with it. If campus protest activity displaces into physical assaults, the policy that suppressed protests has not solved anything; it has just changed the channel. The ADL data point on assaults reaching a 47-year high is the kind of single number that would deserve top billing on its own and Kaplan deserves credit for not letting the topline number dominate.

But the analytical apparatus around that number is shakier than the number itself. The “puts a nail in the coffin” of the protected-speech defense doesn’t actually follow: even if some campus protests crossed into harassment that the ADL reasonably catalogued as antisemitic incidents, the existence of a separate physical-assault trendline doesn’t tell us anything about whether the campus protest speech was protected. The two phenomena can both be real and one can be protected and the other not. Kaplan is using the assault data as a rhetorical lever to retrospectively re-classify the speech he wanted re-classified anyway, which is a non-sequitur.

The piece also has the structural weakness of FP’s recurring antisemitism roundups: a polished argument paragraph at the top, then a long list of incidents arranged for accumulation effect rather than for analytic purchase. The incidents are real and individually well-sourced, but treating “Belgian prosecutors charging Jews for performing circumcisions” (a contested medical-regulation case with Jewish-community participation in the prosecution, as Kaplan notes) as morally equivalent to “Berlin building tagged ‘Kill all Jews’” flattens the moral landscape. The Georgetown Schapiro case (student petition successfully removing a pro-Israel commencement speaker) is real but happens to be the kind of incident where the “antisemitism” charge is genuinely contested — Schapiro’s replacement, ACLU’s David Cole, is himself Jewish — and Kaplan glides over that by characterizing Cole only by his criticism of the ADL.

The Sacks quote — “antisemitism is a projection… caused not by Jews but by internal conflicts in the societies that give rise to it” — is doing more work than Kaplan acknowledges. It’s the kind of statement that sounds profound while functionally absolving any specific cause and pointing nowhere in particular. If antisemitism is a societal-projection symptom, the analytic move is to identify what the projection is of in which society — Western anti-elite resentment, Israeli-policy-driven displacement, far-right vs. far-left vector differences, AI-amplified conspiracy content. Kaplan gestures at the AI-amplification problem (the TikTok rabbi network is a striking data point) but doesn’t develop it. The piece’s overall move — accumulate incidents, gesture at projection, leave the diagnosis abstract — is competent FP-house style, but it’s the diagnostic shallowness, not the data, that limits the contribution.

Back to summary

Chartbook 446 Soft power with Chinese characteristics? Notes on Chinamaxxing.

Adam Tooze · 2026-05-08 · Original →

Chinamaxxing is a bit of a thing in the Anglosphere right now.

I’m far from immune to the enthusiasm.

In the salon series that Wang Qing and I host at Accent Society bookstore, I recently had the pleasure of talking with Dan Wang and Iza Ding about the phenomenon. It turned out to be a fascinating conversation very much energized by our sell-out audience.

Though the panelists disagreed in their assessments of the dynamics of Chinese culture in the current moment, there was general agreement that this is not in a time of particular vibrancy. So, the current vogue for China in the West is not plausibly explained by an exciting cultural efflorescence going on in China itself.

The most likely explanations are to be found in the sheer fact of China’s rise on the one hand, the increasing sophistication of its consumer and online culture and the contrast to the sense of the malaise in the Anglosphere and the exhaustion of the glamor of Western brands.

To be more specific, from our conversation one could distill at least seven vectors of China-Maxxing in the current moment.

#1 The most obvious is technocratic envy - Dan Wang’s thesis in Breakneck, that China is a society run by engineers, bent on “engineering the future”.

#2 Then there is the shock of the encounter with Chinese reality on the part of visitors to China - whether those visits are stage managed by official tourist agencies, or not.

#3 Another dimension of enthusiasm is retro-authenticity - street scenes from Beijing on Chongqing as reminders of a lost world of “down to earth” life.

#4 There is a lot of interest in Chinese ways of wellness

#5 The West has discovered that Chinese netizens have a great sense of humor.

#6 Chinese popular culture is one of the great generators of the styles and paraphernalia of “cuteness”.

#7 The erotic realm: recurring rumors of epidemics of “Yellow fever” sweeping the dating scene.

Whatever prognosis one has about the durability of this convergence, or its individual components, as Iza Ding remarks in a recent piece on the “grinning defiance of Chinese soft power” for the Ideas Letter, it should put paid to the common liberal conception that China, under CPC control, cannot be a generator of soft power.

Whether or not this enthusiasm for things Chinese can be utilized by Beijing is quite another matter. Official China is not oblivious to the phenomenon of Chinamaxxing. I heard it referred to several times from the rostrum at the China Development Forum in Beijing in March. But, on the Accent panel there was was skepticism that the current vogue would be skillfully exploited for propaganda purposes. The idea that there are cultural grand strategists at work in Beijing to compare with the CIA and its support for abstract expressionism in the 1950s seems far-fetched.

The current moment of Chinamaxxing may be intense but it is not without precedent.

What came to my mind was the moment of global enthusiasm for Maoism and the cultural revolution in the late 1960s and early 1970s. “Mao-maxxing” had far more dramatic political implications than the current social media hype.

Then there was the era of Hong Kong-Kung Fu enthusiasm, embodied by celebrities such as the ubiquitous Jackie Chan.

East Asian developmentalism has long had is Western admirers. Today’s technocratic China-Maxxers are often reminded that China is following in the footsteps of Japan, and South Korea and Taiwan.

“Cuteness”, into which Labubu dolls have exploded, was prefigured by Japanese and South Korean precedents.

So, not only is today’s vogue for China-Maxxing driven by China’s new economic strength and power. It is overdetermined by this wider context of global economic, political and cultural development.

The view strongly urged by Dan Wang was that the question we should be asking is not why Chinamaxxing is a “thing” but why, given China’s spectacular development, it is not even more dramatic. In his view, Beijing’s repression of free cultural activity has the effect of minimizing and dumbing down China’s cultural exports, thus undermining their overall impact. A freer China would be more influential.

Imagine the possibilities if Kaiser Kuo is right in his exciting prediction that China is on the “cusp of a genuine cultural renaissance”.

Such questions are, of course, speculative. But they are interesting in raising the question of what, in a multipolar world of uneven and combined development, one should expect to be the degree of lateral and cross-cutting influence. Japan was a developmental triumph whose influence in Europe and the US is present but hardly hegemonic. Brazil is a huge, middle-income country. India is a giant developing country. In both cases cultural intercourse is relatively uninhibited, but their presence in either European or American culture, let alone in other emerging or developing countries, is limited to particular sectors - food, football and particular segments of the workforce (tech in the case of Indian migrants to the US).

What is the benchmark to which we are comparing these kind of partial and sectoral interactions? Is this, once again, a demonstration of the historical singularity of America’s pervasive cultural, technological, political and geopolitical footprint from the 1940s through to the early 2000s? In an increasingly polycentric world can we even imagine a similar convergence of macroeconomic, geopolitical, mass media, cultural and political forces? How do we (re)imagine the working of soft power beyond hegemony?

I love writing the newsletter. If you enjoy it too and fancy buying me a coffee once a month, you know what to do. Click below!

Subscribe now

Claude’s Response

This is a register essay rather than an argument essay, and it should be read that way. Tooze is mapping the conversational territory the New York salon opened up; he isn’t trying to settle whether Chinamaxxing is real, durable, or politically consequential. Within that frame, the piece does two genuinely useful things. First, the seven-vector taxonomy is more disciplined than the typical “China is having a moment” thinkpiece — separating technocratic envy from retro-authenticity from wellness from cuteness from the rest forces the reader to notice that these are different audiences responding to different signals, not one undifferentiated wave. Second, the historical recontextualization (Mao-maxxing, Hong Kong cinema, Japanese cuteness as the precursor to Chinese cuteness) does real work against the Anglophone tendency to treat the current moment as singular.

The weakest move is the closing pivot. Tooze ends by asking how to reimagine soft power “beyond hegemony” in a polycentric world, but the question is posed in a way that lets him avoid the harder version: if you accept Dan Wang’s claim that CCP repression is suppressing what would otherwise be a much larger cultural footprint, then the polycentric-world framing is a distraction from a more specific empirical question — what’s the elasticity of Chinese cultural exports with respect to domestic political constraint? Wang’s claim is testable in principle (compare the international reach of Chinese-diaspora cultural production unconstrained by Beijing — Hong Kong cinema in its prime, Taiwanese pop, mainland directors working abroad — against the throttled mainland version), and Tooze has the apparatus to engage it. He doesn’t.

The other thing worth flagging: the piece is sourced almost entirely from one panel discussion plus Ding’s essay. That’s fine for a register-survey post, but the seven vectors are presented as if they’re distillations of a phenomenon rather than distillations of a conversation among three China-watchers in New York. Readers who haven’t seen the underlying source material should treat the taxonomy as Tooze-Wang-Ding’s hypothesis-set, not as a documented inventory of Western Chinamaxxing in 2026.

Back to summary

Chartbook 445: Is a "China shock" coming for the "big ag" food regime?

Adam Tooze · 2026-05-03 · Original →

China’s integration into the world economy is the most dramatic development of modern economic history. As far as “global imbalances” are concerned, this is a story of net trade surpluses and growing Chinese prowess in industry. But the flip side of that, are imports of raw materials, energy and food. In agriculture China has gone through a highly unusual “opening up”.

Global food trade takes place between blocs, in which production and consumption have developed under tight government regulation and a political economy dominated by major incumbent corporate interests - so-called “food regimes” (a term owed to Harriet Friedmann and Philip McMichael). Agriculture is one of the original sectors to experience what Karl Polanyi called a “double movement”, of market “opening” followed by collective regulatory backlash. Except in moments of crisis, this entrenched political economy does not generally allow for dramatic shifts in trade flows or production regimes. It is designed to prevent them. But since the 2000s it is demand growth in China that has been the driving force of the “neoliberal corporate” food regime and China has played that role as an importer, not an exporter. From a liberal point of view it is one of the great triumphs of the “free trade” era. The question is whether we are on the cusp of a new phase in which China applies to “big ag” the policies with which it has up-ended so many major areas of industrial production?

In the 1990s China engaged in tiny amounts of agricultural trade with the rest of the world, running a small trade surplus. Twenty years later, China has substantially expanded its exports. But it had also become an even larger agricultural importer, with the largest food trade deficit in the world and a self sufficiency ratio that is estimated to be less than 70 percent.

To say that this lopsided development in China’s agricultural trade is unusual, is an understatement. Around the world, agricultural markets are hotly contested and fiercely defended by national policy, lobby groups and corporate interests. To see a lurch into deficit is highly unusual. China’s scale puts it in a different league. To see how unusual China’s trajectory is, compare it to that of the US and the EU - the other major players in global trade. Since 2000 US imports of agricultural goods have increased too, but they are broadly in balance.

Along with China, NAFTA and the dispersed category of ROW dominate US agricultural trade. On paper, Mexico has a similar gdp per capita to China and since the 1990s it was subject to a considerable shock of trade integration through NAFTA. Nevertheless, since the 2010s, Mexico’s food trade balance with the US has shifted strongly into surplus. Over the same period, the EU has steadily widened its export surplus in agriculture.

To understand how China’s huge lurch into agricultural trade deficit was possible, one needs to dig far more deeply into the subordinate political economy of the Chinese countryside in the period of reform and opening up. That is a task for another time. But one key factor in helping to understand this shift is to recognize that it is additive. Whereas Europe and the US may have adjusted their food consumption regimes at the margins over recent decades - more avocados, less lard. But basic patterns are relatively static. Not true for China. Since the 1990s the national diet has been completely transformed, with a huge surge in the consumption of meat and fish protein.

It is this surge in protein consumption that has driven China’s agricultural globalization. In basic grains China is largely self-sufficient.

Source: https://millermagazine.com/blog/chinas-new-grain-equation-balancing-self-sufficiency-and-rising-feed-demand-6743

Indeed, thanks to price subsidies, China has accumulated huge stocks of corn, rice and wheat.

But in high-value animal feed, above all soybeans, the deficit is huge.

China’s huge surge in demand for meat entirely dominates the dynamics of the global soy industry, as this graph from 2019 shows.

https://www.proag.com/news/ers-report-interdependence-of-china-united-states-and-brazil-in-soybean-trade/

Brazilian and American soy bean production has surged over the last quarter century with Brazil leading the way. The growth in Brazilian production has been driven by Chinese demand, which accounts for 73-83 percent of Brazilian exports .

So, to summarize the historical backdrop, the last quarter century has seen the emergence of a dramatically new food system for one sixth of humanity (China) featuring far higher levels of protein consumption supplied by feed cultivated above all in Brazil.

The obvious question, in a moment of change like the one we are living through, is whether this can be expected to continue. An import dependence on the scale of China’s for something as basic as protein, is a vulnerability. It is a vulnerability that neither the US nor the EU live with, let alone other “global players” like Russia. In a geopolitical environment of growing stress, it is hardly surprising that “food security” is a key concern for Beijing’s leadership. In the shock year of 2022, Xi Jinping mentioned the issue once every five days.

Of course, policy buzzwords come and go frequently in Beijing. But what if China were to get serious? This is the question I ask in an op-ed in this weekend’s FT. What if Beijing were to apply to agriculture the same multi-faceted “industrial” policy toolkit, which has had such dramatic impact on areas like new energy? This is the scenario explored in a recent report by Systemiq commissioned by the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation.

As Systemiq and their collaborators at China’s Agricultural University point out, the signs are there. Between the 14th and 15th Five-Year Plans, the key elements of a “whole-system” policy shift seem to being put in place. If this plays out on the timeline of industrial policy, then over the next 15-20 years we might very well see a spectacular shift in China’s food regime. The consequences for the global agricultural system that has taken shape since the early 2000s could be dramatic. Chinese imports from the US could fall by more than 85 percent.

The point here is not to make a specific prediction about the future of global ag markets. The point is to stress how radically new the current configuration of global agricultural trade is. How asymmetric it is in terms of the dependence of China on key inputs for high-value agriculture and how the story of Chinese industrial policy since the 2010s has taught us lessons about who quickly and how dramatically basic parameters in the world economy can be changed.

I love writing the newsletter. If you enjoy it too and fancy buying me a coffee once a month, you know what to do. Click below!

Subscribe now

Claude’s Response

This is a textbook example of what Tooze does best: take a domain (US-China agricultural trade) that gets covered as a domestic American story (soybean farmers, tariff retaliation, election-year midwest politics) and reframe it through non-Anglophone macro data and global-political-economy categories. The food-regime framework (Friedmann/McMichael) is genuinely doing analytical work — it explains why China’s deficit is anomalous (food regimes are designed to prevent such shifts, so China’s emergence as a 30%+ deficit importer of a basic input is not a normal trade pattern but a system-level deviation).
